Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ because و changes to ا in hollow verbs
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: place/means of protection (object of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to show this is a place or means of protection. The و changed to ا because it's a weak letter. This word is what Yusuf said in response.
Added ا after ع to create the pattern for a place/means noun. This is the final form used by Yusuf to say 'I seek protection'
Added ال and merged it with the word, making it a proper noun referring specifically to Allah
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and shadda on ل. This word is the object of مَعَاذَ (refuge) in the phrase 'I seek refuge with Allah'
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' in English
Added هُ at the end to show who/what we're talking about (in this case, referring back to Allah who was mentioned before)
Added the small و mark (ۥ) after هُ which is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to care for and help grow
lord (one who takes care of others)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this person does a lot of caring and helping, making them a lord or master
Adding ي at the end shows this is MY lord. The kasra (ِ) helps connect the letters smoothly.
The alif (ٓ) shows this word is being stretched when spoken. This happens because it's an important word in what's being said.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he made good/treated well (referring to اللَّهِ - Allah)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to show causing something to happen. In this ayah, Allah is the one who made Yusuf's stay good/treated him well.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added مَ at beginning to create a noun for the place where the action happens, and changed the ending to ى to show it's a special type of noun
Added ا after و to make it a noun meaning 'place of dwelling'
Added ي at the end to show it belongs to me ('my dwelling place')

steps.m: my dwelling place (referring to Yusuf's place in the house of Al-Aziz)
steps.n: Added fatha on the final ى to show it's being used in a sentence. In this ayah, Yusuf is referring to his place of residence in the house of Al-Aziz, which he doesn't want to betray by doing wrong.
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what follows. Here it emphasizes that Allah is truly his Lord.
The هُۥ at the end refers to Allah in this context, connecting to رَبِّىٓ (my Lord) later in the sentence. Yusuf is emphasizing that Allah is his master who has given him a good place to stay.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed) into 'not succeed'. In this ayah, it's saying the wrongdoers will not succeed.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes to present tense by adding يُ at start, changing vowels, and adding ُ at end. This word is negated by لا (not) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
This pattern shows someone who does the action - like adding '-er' in English (teach → teacher). In this ayah, الظَّالِمُونَ is the subject (doer) of the verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed).
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this word is in the nominative case, like a subject in English
Adding ون makes the word plural, showing multiple wrongdoers
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - now talking about particular wrongdoers
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced
Added لَ at the beginning to make the meaning stronger
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: he intended strongly (connected to بِهِۦ - towards him)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this was a strong intention. This word connects to بِهِۦ showing who was intended.

steps.m: she intended strongly (connected to بِهِۦ - towards him)
steps.n: Adding feminine marker ت shows the subject is feminine singular. This refers to the wife of Al-Aziz who intended towards Yusuf.
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or the direction of an action
Added the pronoun هِ (him) to show who the action is directed toward
Added the small ۦ mark after هِ which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: he intended strongly (subject of لَوْلَآ - 'if not for')
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this was a strong intention or desire. This word is connected to لَوْلَآ which shows something that would have happened if not for something else.

steps.m: and he intended strongly (subject of لَوْلَآ - 'if not for')
steps.n: Adds conjunction 'and' to connect this to the previous statement about her intention (هَمَّتْ), showing both parties had strong intentions
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning with/toward

steps.m: with/toward her (referring to the situation with يوسف)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show this refers to 'her'. In this ayah, it refers to the situation involving Yusuf (AS).

steps.m: if not for (introduces what would have happened to هَمَّ)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that shows something would have happened, but didn't because something else prevented it. Here it introduces what Yusuf would have done if he hadn't seen evidence from his Lord. The آ at the end is just a longer way of writing ا.

steps.m: that (introduces what happened when he saw)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that introduces what happened after رَّءَا (he saw) in this ayah. It helps explain the sequence of events.
Changed from basic root 'to see' into past tense 'he saw' by adding vowel marks and using ى at the end
Changed hamza to alif at end since it's a weak letter
Added a double letter mark (shadda) on ر because it connects with the word before it
Root letters get vowels added to make a noun meaning 'proof' - like turning 'prove' into 'proof'

steps.m: clear proof/evidence (object of رَّءَا - he saw)
steps.n: Changed the ending to show this is what Yusuf saw (the object of the verb 'he saw'). The alif is written as a long ā (ٰ) here.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or develop something to its full potential
master/lord (one who nurtures and takes care of others)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does a lot of nurturing and caring
The kasra at the end shows possession - like saying 'my Lord' or 'Lord of'
The هِ shows this is 'his lord' - referring back to يوسف in the story. The small ۦ after هِ is just for pronunciation.
This is the basic demonstrative word that points to something
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an alif khanjariya
Added ل with kasra to make it point to something more distant

steps.m: like that/thus (pointing to what happened in the previous ayah)
steps.n: Added ك at the end to make it point to something specific that was just mentioned
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
The ن at the start shows we (plural) are doing the action

steps.m: so that we may turn away (from يوسف the evil and immorality)
steps.n: The لِ shows purpose - this explains why Allah showed His proof to Yusuf. The word connects to عَنْهُ (from him) which comes right after.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from' or 'away from'. In this ayah, it's used with the verb لِنَصْرِفَ (to turn away) to show what is being turned away from.
Added the attached pronoun هُ (him) to show who something is being turned away from. In this ayah, it refers to Yusuf, showing that evil and immorality were turned away from him.
Changed the action into a thing by adding ُ on the first letter. Like changing 'to harm' into 'harm'
Added ٱل to point to specific harm/evil being talked about, and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ٱل

steps.m: the harm/evil (object of لِنَصْرِفَ - that we may turn away)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this is what's being turned away, connecting it to the verb لِنَصْرِفَ (that we may turn away) in the ayah

steps.m: to go beyond proper limits, to be obscene

steps.n: Root letters combined into a noun meaning 'one obscene act' (like changing 'being obscene' to 'an obscene thing')
Added اء at the end to change it from a single bad act to mean all types of bad behavior in general
Added ال to make it specific
Added و (wa) at beginning to mean 'and', and lengthened the final vowel sound (with ا) which is common for this type of word
Adding shadda makes this word stronger - changing it from a conditional 'if' to an emphatic 'indeed'
Adding هُ at the end shows who the word is talking about - in this case 'he'
The small و (ۥ) is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: from (connecting عِبَادِنَا to what came before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something. Here it connects 'Our servants' (عِبَادِنَا) to the previous part of the sentence.
Changed the root into a plural noun showing multiple people who serve/worship

steps.m: our servants (of مِنْ our chosen ones)
steps.n: Added نَا meaning 'our'. This word follows مِنْ (from/of) in the ayah, showing these are among Allah's specially chosen servants.
Added مُ at start and vowel marks to show this is about someone receiving the action of being purified

steps.m: one who is purified (connected to something)
steps.n: The -i ending connects this word to show it belongs to or relates to something else
Added ين to show this refers to multiple people who are purified

steps.m: the purified ones (referring to Allah's chosen servants)
steps.n: Added ال to specify these particular purified ones. In this ayah, it refers to Allah's specially chosen servants who are protected from evil.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Adding 'اِسْتَ' changes 'racing ahead' into 'racing against each other' - like a competition
The 'ا' at the end shows exactly two people did the action together

steps.m: and they both competed in racing (to ٱلْبَابَ)
steps.n: Adding 'وَ' connects this action to what happened before. They were racing to reach the door (ٱلْبَابَ).
Adding alif makes it a common noun pattern

steps.m: the door (object that they raced to)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's the object being raced to in the phrase 'they both raced to the door'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this was done with force. In this ayah, she is tearing Yusuf's shirt.
The ت at the end shows a female did this action

steps.m: and she tore forcefully (قَمِيصَهُۥ - his shirt)
steps.n: Adds conjunction و meaning 'and'
Changes from basic root to concrete noun meaning 'shirt'
Shows this is the object being torn in the sentence
Adds 'his' and shows this is what was torn in قَدَّتْ (she tore)
This is a connecting word that shows the shirt was torn from behind. It links the word دُبُرٍ (behind) to the action قَدَّتْ (she tore).

steps.m: to follow behind, to come after

steps.n: Adding damma vowels changes it from a verb root to a noun meaning 'back'
Added -in ending to show 'from behind/from the back' - tells us his shirt was torn from the back direction
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he found'
Adding alif at start makes it Form IV, which can add causative meaning
Added alif at the end to show two people did the action
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Changes from basic root meaning 'to lead' into a noun meaning 'leader' by adding vowels
Doubles the middle letter to intensify the meaning of mastery/authority

steps.m: her master (object of أَلْفَيَا - they both found)
steps.n: Adds feminine singular object pronoun 'her' to indicate possession. The word is in accusative case because it is the object (thing being found) of the verb أَلْفَيَا (they both found).
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation. This is a particle showing location/presence, not derived from a root.
Added alif at the end to show this word connects to what comes after it (the door). This particle appears in the phrase 'at/near the door' (لَدَا ٱلْبَابِ).
The middle root letter و becomes ا (alif) to make the basic noun form. This is a common pattern for hollow roots (roots with و or ي in the middle).
Added ال to make it definite (the door) and ِ at the end because it's being acted upon in the sentence - they raced to the door.
The root letters got vowels added to make it past tense 'he said'. The middle letter و changed to ا, which is common for these types of words
Adding ت at the end changes 'he said' to 'she said'
This is a question word used to ask 'what'. In this ayah, it's asking 'what is the punishment?'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation

steps.m: to give what someone deserves (good or bad)

steps.n: Root letters were connected with fathas to make a past tense verb meaning 'he rewarded/punished'
Changed the verb into a noun that means the thing given as reward or punishment

steps.m: the reward/punishment (as the subject of a sentence)
steps.n: Changed to show this is 'the reward' and made it the doer in the sentence by adding the 'u' sound at the end

steps.m: who/whoever (referring to the person who intended evil against يُوسُف's family)
steps.n: This is a question word used to ask about people. Here it's used to ask about the identity of someone who did something wrong. It connects to the next part of the sentence that describes what they did.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this is about something that happened in the past
Adding أَ at the start makes this about actively wanting something specific
Standard weak letter transformation where و changes to ا after alif in Form IV
Changed the root into a noun meaning the people who dwell together
Added بِ at the beginning to show 'with' and kasra at the end because it follows a preposition
Added كَ at the end to show it's your family
Changed from a root meaning 'to do evil' into a noun meaning 'evil itself' by adding the ُ (damma) vowel mark
Added long vowel mark over و to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: evil (as the object of أَرَادَ - intended)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is what was intended (object of the verb أَرَادَ - he intended)
This is a basic word that means 'except'. It's used to show that something is different from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it introduces what punishment should be given.
Added a long alif at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word. This word introduces the punishment mentioned next - imprisonment.
This is a basic particle that connects ideas and introduces what will happen. Here it introduces the punishment (يُسْجَنَ - to be imprisoned).
Added the fatha mark (َ) above the first letter to show correct pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show passive voice - something being done to someone rather than someone doing something
Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense passive voice. The word comes after أَن (that) which requires the subjunctive mood, shown by the final fatha.
This is a connecting word that shows two possible punishments being suggested: either imprisonment or painful punishment. The fatha on the hamza and sukun on the waw help with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of punishment. This word is in the nominative case because it is the subject of the verb أن يسجن in a compound sentence with أو (or).
Adding the two dots (tanween) at the end makes it mean 'a punishment' instead of 'the punishment'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
This pattern makes an adjective that means 'full of' the root meaning
Adding the double damma makes it indefinite (like saying 'a' in English)
Added fathas to make past tense form. This shows the action happened in the past and was done by 'he'.
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)

This is a standalone pronoun that refers to a female person or thing (in this case, referring to the wife of Al-Aziz). Not derived from a root. It's the subject of the verb رٰوَدَتْ (she sought/attempted to seduce).
The added ا makes the action more intense and shows repeated attempts
Added ت with sukun to show a female did this action in the past

steps.m: she tried to seduce me (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added نِى at the end to show the action was done to 'me'. This word appears in Yusuf's statement about what happened.
This is a basic preposition that shows movement away from something or indicates the topic being discussed
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation. In this ayah, it connects to the word نَفْسِي (myself) to show Yusuf speaking about himself.
Added vowel marks (َ and ْ) to the root letters to make the word for 'soul'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is the object of a preposition
Added ي at the end to show possession - 'my soul' instead of just 'soul'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make past tense. This word is related to شَاهِدٌ (witness) that comes right after it in the ayah, showing who did the witnessing.

steps.m: and he witnessed (connected to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this action with the previous statement (قَالَ - he said). Shows these events happened in sequence.
Added ا after first letter and ِ after second letter to show who does the action. This word is a witness who testified in Yusuf's case.
Added tanween dammah (ٌ) to show this is referring to any witness, not a specific one
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or starts. Here it shows the witness comes from her family.
The 'n' sound blends into the next letter, shown by the shadda mark

steps.m: to belong to a family or household

steps.n: Root letters put together with vowels to make the simple noun 'family'
Adding kasra to show this word is possessed by something
Adding feminine possessive pronoun suffix هَآ meaning 'her'

This is a conditional particle that introduces the first part of an if-then statement. Here it starts a condition about his shirt - 'if his shirt...' The result of this condition comes after with فَ (then/so).
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic for this type of word. In this ayah, it's describing the state of his shirt.
Changes from basic root to concrete noun meaning 'shirt'
Added ُ to show this is the subject of كَانَ (was)
Added هُۥ to show possession (his). This word is the subject of كَانَ (was)
This root carries the basic meaning of cutting or tearing something lengthwise
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it

steps.m: it was torn (violently) (the shirt of يُوسُفَ)
steps.n: Added emphasis by doubling the middle letter and removing the last vowel. This word refers to Yusuf's shirt being torn in the story.

This is a basic preposition showing the direction something happened - in this case, it shows which direction Yusuf's shirt was torn from. It connects with the word قُبُلٍ (front) to show the shirt was torn from the front.

steps.m: to face or be in front of
steps.n: The root letters are shaped into a noun pattern
The root letters get vowels (u sounds) to make a noun meaning 'front parts', and the -in ending makes it indefinite (not specific)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added ت at the end to show a female did the action
Added فَ at the start to show this happened as a result of something before it
This is a basic third person masculine singular pronoun. In this ayah, it refers to the person who is being described as among the liars.
The و at the start connects this phrase to what came before it, showing this is an additional statement about the person being discussed.
This is a basic particle that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to a group
Added a fatha at the end because it's followed by ال in الْكٰذِبِينَ (the liars). This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the past tense verb
Changes from verb (he lied) to noun (liar) by adding alif and changing vowels
Added ين to make it plural, showing multiple male liars
Added ال to make it specific - 'the liars'. This word follows مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah.
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it starts the condition 'if his shirt was torn...'

steps.m: and if (connects this condition to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels. This word describes the state of قَمِيصُهُۥ (his shirt) in the past.

steps.m: to cover or wrap with clothing

steps.n: Changes from basic root into a word for a piece of clothing
Added ُ to show this is the subject of the verb كَانَ (was)
Added هُ with extension mark ۥ to show possession - 'his shirt'

steps.m: to cut or tear something lengthwise

steps.n: Added a shadda (double mark) on the middle letter to show strong tearing action

steps.m: it was torn (referring to قَمِيصُهُۥ his shirt)
steps.n: Changed the first vowel from fatha (َ) to damma (ُ) to show that the shirt received the action of being torn, rather than doing the tearing itself
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from. In this ayah, it connects the verb قُدَّ (was torn) with دُبُرٍ (back), showing the shirt was torn from the back.

steps.m: to follow from behind, to come after

steps.n: Adding damma vowels creates a noun meaning the back/rear part of something

steps.m: back side, rear part (of قَمِيصُهُ his shirt)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is the object of مِن (from) in the phrase 'torn from the back'
Added fatha vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
The ت at the end shows a female (Potiphar's wife) did the action

steps.m: so she lied (as a consequence of seeing his shirt torn from behind)
steps.n: The فَ at start means 'so' or 'then' - it shows she lied as a result of seeing his shirt torn from behind
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation - هُ (hu) and وَ (wa)

steps.m: and he (the one who is مِنَ ٱلصَّادِقِينَ - among the truthful ones)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, showing that this is an additional statement
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else
Added َ (fatha) at the end because it's followed by ال in ٱلصَّادِقِينَ (the truthful ones). This helps connect the words smoothly when reading.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
This form shows someone who has this quality as a characteristic
Adding ين makes it plural. The ِ shows it comes after مِنَ (from/of)
Adding ال makes it definite. The shadda appears on ص when ال is added.
This is a basic particle meaning 'when' that shows something happened after another thing happened. Not derived from a root.

steps.m: then when (connects to رَءَا - he saw)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened right after something else. This connects to the next word رَءَا (he saw) to show the sequence of events.
Basic past tense pattern (fatha vowels) added to root letters to make 'he saw'

The ي at the end changed to ا, and the أ changed to ء because it's between two vowels. This is a common change in words ending with ي. The word is connected to قَمِيصَهُۥ (his shirt) which is what he saw.

steps.m: to clothe or cover with a garment

steps.n: Changes from basic root into a concrete noun meaning 'shirt'
Shows this is what was seen in the sentence 'when he saw his shirt'
Adds هُۥ to show the shirt belongs to him. This word is what was seen in the phrase 'when he saw his shirt'
Connected the root letters together with fatha to create the basic verb form
The doubled middle letter (shadda) changes it from the basic 'to tear' into 'he actually tore something'

steps.m: it was torn (describing قَمِيصَهُۥ - his shirt)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice by changing the first vowel to damma, showing the shirt was torn by someone

steps.m: from (showing قَمِيصَهُۥ was torn from this direction)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it specifically shows which direction the shirt was torn from.

steps.m: to turn back or be behind

steps.n: Adding damma vowels changes it from a verb root to a noun meaning 'back/rear'
Added tanwin (two dots under) to show it's 'from the back side' - connects to 'his shirt' in the verse
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs
This is an emphatic particle that makes statements stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English. It's one of the most basic words in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: indeed it (referring to this situation being part of their plot)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end to show what is being emphasized. The ۥ mark shows this is pronounced with a long vowel. In this ayah, 'it' refers to the situation with Yusuf's shirt being torn from behind, which reveals the plot.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or source. Here it connects with قُدَّ (was torn) to show the direction of the tearing - from the back.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to change from action to thing
Added kasra at the end to show this plan belongs to someone (in this case, the women)

steps.m: your (women's) secret plan (referring to the women's plot against Yusuf)
steps.n: Added كُنَّ at the end to show this belongs to multiple women being spoken to
This is a particle that adds emphasis to what follows. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'your plot is great'. The shadda (ّ) on ن makes the emphasis stronger.
Changed from a verb meaning 'to plan' into a noun meaning 'a plan' by adding vowel marks
The suffix 'َ' shows that this word is the object of a sentence. It's like saying 'the plot' when you're talking about something someone did *to* the plot.

steps.m: your (feminine plural) secret plan (possessed by you women)
steps.n: The word 'plan' gets كُنَّ added at the end to show it belongs to multiple women being spoken to (like saying 'your plan' when talking to a group of women)
Added vowels to make a past tense verb showing the basic action of becoming great
Changed the verb into an adjective by adding ي between second and third root letters
Added tanween (double damma) to make the word indefinite
This is the base form of the proper noun, which comes from Hebrew 'Yosef' (יוסף). In Arabic, foreign proper names don't have Arabic roots.
Connected the letters together to form the basic word

steps.m: Joseph (the subject of أَعْرِضْ - turn away)
steps.n: Added damma marks to show this is the subject (doer) of the action in the sentence. Here, Yusuf is being told to 'turn away' (أَعْرِضْ)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Added أَ at beginning and sukun on first root letter to show turning away from something
Changed vowels to make it a command - kasra on middle letter and sukun at end
This is a preposition that shows Yusuf should turn away from or avoid something. In this ayah, it connects يُوسُفُ (Yusuf) with هٰذَا (this matter), showing he should turn away from it.
Basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this'
Added ه at the beginning to draw attention to what's being pointed to, like saying 'look at this'
Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation. The ٰ (alif khanjariyah) extends the sound of the ه
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
The prefix 'است' shows asking for something
Changed the end vowels and added ي (yaa) to make it a command for one female - like saying 'you (female) ask for forgiveness!'

steps.m: and seek forgiveness! (command to a female)
steps.n: Added conjunction prefix و meaning 'and'
Adding the fatha vowel mark turns the root into a simple noun meaning 'sin'
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word is affected by something before it
The preposition لِ means 'for/to' and causes the following noun to take genitive case
The كِ at the end shows this belongs to a female 'you'. This connects to the verb استغفري (seek forgiveness) which is also feminine.
This is the basic conditional particle that forms the base of the emphatic particle
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphatic 'indeed'
Adding كِ at the end shows this is addressing a female 'you'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The weak letter و drops out and the vowels change to match the hollow verb pattern

steps.m: you were (feminine, addressing Eve in this ayah)
steps.n: Added تِ at the end to show this is addressing a female 'you'
This is the basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added fatha at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word الخَاطِئِينَ (the sinners). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action
Makes the word plural to show many people who make mistakes
Adds 'the' to make it specific about which wrongdoers we're talking about
Changed from basic root to past tense 'he' form by adding pattern. The middle letter و became ا because it's between two fathas
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
The ة at the end makes it refer to a group
The ٌ makes it indefinite, meaning 'a' group of women rather than 'the' group
This word means 'women' and is the subject - they are the ones doing the speaking in this verse
This is a preposition that shows the wife of Al-Azeez is IN a state of clear error. It connects the person (wife) to where/what state they are in (error).
Root م-د-ن became مَدِينَ by adding vowels and ي to make a noun meaning 'city/civilized place'
Added ة to show this is one specific civilized place (a city)
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to show we're talking about a specific city
Added ِ at the end because this city belongs to or is connected to something (in this case, it's where ٱمْرَأَتُ (the wife) is from)
Forms the base noun meaning 'person'
Root letters connected with vowels to make the word 'person'
Adding alif at the beginning changes the meaning to specifically refer to a woman
Adding ة makes it specifically refer to one woman
Changed initial alif to wasl form and added ُ to show this is the subject doing something. In this ayah, it refers specifically to the wife of العزيز (the minister)
Added vowels and ي to make an intensive adjective showing someone with a strong quality of might/power. This refers to the husband of the woman mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: the mighty one (title of امْرَأَتُ's husband)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific - refers to a particular mighty person (the husband)

steps.m: of the mighty one (title of امْرَأَتُ's husband)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this word belongs to or describes امْرَأَتُ (the wife of)
The added alif makes the meaning more intense, showing repeated or persistent action
The ت at the start shows a female is doing the action now
Adding the vowels 'fatha' (َ) turns the root letters into a past tense verb meaning 'he was young'
The alif maqsurah (ى) at the end changes it from a verb to a noun meaning 'youth'

steps.m: her young man/servant (object of تُرٰوِدُ - she seeks)
steps.n: Added هَا suffix meaning 'her'. This word is being acted upon by the verb تُرٰوِدُ (she seeks/pursues).
This is a preposition showing that the minister's wife is trying to separate/seduce her slave boy away from his proper self/behavior
Connected the root letters to form the base word
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha on first letter, sukun on second
Added kasra at the end to show it's being acted upon by the word عَن (from/about)
Added shadda on first letter for emphasis
Added هِۦ at the end to show possession (his)

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizing شَغَفَهَا - 'she has become passionately in love')
steps.n: This is a particle that emphasizes the verb that comes after it (شَغَفَهَا). It's like saying 'truly' or 'definitely' before stating something that has happened.
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to create the past tense verb form

steps.m: he overwhelmed her with passionate love
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show that 'her' is the object being affected by the passionate love. This relates to the earlier mention of امْرَأَتُ الْعَزِيزِ (the wife of the governor) in the ayah.
Added damma and shadda to make it a noun meaning intense love

steps.m: intense love (object of شَغَفَهَا - it has possessed her)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is what has possessed her heart. In the ayah, this word is the direct object of شَغَفَهَا (it has possessed her).
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word

steps.m: indeed we (connected to لَنَرَىٰهَا - we see her)
steps.n: Added ا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to the next word لَنَرَىٰهَا (we see her)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb. The hamza (أ) changes to alif (ا) at the end due to it being a weak letter.
Added نَ at start to mean 'we', but this is still present tense - just a different form of 'to see'
Added لَ prefix to add emphasis, showing certainty about the action
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) to show the proper spelling of the weak verb ending
Added هَا suffix to show the feminine object (her) being seen
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it shows the women were inside/within the city.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'straying'
Adding the long alif makes the meaning stronger - like saying 'really lost' instead of just 'lost'
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite (like 'a/an' in English) and is possessed by the word مُّبِينٍ (clear) that follows it
Connected the root letters together
Added مُ at start and ِ in middle to show this describes something that makes things clear
Added emphasis by doubling the م sound
Added tanween to show this describes the error (ضَلٰلٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Basic sequential connector showing one thing follows another
Added لَ to emphasize what comes next

steps.m: then when (connects to رَأَيْنَهُۥٓ - they saw him)
steps.n: Added مَّا with shadda to show this introduces a time condition. In this ayah, it connects to when they (the women) saw him (Yusuf)

steps.m: to hear, listen, or perceive sound

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb meaning 'he heard'

steps.m: she heard (subject of أَرْسَلَتْ - she sent)
steps.n: Added ت with sukun at the end to show the subject is feminine. This word describes what 'she' (the wife of Al-Aziz) did before sending for the women.
Connects the root letters with vowel marks to create the basic noun meaning 'a plot or scheme'
Adds kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is connected to something else
Adds هِنَّ to show the plot belongs to a group of women

steps.m: with their plot (referring to when she heard about the women's scheme)
steps.n: Adds بِ at the beginning to show this is what was heard about (connects to سَمِعَتْ meaning 'she heard')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding أ at the start makes the verb mean 'to send/dispatch something or someone' - it makes the action more direct
The ت shows a female subject (she). This verb connects to إِلَيْهِنَّ (to them) in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something
The ى changes to ي when adding endings
Adding هِ shows this is happening to someone/something
Adding نَّ changes it from singular masculine to plural feminine
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added hamza at beginning to make the verb transitive (affecting something else)
Added feminine past tense marker ت
Added و conjunction to connect with previous action. The word مُتَّكَـًٔا is what was prepared.
Added fatha to the letter lam to show it's a preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'
Added هُ (him) to show who the action is for
Added نَّ to change 'him' to 'them (feminine plural)'. This word is used because it's referring to the women in the story.
This root gives us the basic idea of leaning or reclining against something
Adding مُ at start and ت with shadda changes this from an action to a place where the action happens, like changing 'sit' to 'sitting place'

steps.m: a reclining place (as the object being prepared)
steps.n: Adding tanween (ًا) shows this is the thing being prepared in the story - it's what was set up for the women
This is the basic meaning of giving or bringing something
Adding the fatha marks makes it a past tense verb meaning 'he gave/brought'
Adding ت at the end makes it feminine - now means 'she' instead of 'he'
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous action in the story
This word shows that something applies to every member of a group
The shadda (doubling of ل) emphasizes the completeness, making it into a noun meaning something that is complete or whole
The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of giving (she gave every one of them). It connects to آتَتْ (she gave) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (doing something alone) to a noun (describing someone alone) by adding alif after the first letter
Added ة at the end to make it feminine
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show it's indefinite and possessed/connected to something else. The alif became a long alif (ٰ) due to writing style.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added هُنَّ at the end to show this means 'from them' where 'them' refers to a group of females
Added shadda on م for pronunciation because it comes after a word ending in a vowel
This root relates to stillness or being settled, which connects to a knife's ability to make things settle or stop moving
Changes root meaning of 'being still' into a tool (knife) that makes things settle/stop moving
Makes the word 'knife' indefinite and shows it's being used as a direct object
Shows this is a knife (with tanween alif) that was given to each woman
This is the basic concept of speaking or saying something
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two vowels.
Added تِ at the end to show a female subject did the action
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement
Makes it a command form
Makes it a firm command to exit
Added connecting alif at beginning to help pronunciation. This word is what the wife of Al-Aziz commanded Yusuf to do - to come out and show himself to the women.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
The ى at the end changes to يْ when we're going to add something to it, just like how 'y' sometimes changes in English words
Added هِنَّ at the end to show this is happening to a group of women. This connects to the earlier part of the story where the women were mentioned.
Starting with the basic temporal word لَمَّ and adding ا to make it work with past tense verbs

steps.m: then when (connects to سَمِعَتْ - she heard)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this to what happened before. This shows that one action follows another - when she heard something, then something else happened.
Connected the root letters and changed the final ي to ى because it's a weak letter at the end
Changed the final ى back to يْ to prepare for adding suffixes
Added نَ to show multiple females did the action
Added هُ to show who they saw (him)
Adding أ at the start makes this word mean 'more' or 'most' of whatever the root means
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Adding أ at the start and changing vowels makes this mean 'to consider something as great'
Adding ن at the end shows multiple women did the action
Adding هُ at the end shows that 'him' is what they considered great
This is the basic concept of cutting
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows the action was done with intensity or multiple times
The نَ ending shows a group of women did this action
The وَ connects this to the previous actions in the verse when the women saw Yusuf
Connected the root letters to form the singular word for 'hand'
Changes singular 'hand' to plural 'hands' using broken plural pattern
Adds the final vowel mark to complete the plural form
Added هُنَّ to show the hands belong to multiple females
This is the basic concept of speaking or saying something
The weak letter و drops out in this form, and we add ُ on first letter and ْ on second letter to make the basic command form
Adding نَ at the end shows that a group of females did this action in the past
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, meaning 'and'

steps.m: to keep away or protect from
steps.n: The root letters show the basic idea of keeping something away or at a distance
The و changes to ا, making it into a special word that shows strong disagreement with something bad being said about someone
The ا is written as ٰ (alif khanjariya) and a fatha is added at the end. This word is followed by اللَّهِ (Allah) in the ayah to express strong disagreement with saying anything bad about Allah.
This is the basic word for deity or god before any changes
Adding shadda on ل makes this the proper name for the one true God
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'to/for' and kasra at end to show connection. The first ا is removed when combining with لِ.

steps.m: not (negating هٰذَا بَشَرًا - this is a human)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that denies what comes after it. In this ayah, the women are saying 'This is NOT a human being' because they are so amazed by Yusuf's beauty that they think he must be an angel.
Basic pointing word that draws attention to something
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's a shortened version
Added ذ with fatha to make it specifically point to something

steps.m: this (masculine) (referring to يوسف who they're amazed by)
steps.n: Added alif at the end to make it masculine and singular. In this ayah, it's referring to Yusuf (عليه السلام) when the women are amazed by his beauty.

steps.m: to be human, to have skin

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make the word for 'human'

steps.m: a human being (object of رَأَيْنَهُ - they saw him)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this is indefinite (a/an) and is being acted upon. In this ayah, it's describing what the women saw when they looked at Yusuf.

This is a negative particle used with إِلَّا to mean 'none/nothing/not...except'. In this ayah, the women are saying 'This is not (إِنْ) anything except (إِلَّا) a noble angel', emphasizing their amazement at Yusuf's beauty.
Basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for something nearby. The fatha marks help with pronunciation.
Adding the small alif (ٰ) above the ه for emphasis and to show this is a formal written style

steps.m: this (masculine, near, strongly emphasized) (subject of بَشَرًا)
steps.n: Changing the final alif to a long alif (آ) for additional emphasis. This word is being used to point to Yusuf (AS) in amazement, saying 'this is not a human!'
This is a basic particle that shows an exception. In this ayah, it connects to the word بَشَرًا (human) to say 'this is not a human, except (he is) a noble angel'

steps.m: to control or have power over something

steps.n: The root letters for 'power/control' get vowel marks (َ) to make 'malak' meaning angel - showing angels are special powerful beings
Added double damma at the end to show this is indefinite - like saying 'an angel' instead of 'the angel'
Added ي between second and third letters with vowels to show this describes someone or something with this quality
The tanween (ٌ) shows this is indefinite (like 'a/an' in English). This word is describing the angel (مَلَكٌ) in the ayah.
Adding the basic vowel pattern for past tense verbs

steps.m: she said (introducing her direct speech that follows)
steps.n: Changed the middle letter و to ا and added ت at the end to show a female speaker. This word introduces what she says next.
Added ل with kasra to show something far away
Added ك with damma to show we're addressing someone
Added فَ at start to mean 'so' or 'then'
Added نَّ with shadda at the end to show we're talking to multiple women
This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. The definite article ال is an essential part of this word's construction.

steps.m: to blame or criticize someone for doing wrong

steps.n: The weak letter و is dropped when making the command form. Added damma and sukun to show basic command form.
Added تُ at the end to change from a command ('blame!') to past tense ('you blamed')
Added نَّ to show we're talking to multiple women with emphasis
Added نِى at the end to show that 'me' is the one being blamed
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is a preposition meaning 'in'

steps.m: in him/it (referring to the situation mentioned in لُمْتُنَّنِى)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to show who/what is being referred to. This connects back to the situation they were blaming her about.
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation
Added ل at the beginning to add emphasis
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to seek or pursue something persistently
steps.n: Basic meaning implies seeking or attempting something
Added alif after first letter to show repeated attempts at an action
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action in the past

steps.m: I tried repeatedly to persuade him
steps.n: Added هُ to show 'him' as the one being persuaded

steps.m: she definitely tried repeatedly to persuade him
steps.n: The words are the same - just written slightly differently. Both mean 'she tried repeatedly to persuade him'. The هُ at the end means 'him' in both versions.
Basic preposition that here connects the verb 'I sought' (رَاوَدتُّهُ) with 'himself' (نَفْسِهِ) to mean 'I sought from him regarding himself'
Root relates to breath and essence of being
Added vowel marks (fat-ha and sukoon) to the root letters to make the word 'self'
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word عَن (from/away from)
Added ه with kasra to show it belongs to him
Added small ي after ه as a pronunciation aid, doesn't change meaning
Basic meaning of protection
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show he's seeking protection for himself

steps.m: and he sought protection (from يوسف resisting زليخا's advances)
steps.n: Added فَ (and/so) to connect this action with what happened before it in the story
This is the basic conditional word that shows something depends on something else
Added ل at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'definitely'
The إ changes to ئِ when it comes after لَ - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it in the ayah
Basic negative word that changes the meaning of verbs that come after it. When used with a present tense verb like يَفْعَلْ (he does), it makes it mean 'he did not do'
Added emphasis by doubling the ل letter. This happens because the word before it ends with a similar sound.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changed to present/future tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he does/will do (in conditional phrase with لَّمْ)
steps.n: Changed final vowel to sukun because it follows لَّمْ (not) in a conditional phrase

steps.m: what (object of ءَامُرُهُۥ - I command him)
steps.n: This is a standalone particle meaning 'what'. In this ayah, it's the object being commanded.

steps.m: what (object of ءَامُرُهُۥ - I command him)
steps.n: The alif is written with a special elongated form (آ) to show emphasis in pronunciation, but this doesn't change the meaning
Basic meaning of commanding
Changes from verb 'to command' to 'one who commands'
Adds 'his' to show possession
Basic meaning of imprisoning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ prefix and changing vowels
Added emphatic لَ prefix to show certainty and changed final vowel

steps.m: he will most certainly be imprisoned
steps.n: Added نَّ suffix for extra emphasis
Basic meaning of being or existing
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it 'he was' in the past tense
Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show present/future tense
Added لَ to add emphasis
Added وَ to connect this phrase to what came before

steps.m: and he will surely be (with emphasis)
steps.n: Changed ending to نًا (nan) to add strong emphasis, like saying 'definitely will be!'
Added fatha at the end to connect this word to the next word (الصَّاغِرِينَ), showing it means 'from the belittled ones'
Added shadda on م to emphasize the word. This happens because the word before it ends in a similar sound.
Basic meaning of being small or lowly
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form to show who has this quality
Added plural ending to show multiple people
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Doubling of middle letter ب shows the intensity of the nurturing/mastery role
Adding kasra at the end makes it a call/plea to one's Lord
Changed the action into a place by adding the vowels ِ and ْ. Like how 'lock up' becomes 'place of locking up' (prison)

steps.m: the prison (subject of أَحَبُّ - more beloved)
steps.n: Added ٱل to make it specific ('the prison'). The ُ shows this word is the subject doing the action of being 'more beloved' (أَحَبُّ) in this verse
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

Added أَ at the beginning to show comparison, doubled the ب with shadda, and added damma to show it's the subject. In this ayah, Yusuf is saying the prison is more beloved to him than what he's being called to.
Basic preposition meaning movement or direction towards something

steps.m: to me (in relation to يَدْعُونَنِى - they invite me)
steps.n: Added the first person pronoun ي with shadda (ّ) to show strong emphasis, meaning 'to ME specifically'. This connects to the verb يَدْعُونَنِى (they invite me) in the ayah.
This word combines two parts: مِن meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'

steps.m: from what (that يَدْعُونَنِىٓ they invite me to)
steps.n: The ن from مِن combines with the م from ما to make a stronger م sound (shown by the ّ mark). This happens because it's easier to say مِمَّا than مِنمَا.
Added vowels to connect letters. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added يَ at start to show present tense, with دْ (sukun) and عُ (damma) vowels. The ا changes back to و.
Added نَ to show multiple people doing the action
Added نِى to show the action is happening to 'me', with a long vowel mark (ٓ) at the end
Basic preposition meaning direction or destination
Changed ى to ي with sukun to prepare for adding the pronoun
Added هِ (him) at the end to show who the action is directed towards

steps.m: if not (condition for تَصْرِفْ - you avert)
steps.n: This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a condition. Here it connects to the next verb تَصْرِفْ (you avert), meaning 'if you do not avert'

steps.m: and if not (condition for تَصْرِفْ - you avert)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Adding تَ changes it from past to present tense and makes it mean 'you' or 'she'
The sukun at the end changes this from a regular verb to a command that depends on the word 'unless/if not' before it
Starting with the basic preposition عَن which means 'from' or 'away from'. Added sukun to show the end of the base word.
Added ي with kasra to show this means 'me'
The ن combines with the ي, shown by the shadda. The ي is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed from verb (doing) to noun (thing) by adding vowel marks
Added fatha at end to show this is being acted upon by the verb تَصْرِفْ (turn away) in the ayah

steps.m: their (feminine) plotting/scheming (referring to the women's plot against Yusuf)
steps.n: Added هُنَّ to show this belongs to the women mentioned earlier in the story
Added vowels (َ) to make it 'he leaned' in the past tense

steps.m: I incline/lean towards (in response to يَدْعُونَنِي - they invite me)
steps.n: Changed to first person present tense by adding أَ at start and changing vowels. This shows Yusuf speaking about what he might do in response to their invitation.

steps.m: I [will] incline towards (in response to يَدْعُونَنِي - they invite me)
steps.n: The final و is dropped because this is in a special grammar position (after إِلَّا in conditional statement)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to يْ when connecting to pronouns
Added هِ which means 'him/it'
Added نَّ to change it from singular masculine (him) to plural feminine (them)
Changed from basic root meaning 'to be' into a direct command 'be!' by adding the vowel 'u'
Added أَ at beginning to show first person present/future tense
Final ن is dropped to show this is in an 'if' statement or command

steps.m: and I be (connected to أَصْبُ - I incline)
steps.n: Added coordinating conjunction وَ to connect with the previous verb أَصْبُ (I incline)
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added emphasis with shadda on the م
Added fatha at the end to connect with the next word الْجٰهِلِينَ (the ignorant ones)
Changes the basic idea into a word for someone who has this quality. The ا after ج and the ِ after ه work together to create this meaning.
The ين at the end makes it talk about many people instead of just one person

steps.m: the ignorant ones (object of مِنَ - from/of)
steps.n:
1) ٱلْ at the start means we're talking about specific ignorant people, not just any ignorant people
2) This word comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah, showing Yusuf is asking not to become one of these people
The middle letter و changes to ا in the past tense form
Adding است shows someone making an effort to do something or seeking to do it

steps.m: and so he responded (to him) (connected to رَبُّهُۥ)
steps.n: The فَ connects this to what happened before, showing this is a response or result. The ا changes to ٱ after فَ. This word connects to رَبُّهُۥ (his Lord) who is doing the responding.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter ل

steps.m: to/for him (referring to يوسف who is being helped by رَبُّهُۥ)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه with damma to mean 'him'. This shows who the action is being done to or for.

steps.m: to/for him (referring to يوسف who is being helped by رَبُّهُۥ)
steps.n: Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Added basic vowels to connect the letters together
The doubled middle letter shows this person does complete and constant caring
The ُ shows this is the one doing the action in فَٱسْتَجَابَ (responded to) him
Adds هُ to show it belongs to him. This word is doing the action in فَٱسْتَجَابَ (responded to) him

steps.m: to turn away or avert something from something else

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past. This word follows after 'his Lord responded to him' in the ayah.

steps.m: and so He turned away (subject of فَٱسْتَجَابَ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action happened as a result of the Lord responding. The subject 'He' refers to Allah who is doing the action.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added attached pronoun ه with damma to show it refers back to the word كَيْد (plot) mentioned right after this word in the ayah
Changes from verb 'to plot' into noun meaning 'plotting'
Shows this is what was turned away (object of the verb فَصَرَفَ meaning 'he turned away')
Added هُنَّ to show this belongs to them (feminine plural, referring to the women)
Added kasra and shadda to show this is an emphatic particle that strongly confirms what comes after it
Added هُ (with damma) at the end to show this refers to 'he/it'
Added the small و mark (ۥ) after ه which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
This is the basic form of the standalone pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it's the subject of السَّمِيعُ (The All-Hearing), telling us who is being described.
Added damma (ُ) and fatha (َ) marks to show the correct pronunciation: 'huwa'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
This form shows someone who hears a lot or has the quality of hearing to a great degree
Adding ال with shadda on the س shows this is THE one who hears everything
The word gets a damma (ُ) at the end to show it's being used as one of Allah's special names and qualities
Added vowels and ي to show this is an intensive form meaning someone who knows everything completely
Added ال to make it definite, showing this refers to Allah's specific attribute of complete knowledge

steps.m: The All-Knowing (as a divine attribute, describing هُوَ)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is describing هُوَ (He) in the ayah
This is the basic form of the word that shows something happens after something else

steps.m: then (connects this ayah to what came before it)
steps.n: Added marks to make it mean 'then' - connects one idea to the next, like saying 'and after that'
Basic meaning of letters together
Added fathas to make it past tense
Changed و to ا because it's at the end of the word
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for them/to them (connected to بَدَا - it appeared)
steps.n: Added هُم (them) to show who the action is for. This word connects to بَدَا (it appeared) to show what appeared to them.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. It has no root because it's a particle.
The م gets a shadda (double mark) because it comes after a word ending in a similar sound (the م in لَهُم). This makes it easier to pronounce.
A small ن (called tanwin) is added at the end to help connect smoothly with the next word (بَعْدِ)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and create the basic noun meaning 'after'. The concept comes from something being distant in time.

steps.m: after (connected to مَا which follows it)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (مَا) in the ayah
This is a relative pronoun that refers to something mentioned before. In this ayah, it refers to the signs/proofs (الْءَايٰتِ) that they saw.
Added fathas and changed ي to ى because it's at the end of the word
Changed from 'he saw' to 'they saw' by replacing the ى with وْ at the end

steps.m: they saw (subject of لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ - they will surely imprison him)
steps.n: Added وا with damma to show many people did the action. This word is connected to لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ (they will surely imprison him) - after they saw the signs, that's what they decided to do.
The first two root letters combine into a long آ sound. This is a common pattern with this type of root.
Forms the plural by adding ات, which is used for feminine nouns
Adding ال makes it refer to specific signs/miracles that were seen (referring back to رَأَوُا - they saw)

steps.m: the signs, the miracles (object of رَأَوُا - they saw)
steps.n: The ِ ending shows this word is being acted upon by the verb رَأَوُا (they saw)
Added basic present tense form by adding يَ at start and vowels. This shows someone is doing the imprisoning right now.
Added لَ at start to show this will definitely happen
Added نَّ at the end to make it even more certain
Added هُ at the end to show who will be imprisoned (him)
This is the basic form of the word that shows reaching a limit. Since it's a particle, we start with its simplest form rather than a root.
Added fatha and shadda to strengthen the meaning and show it's a definite endpoint

steps.m: until, up to (لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ - they would imprison him)
steps.n: Added the elongated ى (alif maqsurah) at the end to show this word connects what comes before it with what comes after it in time or space. Here it connects their decision to imprison him with 'for a time' (حِينٍ).
The root letters that mean 'time passing' got a kasra (i) sound in the middle to make it mean 'a time period'
Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite and is being acted upon by حَتَّىٰ (until)
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to قَالَ (he said) later in the verse.
Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and'
This is a preposition meaning 'with' that shows someone or something is accompanying someone/something else. In this ayah, it's used with يوسف (Joseph).
Added هُ at the end to show who is being referred to (him = يوسف). This combines the preposition مَعَ (with) with the pronoun هُ (him).
Changed the word from an action (confining) to a place (prison) by adding special vowel marks
Added ٱل at the beginning to make it specific, like saying 'the prison' instead of just any prison
Added َ at the end because it's the object being entered (after وَدَخَلَ 'and he entered')
Adding short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he was young' in the past

steps.m: two young men (who entered prison with يوسف)
steps.n: The ان ending shows there are exactly two young men. These are the two prisoners mentioned in the story who entered prison with Prophet Yusuf.
Added short vowels (fatha) to make the past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first two letters to form the basic word for 'one'
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing something (in this case, the one who is speaking)
Added هُمَا with long alif at the end to show this refers to one of two people (the two young men mentioned earlier in the story)
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain or definite
Added ي at the end to show 'I' am doing the action. This word connects to أَرَىٰنِىٓ (I see myself) in this ayah.
Changed the writing style of ي to ى with ٓ (alif khanjariyah) which doesn't change the meaning
Root letters r-a-y became 'he saw' by adding the vowel marks (fat-ha)
Added hamza at beginning to make it causative (showing something to someone)
Added alif maksura at the end to show the long vowel sound
Added نِى at the end to show the object is 'myself'
Added elongation mark to show lengthened pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: I press/squeeze (in the dream of أَحَدُهُمَآ)
steps.n: Changed to present tense first person ('I') form. This is being said by one of the two young men in prison describing his dream to Prophet Yusuf.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make the noun for wine. This is what the prisoner saw himself squeezing in his dream that he told to Prophet Yusuf.
Added ًا (an) at the end to show this is the object being acted upon
The middle letter و changes to ا when making past tense. This is a common pattern with words containing و.
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it in the sentence, showing these actions happened one after another

steps.m: to be behind or come later

steps.n: The long alif at start makes this into a word meaning 'other/another'
Added ال to make it specific - this refers to the second of the two young men mentioned in the story

steps.m: the other (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said) in this ayah
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English
Added ي at the end to show the speaker is talking about themselves. The ّ (shadda) combines with the ي to make نِّ
Changed the final ي to ى with an alif (ٓ) above it. This is just a writing style change that doesn't affect the meaning
Basic root 'to see' became 'he saw' by adding the vowels a-a (fatha marks) to make it past tense for 'he'
Adding أ at the beginning changes the meaning to 'make someone see' or 'show'
Added alif maksura (ى with two dots) at the end to show the proper spelling of this verb
Added نِي at the end to show the action is happening to 'me', and added the long vowel mark (ـٓ) to show emphasis
Added the short 'a' vowels (fatHa) to make it mean 'he carried' in the past
Changed from past tense 'he carried' to present tense 'I carry' by adding أ at start
Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'above'. This word is possessed by رَأْسِى (my head) in this ayah, showing the bread was above his head.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being possessed by another word (رَأْسِى/my head)
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to form the basic noun
Added kasra to show this word follows فَوْقَ (above/over) in the ayah

steps.m: my head (being acted upon by فَوْقَ)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show the head belongs to the speaker
Root letters got short vowels (damma and sukoon) to change from the action of baking to the food itself

steps.m: bread (object being carried in the dream)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being carried in the dream mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, and damma at end
Changed from 'he eats' to 'she eats' by replacing يَ with تَ at start
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the thing that flies
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ط with shadda because it comes after ال
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the eating in the sentence
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added هُ at the end to show this is happening to or from 'him/it'. In this ayah, it refers back to the interpretation (تَأْوِيل) that the two young men are asking about.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to add intensity to the meaning
Added نا at the end to show who is being told (us). This word appears in a request to Yusuf to interpret dreams.

steps.m: to interpret or explain the true meaning

steps.n: Adding short vowels (fatha) to make the basic past tense verb form
Changed from basic verb 'to return/interpret' into a noun by adding 'ta' at start and 'ee' sound in middle to mean 'interpretation' - this is a common pattern for making nouns from verbs
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by something
Added هِ to show it belongs to something (referring to the dream)
Added بِ to show this is about the means or method of interpretation
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English
Added نا at the end as 'we'. This word connects to نَرَىٰكَ (we see you) in this ayah, showing who is doing the seeing.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added نَ at start to show 'we' and changed ending to show present tense
Added alif khanjariya (small alif) to show the long vowel sound
Added كَ at the end to show who is being seen
This is the basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere, belongs to a group, or is taken from something
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word (المحسنين). This is a helper vowel that makes the words flow together better when speaking.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing the middle vowels turns 'was good' into 'doer of good'
The ين ending shows this is plural (more than one). The word is in the genitive case because it comes after the preposition مِنَ (from/of).

steps.m: the ones who do good (of يَرَىٰكَ)
steps.n: The ال shows we're talking about specific good-doers. This word comes after مِنَ (from) in the phrase 'we see you as being from the good-doers'

steps.m: the ones who do good (acted on)
steps.n: The fatha (small diagonal line) at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Added fathas (َ) after each letter to show this happened in the past and was done by 'he'
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has و in middle)

steps.m: not (negates يَأْتِيكُمَا - will come to you two)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it not true. Here it's saying the food will not come to them.

steps.m: not (negates يَأْتِيكُمَا - will come to you two)
steps.n: Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Added يَ at the beginning to show it's happening now or in the future, with vowel marks to complete the word structure

steps.m: he comes/arrives to both of you
steps.n: Added كُمَا directly to show the action affects exactly two people
Changes from action of feeding/tasting into the thing that is fed/eaten. This word relates to تُرْزَقَانِهِ (you both are provided with it) in this ayah.
Adding the tanween (double damma) makes it 'a food' instead of 'the food'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to passive voice present tense - shows receiving rather than giving
Added dual form to show two people are receiving

steps.m: you two are provided with it (subject of طَعَامٌ)
steps.n: Added pronoun suffix to show what is being provided. This word relates to طَعَامٌ (food) in the ayah, showing who receives the food.

steps.m: except (what I will tell you about before it comes to you)
steps.n: This is a particle used for making exceptions. It connects what comes before it with what comes after it by excluding something. Here it introduces what Yusuf will tell them about their food before it arrives.
The shadda (ّ) makes the middle letter stronger, which makes the meaning of telling news stronger too
Adding تُ at the end shows that 'I' am the one doing the action
Changed كُمْ to كُمَا to indicate the object is specifically 'you two' rather than 'you all'

steps.m: to return something to its origin/beginning
he interpreted (made something return to its original meaning)
steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show someone actively explaining the true meaning of something

steps.m: interpretation (the act of finding original meaning)
steps.n: Changed from 'he interpreted' to 'interpretation' by adding تَ at start and ي in middle - this makes it a noun showing the action instead of doing the action
Added بِ at beginning to show 'with/by means of' and ِ at end because it follows a preposition

steps.m: with its interpretation (connected to نَبَّأْتُكُمَا - I informed you both)
steps.n: Added هِ (hi) at the end to show 'whose interpretation' - connects back to the food mentioned
Root letters form a simple word meaning 'before' (like 'pre-' in English)

steps.m: before (connected to أَن يَأْتِيَكُمَا - 'that it comes to you two')
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (أَن)
This is a connecting word that introduces what will happen next. Here it connects to the verb يَأْتِيَكُمَا (it comes to you two) to show what will happen.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed from past tense (he came) to present tense (he comes) by adding يَ at the start and changing the middle vowels

steps.m: it comes to you both (referring to food in طَعَامٌ)
steps.n: Added كُمَا to show the action affects both people being spoken to. This refers to the two prisoners Yusuf is speaking with about their food (طَعَامٌ).
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this'
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) and alif with bar (ٰ) changes 'this' to 'that', showing something farther away
Adding كُمَا at the end shows this is addressing exactly two people
Combined two words: مِن meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'
When مِن comes before a word starting with م, the ن merges into the م making it stronger (shown by the ّ mark). This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Adding shadda makes it mean 'to make someone know' = 'to teach'
Adding نِ at the end shows the action was done to 'me'
Adding ي after نِ completes the 'me' pronoun suffix

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture completely

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does complete nurturing and care

steps.m: Lord (being described or connected to something)
steps.n: The kasra (ِ) shows this word is connected to something else coming after it

steps.m: my Lord (the one who taught me)
steps.n: Added ى to show it's my Lord, and the long vowel mark (ٓ) to show emphasis. This Lord is the one who taught me, as shown by عَلَّمَ (taught) in the verse.
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'truly' or 'certainly' in English
Added ي at the end to show 'I' am speaking. The ن gets a shadda because it combines with the ي. This word connects to طَعَامٌ (food) that Yusuf is talking about.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added sukun (stop mark) to show this is a complete word when stopping
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action. This word connects to 'the religion of a people' (مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ) which comes after it in the ayah.
Added kasra under first letter and connected the letters to make a basic noun

steps.m: a complete system of beliefs and practices (like a religion)
steps.n: Added ة at the end and shadda on middle letter to show this is one complete system

steps.m: the religion/way (that I have left behind)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being left behind, connecting to تَرَكْتُ (I left) in the ayah
Forms the basic noun for a group of people who establish or maintain something together

steps.m: a people (who do not believe in اللَّهِ)
steps.n: The tanwin (double kasra) shows this is indefinite (any people) and is being described by what follows it in the ayah. This word is the subject of لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (they do not believe).
Basic negative particle that negates what comes after it
The ن from إِنِّى combines with the ل to create a shadda. This is a common sound change when these words come together.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed pattern by adding alif at start to make it mean 'actively putting trust in something/someone' rather than just 'feeling secure'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something is done or the means by which it happens. Added ِ at the end because it follows the preposition بِ. The ٱ is just a different way of writing ال.
Added damma to show proper pronunciation of the pronoun meaning 'they'
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, showing these are the same people mentioned earlier who don't believe in Allah
Changed from basic root letters to a describing word by adding the short vowels 'a' and 'i' to show something that comes after or is delayed
Changed first letter to long alif to show it's more/most delayed (therefore last/final)
Added feminine ending to create special meaning referring to the afterlife

steps.m: in the Hereafter (connected to يُؤْمِنُونَ - they believe)
steps.n: Added 'bi' (in/with) at start and 'al' (the) to show belief IN the Hereafter
Basic third person masculine plural pronoun. In this ayah, it refers back to the people who don't believe, connecting to the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe).
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action
Added ون at the end to show this refers to multiple people. The alif changed to a long alif (ٰ). This word describes 'they' (هم) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and merged the ت with shadda to show doing something thoroughly
Added وَ (and) at start and changed اِ to ٱ because it comes after وَ
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action in the past

steps.m: to follow a path or way

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changes from action (following) to name of the path/way that is followed (like a religion)

steps.m: the way/religion (as object of اتَّبَعْتُ - I followed)
steps.n: Added fatha at end to show this is the object being followed in the phrase 'I followed the religion'
Root letters أ-ب-و became the simple word 'father' by adding the vowel mark fatHa (َ)
Changed from 'father' to 'fathers' by making it plural - added long aa sound after first letter and aa sound at end
Added kasra at end to prepare for possession
Added ي with elongation mark to show possession (my)
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew 'Avraham' meaning 'father of many'. It's one of the most important names in the Quran as Ibrahim was a great prophet.
Added fatha at the end to show this name is connected to the word مِلَّةَ (religion) that came before it in the ayah, meaning 'the religion of Ibrahim'
The fatha at the end shows this name is receiving the action from the earlier verb اتَّبَعْتُ (I followed)

steps.m: and Isaac (connected to إِبْرٰهِيمَ in list)
steps.n: The و connects this name to the previous name Ibrahim in the list of ancestors being followed

steps.m: Jacob (name mentioned in list with إِبْرٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحٰقَ)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew Ya'akov (יַעֲקֹב). In the Quran, he was a prophet and the son of Isaac.
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of a verb (اتَّبَعْتُ - I followed)

steps.m: and Jacob (completing list with إِبْرٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحٰقَ)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this name to the previous names in the list (Ibrahim and Isaac)
This is a negative particle that negates the verb كَانَ (was) that comes after it in this ayah. Together they mean 'it was not'.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense
The و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Starting with the preposition ل which shows purpose or direction

steps.m: for us (connected to كَانَ - it was)
steps.n: Added نَا which means 'us' to show who the preposition is directed towards. The آ (long alif) is used instead of regular ا to show it's at the end of the word. This word connects to كَانَ (it was) in the ayah.

steps.m: that (introduces what was not possible: نُّشْرِكَ)
