steps.n: This is a particle that connects to what comes after it. Here it introduces what was not possible for them to do - associating partners with Allah. It connects with نُّشْرِكَ (that we associate partners).

steps.m: to join something with something else as a partner/associate

steps.n: Changed from basic root letters into a command form by adding special marks (like dots and lines) above and below the letters
Added نُ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: that we join as partner (with بِٱللَّهِ - with Allah)
steps.n: The shadda on ن shows this is connected to the previous word أَن (that), making it mean 'that we...'
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
The -i sound at the end shows this means 'of Allah' or 'belonging to Allah'

steps.m: with/by Allah (object of نُّشْرِكَ - we associate)
steps.n: The بِ at the beginning means 'with/by' and ٱ is added before the ل when it has a shadda. This word is what is being associated with in the phrase 'that we associate [partners] with Allah'.
This is a preposition that connects words by showing where something comes from, what it belongs to, or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects شَىْءٍ (anything) to what follows it.

steps.m: to want or be a thing

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make it into a noun meaning 'thing'
The word gets a double line under it (tanwin) because it comes after مِن. Also, the letter ي is written differently (as ى) when followed by ء.
The basic word ذا means 'this' for something close. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's being stretched to point to something farther away.
Adding لِ makes the word point to something even farther away, changing 'this' to 'that'

steps.m: that (referring to what was mentioned before in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes it specifically point to something mentioned before. Here it refers to the previous statement about following the religion of Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Yaqub.

steps.m: from/of (connects شَىْءٍ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that connects words to show where something comes from or what it belongs to. Here it connects شَىْءٍ (anything) to what comes before it, showing we shouldn't associate anything with Allah.

steps.m: to give extra or do more than required

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create a noun meaning the result of giving extra - which is a favor or blessing
The ِ at the end shows this favor belongs to Allah, connecting it to the next word اللَّهِ (Allah)
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل, making this the unique name for The One God
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word بِ (with/by) before it. Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
When adding a suffix to عَلَى, the ى changes to يْ to make it easier to connect with the next part
Added نَا at the end to show this means 'us'
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on top of'. Since this is a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous mention of عَلَيْنَا (upon us) in the ayah

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others

steps.n: Changed from a root meaning 'being social' to mean all humans/mankind (since humans are social beings)
Added ال to show we're talking about specific people, and the ن gets a shadda when ال is added

steps.m: of the people (after على/upon in this ayah)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it comes after على (upon) in this ayah
Added alif and kasra to form the basic particle. This word connects contrasting ideas.
Added shadda and fatha at the end to make the word stronger. This makes the contrast more emphatic.
Added و at the beginning to connect this contrasting statement to the previous one. In this ayah, it connects to the previous statement about not associating partners with Allah.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to make it mean 'more/most' instead of just 'many'. Like changing 'big' to 'bigger/biggest' in English. The sukun (little circle) on ك makes it easier to say.
This is an irregular plural form derived from the concept of being sociable
The word 'people' (ناس) comes from a root about gathering together. Adding ال (the) makes it 'the people'. The ن gets doubled (shadda) because it comes after ال.

steps.m: of the people (after على/upon in this ayah)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) at the end because this word comes after على (upon) in the ayah
Basic negative particle that negates the verb يَشْكُرُونَ (they thank/are thankful) that follows it
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense verb form
Changes from past to present by adding ي at start and changing middle vowels

steps.m: they are grateful/thank (referring to most people in أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ)
steps.n: Changed from 'he' to 'they' by adding ون at the end to match with 'most people' in the ayah

steps.m: to accompany or be close to someone

steps.n: Added ا after first letter to show who does the action (like adding -er in English). Also added vowel marks to show pronunciation.
Added ى at the end to show both possession (my) and dual form (two)
Added يٰ at the start to show we're calling out to these companions
Root meaning 'to lock up' becomes the place where locking up happens (prison) by adding vowels
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite

steps.m: of the prison (possessed by صاحبي - my two companions)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this prison belongs to or is connected to صاحبي (my two companions)

steps.m: to nurture, care for, or have authority over

steps.n: Root letters combined with shadda to make 'lord/master' - someone who takes care of others
Changes from single lord to multiple lords by adding أَ at start and ا before last letter - this is a special plural form
Added tanween to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added hamza with fatha at beginning to make the statement into a question
Added the shadda to show this is done with extra force or intensity
Added ت at start to show this happens to someone/something rather than someone doing it
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show this describes someone affected by the separation
Added ون at the end to show this refers to multiple men, and added shadda on م to connect with previous word in the ayah
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'goodness'
This is a connecting word that links two parts of a question. Here it connects back to the earlier question word ءَأَرْبَابٌ (are lords).
Added a small i-sound (kasra) at the end because it joins with 'Allah' in the next word
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
Added damma to show this word is the subject
Adding short vowels (fatha and kasra) turns the root into a past tense verb meaning 'became alone'
Changed from a verb (being alone) to a noun (one) by adding alif - this is a common pattern for making numbers and adjectives

steps.m: The One (as a name/title of Allah)
steps.n: Added 'the' to make it a specific title for Allah

steps.m: The One (as a name/title of Allah)
steps.n: Changed the alif to a long alif (ٰ) and added damma at the end to show it's the subject
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The shadda (ّ) doubles the middle letter and ار ending shows this is someone who does this action intensively and repeatedly
Adding ال makes it specific - here it refers to Allah as The Supreme Overpowering One

steps.m: The All-Overpowering One (attribute of اللَّهُ, subject)
steps.n: The ُ ending shows this is the subject of the sentence, and the hamza is written as ٱ when connected to ال
This is a question word that asks about things (not people). In this ayah, it's asking about what they worship besides Allah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The ت at the start changes it from 'he worshipped' to 'you worship', and shows it's happening now instead of in the past
The ون ending shows this is addressing multiple people (you all)
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to. Here it connects 'besides Him' (دُونِهِ) with what follows in the ayah.
Adding damma (ُ) changes it to mean 'besides/other than'
Adding kasra shows this word is connected to something that comes after it
Adding هِ with the small ۦٓ after it means 'him'. This word comes after مِن (meaning 'from/besides') in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that limits or restricts what comes before it to what follows after it. In this ayah, it restricts worship to Allah alone.
The alif is lengthened to آ because this word appears at a pause position in recitation. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
Changes from verb 'to name' into the noun 'a name'
Changed from singular 'name' to plural 'names' by using the special pattern أَفْعَال (af'aal) for plurals

steps.m: names (as objects that you made up)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this word is the object of سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ (you named them) in this ayah
Added vowel marks and doubled the middle letter to show intensive meaning
Added ي with sukun to complete the word before adding suffixes
Added تُ (tu) at the end to make it 'I named' in the past tense
Added م with damma to show plural 'you'
Added و to show 'him' as the object being named
Added هَآ to show the object being named
This is the basic singular form of the second person pronoun
Changed the final vowel to prepare for plural ending
Added م with sukun to make it plural, and fatha on first letter for proper pronunciation
Connected root letters with vowel marks to form basic singular noun
Changes from singular 'father' to plural 'fathers' with a special plural pattern
Changed ending to show it's doing the action (nominative case)
Added possession suffix for 'your (plural)'
Added conjunction prefix 'and'
Added fatha to show basic pronunciation of the word
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning, like saying 'that which' instead of just 'what'
Changed the alif to alif madd (آ) to show a longer sound, but this doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at beginning makes the meaning causative - someone making something else descend
Changed first letter to ل and added ال to make it specifically refer to Allah, the One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of أَنزَلَ - He sent down)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize uniqueness, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added damma to show Allah is the one doing the action in this sentence
Added kasra to the letter ب to show it's a preposition

steps.m: with it/her (referring to the names/أَسْمَآءً mentioned before)
steps.n: Added ها which means 'it/her' to show what we are doing something with. Here it points back to the names (أَسْمَآءً) mentioned before.
Basic preposition that shows where something starts or what it's made of, like saying 'from' or 'of' in English. In this ayah, it's used with دُونِهِ to mean 'besides/other than Him'.
Added kasra (ِ) under the م to show how to pronounce it correctly. This word connects with دُونِهِ to mean 'besides/other than Him'.

steps.m: to have control or power over something

steps.n: Root meaning 'control' becomes noun 'power' by adding the vowels u and sukoon
Added ان at the end turns 'power' into 'clear authority/strong proof' - like making something stronger

steps.m: any authority/proof (in relation to ما أنزل الله - what Allah has sent down)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is talking about any authority in general, not a specific one. This word is connected to what Allah has sent down (ما أنزل الله) in the verse.
This is a basic particle that introduces conditions or statements. It's like saying 'if' or 'that' in English.
Added kasra at the end because this word connects to ٱلْحُكْمُ (the judgment) that comes after it. Together they mean 'the judgment is only...'
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Added 'the' at the beginning and a damma at the end to show this is the main subject in the sentence

steps.m: except (what comes after إِلَّا is excluded from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: This is a special word that shows something is being excluded or is an exception. It's like saying 'but not' or 'only' in English. In this ayah, it's used to show that the names they worship are just names - nothing more.
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis on the letter ل.
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or ownership, and ِ at the end to show connection. The initial ا is dropped when لِ is added.
This is the basic concept of commanding or ordering
Added fathas after each letter to show this happened in the past with 'he' as the doer
Starting with the subordinating conjunction أَنْ which introduces a new clause
Adding the negation particle لَا to negate what comes after
The نْ from أَنْ combines with the ل from لَا, creating a shadda (ّ). This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added وٓا۟ at the end to show multiple people (you all) are doing the action
This is a restriction particle that limits what comes after it. In this ayah, it shows that what they worship are nothing but mere names.
The final alif is lengthened for emphasis in Quranic recitation, but this doesn't change the meaning.
إِيَّا is a special word used to emphasize pronouns when they are the object of an action

steps.m: him specifically (as the object of تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ - you worship)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show this refers to 'him'. This word is the direct object of تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ (you worship) in this ayah, showing who is being worshipped.
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif with bar (ٰ) shows we're pointing to something far away instead of nearby
Adding لِ emphasizes the distance, like saying 'over there' instead of just 'there'
Adding كَ makes it point to something specific being discussed - in this case, it's pointing to 'the upright religion' mentioned right after it
Added kasra to create the basic noun form showing a complete system or way that is followed
Added ال to make it specific and shadda to emphasize the د since it comes after ال
Added damma at the end to show this word is the subject of the sentence
This root carries the basic meaning of standing or being upright, which leads to related meanings of being correct, valuable, or established
The weak letter و changes to ي and gets doubled with shadda to show something that is intensely or perfectly upright/correct
Adding ال makes it specific, pointing to a particular upright thing or system rather than any upright thing

steps.m: the perfectly upright/correct one (describing الدين/the religion)
steps.n: The ُ shows this word is describing الدين (the religion) in this ayah, showing that the religion mentioned is the perfectly upright one
Basic particle that introduces a contrast with what came before. The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show a strong contrast.

steps.m: and yet/but still (connects while contrasting with previous statement)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this contrasting statement with what came before, showing the relationship between the ideas
Adding أَ at the beginning turns the word into a comparative/superlative form
Adds أَ at the start to make it mean 'more' or 'most' - like turning 'many' into 'more/most numerous'
Changed from 'more' to 'made more' by adding the fatha mark at the end

steps.m: to be friendly, sociable, gather together
steps.n: This root relates to people coming together and being social with each other
Forms the plural noun 'people' from the root meaning of gathering and being social
Adds emphasis to show these are THE people, not just any people
Adds the definite article 'the' to specify these particular people

steps.m: of the people (emphasized, possessed by أَكْثَرَ)
steps.n: Adds kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by أَكْثَرَ (most), forming the phrase 'most of the people'
Basic negative particle that negates the verb يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) that comes after it, making it mean 'they do not know'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The prefix ي shows this is happening now (present tense) and 'he' is doing it

steps.m: they know (referring to most people/أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ)
steps.n: The suffix ون indicates masculine plural subject (they)

steps.m: to accompany or be close to someone

steps.n: Adding short vowels (fat-ha, kasra, fat-ha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he accompanied' in the past
Added alif after first letter to show someone who does the action
Added ي at the end to show they are my companions, and changed the ending to show there are two of them (dual form)
Added يٰ at start to make it a call or address to someone
Changes from verb 'to imprison' to noun meaning 'imprisonment'
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: of the prison (possessed by صاحبي - two companions)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this prison belongs to or relates to the two companions (صاحبي) who are being addressed in the ayah
Added shadda to emphasize the word. This particle introduces a new topic or contrasts with something previously mentioned. In this ayah, it's used to contrast between the fates of two prisoners.
The final alif is elongated to indicate a natural pause in speech, as this word typically comes at the start of a new phrase or topic
Added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'one' that will be the subject of 'will give drink'

steps.m: one of you two (subject of فَيَسْقِى)
steps.n: Added كُمَا to show this refers to 'one of you two' when speaking to two people (the two prisoners)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: and then he will pour drink (for رَبَّهُۥ - his master)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this action follows after something else. This describes what one of the prisoners will do for his master after leaving prison.

steps.m: to nurture, care for, raise up

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and takes care of others completely
Added هُ at the end to show this is his lord. This word is being acted on by the verb يَسْقِى (serves/gives drink to) in this ayah.

The fatha and sukun pattern creates a noun meaning the substance that results from fermentation. This word خَمْر specifically refers to wine because the fermentation process 'covers' or changes the original grape juice.
The ًا (tanween fatha) shows this word is receiving the action. Here, the wine is what's being served by one prisoner to his master.
This is a special word that introduces a new topic or shifts focus to something new. It's like saying 'Now, talking about...' in English.

steps.m: and as for (connects to فَيُصْلَبُ - he will be crucified)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this statement to what came before. In this ayah, it's connecting to the previous statement about one prisoner to introduce what will happen to the other prisoner.

steps.m: to come after or be behind

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (to come after) to an adjective meaning 'other/another'. The ُ at the end shows it's describing something
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific - referring to the second of two prison mates being discussed
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive voice by adding يُ at start and changing vowels to show something is being done to someone
Added فَ at beginning to show this happens after something else (connects to previous statement about what happens to the two prisoners)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense by adding تَ and changing vowels

steps.m: and then she eats food (subject of الطَّيْرُ - the birds)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this action happens after and as a result of what was mentioned before in the ayah
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to change from action to noun
Added ال at beginning and shadda on ط to show it's a specific bird or group of birds. The shadda appears because ل and ط merge together in pronunciation.

steps.m: the birds (subject of فَتَأْكُلُ - and then it/they will eat)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the eating in فَتَأْكُلُ (and then it/they will eat)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added kasra under م to show how to pronounce it correctly
Adding the vowel marks (َ and ْ) turns the root letters into the basic noun meaning 'head'
Adding the kasra at the end shows this word is the object of a preposition
Adding هِۦ at the end shows possession - 'his head'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: it was decided (about the matter you both asked about)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it. This connects to الْأَمْرُ (the matter) mentioned right after this word.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and change from verb to noun meaning
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: the matter/affair (subject of قُضِىَ - was decreed)

This is the base demonstrative word that points to something. It's one of the special words in Arabic that doesn't come from a three-letter root.
Adding ٱلّ (al with shadda) changes this from just pointing to something to connecting two ideas together. Here it connects 'one of you' with 'will serve his master'.

steps.m: in it (referring to الْأَمْرُ - the matter)

Changed to present tense with ت prefix for 'you'

The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). Added fatha vowels to show past tense.

steps.m: and he said (connected to previous statement)
steps.n: Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, showing sequence or connection of ideas

steps.m: to think or believe something without being completely sure

steps.m: one who is saved (in a chain of possession)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this word is indefinite and is part of a possession chain (idafa) in the sentence

Changed from 'I' to 'you all' by adding م with a shadda on the ت

steps.m: to leave something as it is

steps.n: The root letters get vowels added (َ َ ُ) to make it 'I leave' in present tense
Removing وَ (wa) changes it from 'and we leave' to just 'leave!' - a command for a group
Adding هُ shows what is being left (it)

steps.m: so leave it! (command to many people)
steps.n: Adding فَ connects this command to Yusuf's previous statement about planting for seven years (فَ means 'so' or 'then')
This is a basic preposition showing that something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it shows the location where they should leave the harvested grain - in its ears/stalks.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a writing change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Adds vowel marks to make the word pronounceable
Added kasra at the end to show this grain spike belongs to something or is connected to 'in' (في) in the sentence

steps.m: its grain spike (referring to what was harvested)
steps.n: Added هِۦٓ to show possession. The ۦٓ marks help with pronunciation and connecting to the next word.

steps.m: except (what comes after this word is excluded from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: This is a fixed word that shows exception - it tells us that what comes after it (قَلِيلًا - a little) is not included in what was mentioned before (the harvested grain that should be left in its ears).

steps.m: to be small in amount or number

steps.n: The ي makes it into an adjective describing something that has the quality of being small in amount

steps.m: a little (describes how much is eaten in تَأْكُلُونَ)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to make it describe 'how much' (works with the verb 'eating' to say 'eat a little')
Starting with the two separate words that combine to form this word: مِن (from) and مَا (what)

steps.m: from what (of what تَأْكُلُونَ you eat)
steps.n: When مِن comes before a word starting with م, the ن merges into the م creating a doubled م sound (shown by the ّ mark). This is a pronunciation rule and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added تَ at start to make it 'you/she' and changed middle vowels to show present tense
Added ونَ to show that many people are eating together
This word connects two events in sequence, showing that one happens after another. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَأْتِى (comes), showing what happens next in the sequence of events.
Connected the root letters together
Added يَ at beginning to show present/future tense, with أْ to help pronunciation
Added ى at end to show present tense form
Basic preposition showing starting point or source. In this ayah, it connects 'that' (ذٰلِكَ) to 'after' (بَعْدِ) to mean 'after that'.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (ذٰلِكَ meaning 'that')
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby. It's a foundational word that doesn't come from a root.
The alif is written as a small mark above the ذ to show it's still pronounced like ذا but written shorter.
Adding لِ changes the word from pointing to something near to pointing to something far away.
Adding كَ at the end turns this into the complete word meaning 'that over there'
Added vowel marks to the root letters to make it mean 'seven' as a countable number
Added sukun (silent mark) on middle letter to make it a basic noun form
Added tanween damma to show this is an indefinite noun meaning 'seven years' based on context
Added vowels and ي to make a word describing something that has this quality
Changed vowels and added ا to make it plural - like how 'strong' becomes 'strong ones'
Added ٌ to show this is the subject doing the eating in يَأْكُلْنَ (they eat)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, changed vowels to show ongoing action

steps.m: they (feminine) eat (referring to the seven hard years that will eat/consume what was stored)
steps.n: Added نَ suffix to show feminine plural subject (referring to the seven hard years mentioned in the ayah), with sukun before the suffix
A relative pronoun meaning 'what' - it's connecting to the verb يَأْكُلْنَ (they consume) to show what is being consumed. In this ayah, it refers to what was stored/saved up.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding a shadda (double mark) on the middle letter changes it from 'went forward' to 'sent ahead' - making the action stronger
Changed to command form (imperative) by removing final vowel

steps.m: you all sent ahead (in the past)
steps.n: Changed تَ suffix to تُمْ to make it plural 'you all'
Added fatha to the basic preposition ل to connect it with what follows
Added هُ to show this is 'for him/it'

steps.m: for them (feminine) (object of يَأْكُلْنَ - they consume)
steps.n: Added نَّ to change 'him' to 'them (feminine)'. This word shows who receives the action of consuming (يَأْكُلْنَ) in the verse.

steps.m: except (what comes after it is excluded from يَأْكُلْنَ - they consume)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that excludes something from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it excludes 'a little' (قَلِيلًا) from what the seven difficult years will consume. It shows that while most stored grain will be eaten, a small amount will remain.
Adding vowels to show this describes a quality of being small/few
Added ي to make it an adjective describing how much of something there is

steps.m: a little (of what you stored) (object of يَأْكُلْنَ)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how much will be eaten. This word is the object of يَأْكُلْنَ (they eat) in this ayah.
Combined the two basic words مِن (from) and مَا (what)
The ن in مِن combines with the م in مَا, making a stronger م sound (shown by the ّ mark)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Added تُ prefix for present tense and changed vowels to show ongoing action

steps.m: you all (masculine) store/protect (referring to storing grain in this ayah)
steps.n: Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people are being addressed about storing grain
This word connects two events in time, showing that one happens after another. Here it connects to the verb يَأْتِى (comes) to show what happens next in the sequence of years being described.
Connected the root letters together
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and who is doing the action (he), added ْ (sukun) on ت to help pronunciation
Added ى at the end to show it's happening in the present
Basic preposition showing where something starts from or what comes after something else. In this ayah, it connects 'that' (ذٰلِكَ) with 'after' (بَعْدِ) to mean 'after that'.
Added a small ن (called nun ghunna) at the end to show how to pronounce it smoothly with the next word
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (ذٰلِكَ meaning 'that')
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (alif khanjariya) to show elongation
Adding لِ changes the meaning from pointing to something near to pointing to something far

steps.m: that (pointing to something far in the past or future)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it specifically point to something distant in time or space. In this ayah, it points to a future year being discussed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The و changes to ا because it's between two vowels. The word becomes a noun meaning 'year' and is the subject of يَأْتِى (comes/will come) in this ayah.
This is a preposition showing location or time
Added هِ to show what the preposition applies to. The هِ refers back to عَامٌ (year) mentioned earlier in the ayah, showing that something happens 'in that year'.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Changed from active (he helps) to passive (he is helped) by changing the vowel patterns

steps.m: to be social, to keep company with others

steps.n: Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he was social'
Changed from verb 'to be social' to the plural noun meaning 'people' who live in society together
Added ال (the) at the start to mean 'the people' instead of just 'people'
Added damma to show this is the subject receiving rain/aid in the phrase 'يُغَاثُ ٱلنَّاسُ' (the people are given rain/aid)
Adding the conjunction 'and' at the beginning
Adding the preposition 'in' after 'and'
Adds pronoun 'it' referring back to the year (عَامٌ) mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: to press or squeeze out liquid

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show past tense
Changed to present tense by adding ya- prefix and changing vowels

steps.m: they press/squeeze (referring to people pressing grapes and olives for juice/oil)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show many people doing the action
Added fatha vowels to make it past tense. The و changes to ا because it's between two vowels - this is a common change in words with و in the middle.
Adding و at the beginning connects this word to what came before it
Changes from verb 'to rule' into noun meaning 'one who rules'

steps.m: the king (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added ال to show we're talking about a specific king, and ُ to show this king is the one doing the action (saying) in this verse
Root 'come' changed to 'bring!' command by adding hamza and changing vowels - like telling one person 'Bring it!'
Added و to make the command plural (addressing multiple people)
Added نِى at the end to show the command is to bring 'me'
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning with/by/in
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'him', referring back to Yusuf in this story
Added small ي (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide to show the هِ connects smoothly to the next word
This is a word that shows when something happens. The ا at the end makes it work with other words in a sentence.

steps.m: and when (connects to جَآءَهُ - came to him)
steps.n: The فَ is added at the beginning to connect this with what happened before. It's like saying 'and then' or 'so when' in English. In this ayah, it connects to when the messenger came to Yusuf.
Root letters ج-ي-أ changed to جَاءَ by adding fathas and turning ي into ا to make 'he came'

steps.m: he came to him (messenger came to king)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who was come to. In this ayah, the messenger (الرسول) came to the king.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (sent) to a noun (messenger) by adding و
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda because it comes after ال
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in قَالَ (he said)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense form
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he returned' to 'return!' by making it a command form (imperative)
Adding sukun at end to complete the command form
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to رَبِّكَ (your Lord) to show 'to your Lord'
The small alif (ىٰ) at the end is just a writing style that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to care for and develop something/someone to their full potential
master who takes complete care (of someone/something)
steps.n: The doubled ب (shown by ّ) makes this word mean someone who does a lot of caring and developing

steps.m: master who takes complete care (connected to إِلَىٰ)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word connects to the word إِلَىٰ (to) that comes before it

steps.m: your master who takes complete care (connected to إِلَىٰ)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end shows this master belongs to you (singular). The whole phrase إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ means 'to your master'
Added vowel marks to make it a command form. The sukun (ْ) on first letter and fatha (َ) on middle letter with sukun on last letter makes it a command.

steps.m: so ask him! (about the women) (command after ارْجِعْ)
steps.n: The هُ shows who should be asked - in this case referring back to the king. This command comes after being told to 'go back' (ارْجِعْ).
This is an interrogative particle used to ask questions. In this ayah, it's being used by the king to ask 'what is the matter with the women?' The fatha on the م helps show this is a question word.
Changed root letters into a noun by adding vowels - a fat-ha (a) and sukoon (stop)
Added an alif after the first letter to make it a longer word form

steps.m: state/condition (subject of what is being asked about)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the subject being asked about in the question 'What is the state/condition?'
Adding ة makes it refer specifically to a group of women

steps.m: of the women (object of فَسْـَٔلْهُ/ask him about)
steps.n: The -i ending shows these women are being asked about (like saying 'about the women' instead of just 'women')

steps.m: of the women (object of فَسْـَٔلْهُ/ask him about)
steps.n: Adding 'ال' (the) makes it refer to specific women - the ones in the story who cut their hands

steps.m: who, those (feminine plural referring to the women who cut their hands)
steps.n: Added a long alif (ا) to change the relative pronoun from singular feminine to plural feminine. This word refers back to the women (ٱلنِّسْوَةِ) mentioned in the previous part of the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the action more intense or repetitive
Changed the last vowel to sukun to prepare for adding the feminine plural ending
Added نَ to show multiple women did this action. In this ayah, it refers to the women who cut their hands when they saw Prophet Yusuf's beauty.
Connected the root letters to form the basic singular noun
Changes from singular 'hand' (يَد) to broken plural 'hands' (أَيْدِي) by adding alif at start and changing middle vowels

steps.m: their hands/powers (referring to the women who cut their hands)
steps.n: Adds feminine plural possessive suffix to show these belong to the women mentioned in the story
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' in English
Added the kasra (ِ) and shadda (ّ) marks. The shadda makes the emphasis stronger. This word emphasizes the statement about رَبِّى (my Lord) that follows it.

steps.m: to care for and help grow
lord (one who takes complete care of others)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this person does a lot of caring and helping
Adding ي at the end shows this is my lord, like adding 'my' in English
Changed from action to the name of the action itself
Added بِ to show how something was done
Added هِنَّ to show it belongs to multiple women (referring to the women mentioned in the previous ayah who cut their hands)
Adding ي between the second and third root letters with the vowels makes this an intensive form, showing complete and perfect knowledge. This form is often used for Allah's attributes.
The tanween (double damma) shows this is describing Allah's attribute of complete knowledge in a general, unlimited way
Added fathas to make the basic past tense form before weak letter change
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
This is a question word used to ask about things. In this ayah, it's being used by the king to ask the women about their situation (خَطْبُكُنَّ) when they tried to tempt Yusuf.

steps.m: to speak to, address, be concerned with

steps.n: Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
Changes from verb 'he addressed' to noun 'matter/concern'. This is what's being asked about in the question 'what is your matter?'
Adds possession - 'your matter' rather than just 'matter'. This word is the subject of the question word ما (what), so it takes the nominative case.
Changes 'your' to refer to multiple women being addressed. In this verse, the king is asking the women about their matter/concern regarding Yusuf.
This is a time particle that connects to the verb رٰوَدتُّنَّ (you sought). It shows when something happened in the past, like saying 'at the time when you sought...'

steps.m: to try to persuade or influence someone

steps.n: The long ا makes the meaning more intense, showing repeated attempts to persuade
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action

steps.m: I tried to persuade you (feminine plural)
steps.n: Added نَّ to show the action was done to multiple women

steps.m: I tried to persuade you (feminine plural)
steps.n: Changed alif to alif with line above (آ) and added shadda on ت for easier pronunciation
This is a proper name, meaning 'may He (God) add/increase', originally from Hebrew (יוסף Yosef)
Adding damma vowel marks to help with pronunciation

steps.m: Joseph (as the object of رٰوَدتُّنَّ - you all tempted)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows Joseph is being acted upon - he is the one the women tried to tempt
This is a preposition that shows movement or separation away from something. In this ayah, it shows Yusuf being tempted away from his proper self/soul (نَّفْسِهِۦ).

steps.m: to breathe, to be valuable to oneself

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make the common word for 'self/soul'
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to/is possessed by something
Adding هِ (hi) at the end shows it belongs to 'him' - changes from just 'self' to 'his self'
The small ي (called a silent ya) helps with pronunciation, but doesn't change the meaning
Added fatha vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed from 'he said' to 'say!' - it's now a command form. The middle letter ا is dropped and vowels change to make it a command.

steps.m: they (feminine) said (responding to the king's question)
steps.n: Added نَ at the end to show multiple women are speaking. In this ayah, this refers to the women responding to the king's question about what happened with Yusuf.

steps.m: to keep away, to remove from

steps.n: Added vowels to the root letters to make it mean 'he kept away' in the past
The alif changed to a long ā (ٰ). This word became a fixed expression meaning 'far be it' or 'God forbid', usually followed by 'for/from Allah'
Added shadda to emphasize the ل, which is a special feature of this divine name
Added لِ at beginning to show purpose/direction and ِ at end to connect it to what comes after
This is a negation particle that makes the verb عَلِمْنَا (we knew) negative, changing it to 'we did not know'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the last vowel to a sukun (stop mark) to prepare for adding 'we'

steps.m: we knew (subject of قُلْنَ - they [women] said)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did this action. This word is part of what the women said in response.
Starting with the basic preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else
The ى changes to يْ before adding a pronoun suffix - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning
Adding هِ at the end shows this means 'upon him' - the هِ always means 'him' or 'his' when added at the end of words
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects to the word سُوٓءٍ (evil/bad) to show they are free from any evil/bad actions.
Root changed to mean 'badness' or 'evil' by adding damma (u sound) to make it a noun
Added elongation mark (ٓ) over و to show long vowel sound

steps.m: any harmful thing (in response to being asked about يُوسُفَ)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is indefinite and is being acted upon. In this ayah, it's used to deny knowing of any bad thing about Yusuf.
The root letters ق-و-ل became قَالَ (he said) by adding vowels and changing و to ا in the middle
Added ت at the end to show the subject is feminine (she)
Basic noun form for 'person'
Adding alif at start and changing ending to make feminine form meaning 'woman' (related to word for 'person')
Adding feminine marker
The word 'wife' changed slightly at the start (ٱ) and got an 'u' sound at the end (ُ) because she is the subject - the one doing the speaking
Adding ي in the middle makes this describe someone who has a lot of power or high status

steps.m: the mighty one (referring to the minister)
steps.n: The definite article ال makes it refer to a specific person - in this case, the minister of Egypt

steps.m: of the mighty one (referring to the minister's wife speaking)
steps.n: Adding kasra ending shows this describes whose wife is speaking (wife of the mighty one)
Added short 'a' sounds (fatha marks) above letters to make it a readable word meaning 'a moment'
Added long alif at start to emphasize the meaning

steps.m: now (in response to امْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ's confession)
steps.n: The word 'now' comes from 'time/moment'. Adding ال (the) makes it specific - 'this moment right now'. The first letter changes shape when ال is added. Used to point to the exact moment when the truth was revealed.
Quadriliteral root formed by reduplication
Added all vowel markings at once to form the complete past tense verb. This word comes after 'she said' (قَالَتِ) in the ayah, showing when the truth became clear.
A repeated root حص-حص that means something becoming very clear or obvious (past tense)
The doubled letter (shadda) makes it into a noun meaning the concept itself

steps.m: the truth (subject of حَصْحَصَ - became clear/manifest)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it definite, and the ُ shows it's the subject doing the action in حَصْحَصَ (became clear/manifest)
First person singular personal pronoun. In this ayah, it's used by the wife of Al-Azeez to emphasize that she was the one who tried to seduce Yusuf, admitting her guilt.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show repeated attempts or back-and-forth action
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding -tu at the end and doubling the t sound

steps.m: I tried repeatedly to persuade him
steps.n: Added attached pronoun suffix meaning 'him' as the object. This is a complete verb form with its object pronoun attached.

This is a preposition that shows separation or moving away from something. Here it connects راودتن (you sought) with نفسه (himself) to show they tried to tempt Yusuf away from his principles/proper behavior.
Root letters were connected with vowels (َ and ْ) to make the noun meaning 'self' or 'soul'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is being used as an object of a preposition
Added 'his' by putting هِ at the end, and made the ن stronger with ّ (called shadda)
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added إِنَّ which is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it more certain
Added هُ which means 'he' and refers back to Prophet Yusuf in this ayah
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper for the هُ

steps.m: from/among (shows something is part of a group)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from or is part of something else. In this ayah, it's used to show Yusuf is part of the group of truthful people.
Added a fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word الصَّادِقِينَ (the truthful ones)

steps.m: surely/truly from/among (connected to next word الصَّادِقِينَ)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'truly'. This emphasizes that Yusuf is certainly among the truthful ones.
This pattern shows someone who does the action - a truth-teller
Added alif after first letter and vowels to show someone who does the action
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Added ين to show multiple truthful people
Changed initial alif to waṣla form and added final fatḥa because it comes after لَمِنَ (surely from/of) in the verse
This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to something far away
The alif is written as a small mark above the ذ to show it's still pronounced but written differently
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) changes it from pointing to something near to pointing to something far
Adding كَ at the end makes it stronger and clearer when pointing to something far
Added vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on middle letter, and ُ at end
Changed ending to 'a' sound because it follows a word that means 'that'

steps.m: so that he may know (purpose of يُوسُفُ sent the message)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show purpose - this explains why Yusuf sent the message
Added emphasis to show this is a strong statement of fact
Added ى at the end to show this is about 'I/me'. This word connects to لَمْ أَخُنْهُ (I did not betray him) in this ayah, emphasizing that the speaker is declaring their innocence.
This is the basic negative word that shows something didn't happen
Adding مْ changes this from a general negative to one specifically for past actions. It makes the verb أَخُنْهُ (betray him) change from present to past tense.
Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' am doing the action, and ُ on first letter and ْ on second letter to show it's happening now. The و drops out because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: I act unfaithfully to him (negated by لَمْ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is affected by the action (him). The word is part of لَمْ أَخُنْهُ meaning 'I did not act unfaithfully to him'

steps.m: to be hidden or absent from sight

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be hidden' into noun meaning 'hidden thing'
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: in/with the unseen (related to أَخُنْهُ - I did not betray him)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show how something was done, and ِ at the end because it follows the preposition بِ. This word explains how the person did not betray - they didn't betray even in secret/unseen situations.
This is a particle that emphasizes what comes after it. It connects with اللَّهَ (Allah) to mean 'indeed Allah'
Added وَ to connect this phrase with the previous statement about not betraying in secret
Changed to the proper name for The One God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَعْلَمَ - that he may know)
steps.n: Allah with a shadda (double L sound) and ending with -a because it's being acted upon by 'that he may know'
This is a negative particle that comes before يَهْدِى to say 'does not guide'. It's like adding 'not' before a verb in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

Added يَ at the beginning to show it's happening now. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) and the last root letter gets a kasra (ِ). This word is doing the action to كَيْدَ (the plot/plan) in this ayah.
Changed from action to the name of the action itself
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb يَهْدِى (guides) in this ayah
Added vowels (fatha) to make it 'he betrayed' in the past tense
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Makes the word plural to show multiple betrayers
Adds 'the' (ال) at the beginning to make it specific. This word is connected to كَيْدَ (plot), showing who the plot belongs to - 'the plot of the betrayers'.
This is the basic negative particle that makes statements negative, like 'not' in English
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it
Added fatha on م and lengthened the final alif for emphasis. This word negates the verb أُبَرِّئُ (I free/absolve) that comes after it.

steps.m: to free from blame or declare innocent

steps.n: Added shadda to make the meaning stronger

steps.m: I declare innocent (subject of the verb)
steps.n: Changed from 'he declared' to 'I declare' - the أُ at start shows it's 'I' and the middle vowels show it's happening now
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create the noun meaning 'self'. This word is what's being absolved in the phrase 'I do not absolve my self'.
Added ي with kasra (ِ) before it to show possession ('my'), and lengthened the ي with ٓ to emphasize the word
This is the basic form of the word that shows possibility or condition
Adding shadda changes this from a word meaning 'if' to one that adds emphasis to the statement that follows. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'my Lord is forgiving'.
Added vowel marks to make it a noun referring to the inner essence of a person
Added ال to make it specific. The ن gets a shadda (double mark) when ال is added.
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb أُبَرِّئُ (I absolve/free)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added shadda and alif to show someone who does this action a lot
Added ة to make it feminine, referring to النَّفْسَ (the soul) which is feminine
Added لَ at beginning to emphasize that the soul really does command evil
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject of إِنَّ
Changed the action into a noun meaning the evil thing itself
Added ال to make it specific evil being talked about

Added بِ at start to show how the evil is used, and added ِ at end because it follows another word. The long vowel ـو shows emphasis. This word describes what the soul commands to do in the phrase 'commanding to evil'.

This is a particle used for exceptions. In this ayah, it excludes what follows (what my Lord has mercy upon) from what came before (the soul commanding to evil). It connects these parts of the sentence.

This is a particle that creates an exception, connecting to the verb رَحِمَ (had mercy) that follows it. In this context, it means 'except what' or 'except that which', showing that Allah's mercy is the exception to the soul's tendency towards evil.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word comes after إِلَّا (except) in the ayah.

steps.m: to care for, raise, nurture completely

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does the nurturing in a complete way
The kasra shows this word is connected to what comes after it
Adding ي at the end shows this is MY lord
Adding fatha at end shows this word is being used as an object in the sentence
This is a complete particle that has no root. It introduces and emphasizes the statement that follows it. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the soul is truly commanding to evil.
Doubling the ب makes it stronger - shows complete mastery/lordship
Added kasra to connect it with the coming ى

steps.m: my Lord (subject of رَحِمَ - had mercy)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show possession. This word is the subject of رَحِمَ (had mercy) in this ayah.
Added و and vowels to show this describes someone who does a lot of forgiving
The ٌ shows this is describing Allah's quality of being very forgiving. This pairs with رَّحِيمٌ (very merciful) to show two of Allah's qualities.

steps.m: to be merciful, to show kindness

steps.n: Forms intensive adjective pattern فَعِيل indicating one who shows abundant mercy
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning even more strongly
Added tanween damma to show this describes Allah's quality of mercy mentioned in رَبِّى (my Lord)
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to the root letters to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he said'
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا, which is common for hollow verbs
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This word connects to ٱلْمَلِكُ (the king) who is doing the saying.
Added vowels to form the basic noun for someone who rules
Adding ٱلْ makes it specific - this refers to the king of Egypt speaking to Yusuf

steps.m: the king (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Adding ُ shows this is the subject doing the action of saying (قَالَ)
Command form made by adding hamza at start. The middle letter changes to a hamza (ئ). The last letter ي becomes a short i sound (ِ).
Added و and changed vowel to ُ to make the command plural (addressing multiple people)

steps.m: come to me! (command to many people)
steps.n: Added نِى to show the command is to come 'to me' (the king speaking to his servants)
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning with/by/in
Added the pronoun هِ (him) to show who the preposition refers to. Here it refers to Prophet Yusuf who the king wants to bring.
Added ۦٓ which is just a pronunciation mark showing elongation of the sound. It doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be pure, select, make special

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added 'ast' at start to mean 'I seek to', changed middle vowels to make it mean 'select specially for myself' - turns 'pure' into 'I want to select for my own use'

steps.m: I seek to select him specially for myself (addressing the king about يوسف)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being selected. This connects to the story where the king is speaking about wanting to select Yusuf for a special position.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'self' or 'soul'
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is being done 'for' or 'to' something
The word gets a kasra (i sound) at the end because it's connected to the next word, showing possession or belonging
Added ى at the end to show this means 'my self' (myself)
This is a temporal word that shows when something happens. The shadda (double letter) and ا at the end make it specifically mean 'when'

steps.m: and when (connects to كَلَّمَهُ - he spoke to him)
steps.n: The فَ connects this with what happened before, showing one event followed another. Here it connects 'when' to 'he spoke to him' in the story
Adding the vowels and doubling the middle letter shows this is happening with extra emphasis or importance. This matches how the king is having a significant conversation with Yusuf.
Adding هُ at the end shows who was spoken to (him). This refers to Yusuf being spoken to by the king.
Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it mean 'he said' in the past

steps.m: he said (subject of the sentence, speaking to يوسف)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic for this type of word. The king (الملك) is speaking to Yusuf after talking with him.
This is a special word used to emphasize what comes after it, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Added كَ at the end to mean 'you' (speaking to one male). In this ayah, this word connects to مَكِينٌ (established/dignified) to say 'indeed you are established/dignified'
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Added ال to make it specific - this is the special form ٱلْ used when starting a word
Added fatha at the end because it's receiving the action (object) in the sentence
This is a location word that shows something is near or with someone. It's like saying 'at' or 'with' in English.
Adding نا shows this belongs to 'us'. In this ayah, it's used to show that Yusuf is now in a position of authority with/near the king.
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a strong quality. This word describes Yusuf's new position with the king.

steps.m: of high rank (describing Yusuf's position with the king)
steps.n: The tanween (double damma) shows this is describing Yusuf's new status in general terms
Adding basic vowel marks to make the word readable
Adding ي changes this from a verb (being trustworthy) to an adjective (someone who is trustworthy)
Added fatha marks to show past tense. This is the basic form before the weak letter changes.
This is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Added fathas to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he made' to 'make!' by adding alif at start and changing the ending to make it a command to one person
Added نِ at the end to show the command is being done to 'me'
Added ا at start to help pronounce the word - this ا is called hamzat al-wasl

This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, in charge of, or has authority over something else. Here it connects 'make me' (يَجْعَلْنِى) with 'treasuries' (خَزَآئِنِ) to say 'make me in charge of the treasuries'
Added an alif symbol (ىٰ) at the end to show how to pronounce the word correctly. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'stored' to mean 'a place for storing' by adding long i sound and -ah ending
Changed to broken plural pattern showing multiple storehouses/treasuries
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by/belongs to something else in the sentence
The root letters are joined with a fatha on the first letter to make the common noun meaning earth/ground
Adding a sukun (ْ) on the ر makes this the standard noun pattern for 'earth'

steps.m: of the earth/land (possessed by/connected to خَزَآئِنِ - storehouses)
steps.n: Adding kasra shows this word is connected to خَزَآئِنِ (storehouses) in the phrase 'storehouses of the earth'

steps.m: of the earth/land (possessed by/connected to خَزَآئِنِ - storehouses)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to the specific earth/land rather than any earth/land
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'truly' at the start of a sentence

steps.m: Indeed I (speaking to خَزَآئِنِ الْأَرْضِ - treasuries of the land)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show 'I' am speaking. The نّ takes a kasra (ِ ) to connect smoothly with the ى. This is Yusuf speaking about himself regarding the treasuries.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding ي in the middle changes it from 'he guarded' to 'someone who guards a lot', like turning 'protect' into 'protector'
The added marks (tanwin) at the end show this is describing Yusuf as 'a guardian' rather than 'the guardian'
Added vowels and ي to show this describes someone with complete knowledge. This is an intensive form showing the quality is very strong.
The double-damma (ٌ) shows this is describing someone or something in general terms
Added كَ at the end to make it point to something specific
Added كَ at the beginning to show comparison or similarity

steps.m: and like that (connecting to what happened to Yusuf)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement. This word connects to what follows about how Allah empowered Yusuf.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: We firmly established (subject of نُصِيبُ - we bestow)
steps.n: Adding نَّا at the end shows that 'we' did the action. This connects to how We (Allah) bestow Our mercy later in the verse.
This is a proper name, so we start with its basic form with damma vowels
Added لِ at the beginning to show this is being done for Joseph's benefit

steps.m: for/to Joseph (object of مَكَّنَّا - we established)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Joseph is receiving the action of being established/empowered
This is a basic preposition showing location. It connects the verb يَتَبَوَّأُ (settles) to where the settling happens (in the earth).
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun
Adding definite article makes the noun definite
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after the preposition فِى (in) in the ayah

steps.m: to settle or establish in a place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding تَ at the beginning shows doing the action to yourself

steps.m: he settles himself firmly (in الْأَرْضِ - the land)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end. This word shows what Yusuf does in the land mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Added sukun to show end of first part. This word shows something coming from or originating from something else.

steps.m: from it (feminine, referring to الْأَرْضِ/the land)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to الْأَرْضِ (the land) which is feminine in Arabic.

steps.m: where/wherever (location word used with يَتَبَوَّأُ - he settles)
steps.n: This is a location word that shows where something happens. In this ayah, it shows where Yusuf can settle.

steps.m: where/wherever (location word used with يَتَبَوَّأُ - he settles)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce this word correctly: fatha (َ) on ح, sukun (ْ) on ي, and damma (ُ) at the end
The root letters combine with vowels to make 'he wanted'. The middle letter ي changes to ا, which is common in Arabic verbs.
Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end
Extended alif is written with آ but pronunciation stays the same
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes to present tense 'we' form. In this ayah, this verb shows how Allah bestows His mercy, with بِرَحْمَتِنَا (with Our mercy) showing how the reaching/striking happens.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from action 'he was merciful' to the thing itself 'mercy' by adding ة at the end and putting a sukoon (little circle) on ح
Adding preposition 'with/by' to create prepositional phrase

steps.m: with our mercy (object of نُصِيبُ - we bestow)
steps.n: The word 'mercy' gets 'our' added to it (نا at the end). The ب at the start means 'with', making it 'with our mercy'. This mercy is what we are giving/bestowing.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people in general. Here it connects to the next word نَّشَآءُ (we will) to mean 'whoever we will'
Added fatha mark on م to show correct pronunciation
Added vowels and combined the middle letter ي with ا to make a long vowel sound
Added نَ at start to show 'we' are doing it, and changed the ending to ُ to show it's happening now
Added ا in middle to show this is a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)

steps.m: not (negates نُضِيعُ - we let go to waste)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it negative. Here it negates the verb نُضِيعُ (we let go to waste), making it mean 'we do not let go to waste'

steps.m: and not (connects to previous statement about showing mercy)
steps.n: The و connects this negation to the previous statement about showing mercy to whom Allah wills, adding the additional point that Allah does not waste the reward of those who do good
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes to present tense 'we' form by adding نُ prefix and changing vowels

steps.m: we waste (in the context of أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ - the reward of the doers of good)
steps.n: Adds the final damma to show this is the main verb. In this ayah, it's specifically talking about not wasting the reward of those who do good.

steps.m: to give payment or reward for work

steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun. This word is what's being acted on by نُضِيعُ (we do not let go to waste) in this ayah.
Added a fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb نُضِيعُ (we do not let go to waste)
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing inside vowels turns 'being good' into 'person who does good deeds'

The suffix ين makes it masculine plural, indicating multiple good-doers. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after أجر (reward) in a possession relationship, meaning 'reward of the good-doers'.

steps.m: the good-doers (whose reward is not lost)
steps.n: Adding the definite article ال makes the word definite
Root letters put together to make the noun meaning 'reward'
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added emphatic lām to emphasize the statement and make it definite
Added conjunction to connect with previous statement

steps.m: to come after, to be later

steps.n: Changes the verb into a word describing someone/something that comes after others

steps.m: the life that comes after (this life)
steps.n: Adding ة makes this word specifically refer to the afterlife, like how we have special words for important concepts
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific afterlife that everyone knows about, not just any future life
Adding ِ shows this word belongs to or is connected to the word أَجْرُ (reward), making the phrase 'reward of the afterlife'
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create comparative/superlative form
Added double damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action. This word is describing وَلَأَجْرُ (and surely the reward) in this ayah.
This is a demonstrative word that points to specific people or things. It doesn't come from a root.
Added لِ at the beginning to mean 'for'. When لِ comes before ٱل, the ٱ is dropped and the ل gets a shadda (ّ) to show it's doubled.
Added extra emphasis by doubling the first ل with a shadda
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed the first letter to hamza with madd (ءَا) to show causing someone to feel secure/trust, which came to mean 'believe' or 'have faith'
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people (they) did the action

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action

steps.m: and they were (connected to يَتَّقُونَ - they used to fear Allah)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to the next word يَتَّقُونَ, showing they were in a continuous state of fearing Allah
Connected the root letters with vowels to make past tense 'he' form
Added ا and ت at the start to mean 'protecting oneself'. The و changed to ت because two similar letters came together
Changed from past to present by adding ي at start. The ت merged with ت to make تّ (with shadda). Shows ongoing protection/fear of Allah

steps.m: they protect themselves (referring to those who believed in the previous ayah)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا (those who believed) mentioned earlier
Added fatha vowels and changed middle letter ي to ا to show past tense
Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'
Forms the basic singular noun 'brother' from the root
Changes from single brother (أَخ) to the plural form brothers (إِخْوَ) by adding extra letters
Adds ة to complete the plural noun form
The damma shows this word is the subject doing the action in فَدَخَلُوا (they entered)
This is a proper name of Hebrew origin (Yūsuf/יוֹסֵף), meaning "He (God) will add/increase". Since it's not from Arabic, it doesn't have an Arabic root.

steps.m: Joseph (object of فَدَخَلُوا - they entered upon)
steps.n: The fatha (َ) at the end shows that Joseph is being acted upon - he's the one they entered upon
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense
Added وا to show multiple people did the action

steps.m: so they entered (after إِخْوَةُ يُوسُفَ came)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened as a result of or after the brothers of Yusuf came
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for adding the pronoun. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِ at the end to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to Joseph (يُوسُفَ) who was mentioned earlier.

steps.m: he recognized (referring to Yusuf recognizing his brothers)
steps.n: Added fatha vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past. In this ayah, this refers to Prophet Yusuf recognizing his brothers when they came to him.

The هُمْ suffix means 'them' - here referring to Yusuf's brothers who entered upon him but didn't recognize him (as mentioned in the next part of the ayah وَهُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ - while they did not recognize him)

steps.m: and so he recognized them (while they didn't recognize him)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happened as a result of them entering upon him (فَدَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ). It creates a sequence: they came → they entered upon him → so he recognized them (while they didn't recognize him)
This is a pronoun that means 'they' (for a group of males). In this ayah, it refers to Yusuf's brothers who entered his presence.
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'
This is a preposition that shows who something is for
The هُ at the end means 'him'. This word connects to فَعَرَفَهُمْ (then he recognized them) to show who did the recognizing.

steps.m: to not recognize or deny something

steps.n: Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show who does the action. This word refers to Yusuf's brothers not recognizing him.

steps.m: they who do not recognize (referring to Yusuf's brothers)
steps.n: Added ُونَ at the end to show we're talking about multiple people (Yusuf's brothers)
This is a compound particle made of لَ + مَا with a shadda added to show emphasis. It indicates something happening at a specific time that leads to something else.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows that the preparation was done thoroughly or with extra care
Added هُم at the end to show who was being prepared (them)

steps.m: to prepare or equip with supplies

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to prepare' into noun meaning 'equipment/supplies'
Added هِمْ at the end to show these are their supplies

steps.m: with their provisions/supplies (related to جَهَّزَهُم - he equipped them)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show these are the supplies being used to equip them, connecting back to the verb جَهَّزَهُم (he equipped them) earlier in the ayah
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is an intermediate form before the weak letter change.
The و (weak letter) changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
The initial أ changes to ئ due to pronunciation rules with the command form
Added و to make the command plural (addressing multiple people)
Added نِى at the end to show the action is directed towards 'me'
The root letters أ-خ-و become أَخ (brother) by dropping the و at the end
The tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end makes it indefinite, like saying 'a brother' instead of 'the brother'

steps.m: with a brother (connected to ٱئْتُونِى - bring me)
steps.n: The preposition بِ (with) is added at the beginning. This word connects to ٱئْتُونِى (bring me) in the ayah, showing what should be brought.

steps.m: for you all (addressing the brothers of يوسف)
steps.n: Combined ل (for/to) with كم (you all) and added basic vowel marks. This word is addressing Yusuf's brothers in the context.

steps.m: for you all (addressing the brothers of يوسف)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to show emphasis and connection with previous word
This is a basic preposition in Arabic that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to.
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a word ending in ل or ن, which is a common pronunciation rule in Arabic to make it easier to say. It doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: father, care for as a father

steps.n: Connected the root letters with basic vowels to form the noun 'father'
Added ي at the end to mean 'my father'
Added كُمْ at the end to show the father belongs to multiple people being spoken to
Added fatha to show clear pronunciation

steps.m: look! behold! (introducing what Yusuf says next)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to change this from a negative word to an attention-getter. This tells the listeners to pay attention to what comes next in Yusuf's speech.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed to present tense form. The ى at the end is due to the weak letter rule.
Added وْنَ to show that many people are doing the action. The و appears due to weak letter rules.
Added shadda to make the meaning stronger. This shows we're really emphasizing what comes next.
Added ي at the end to show I'm talking about myself. Also changed the vowel before it to match.
Changed the writing style of ي to ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top. This doesn't change the meaning, just how it looks.

steps.m: to fulfill or complete something fully

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to show doing something completely or making it happen
Changed the vowels to show something being done to it rather than doing it
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the act of measuring
Added the definite article 'the' to specify this particular measurement

steps.m: the measurement (object of أُوفِى - I give in full)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb أُوفِى (I give in full)
This is the basic personal pronoun meaning 'I' or 'me'. It's one of the fundamental pronouns in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
The small circle (۠) is added above the final alif to show this is being said with emphasis, like saying 'I myself' or stressing 'I' when speaking.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English.
Root letters get vowels to make the noun meaning 'good/better/best'
Adds damma to show this word is in the subject position
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to make it mean 'one who sends down' instead of just 'sent down' - this مُ pattern shows who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular hosts/providers
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a condition - in this case, it introduces the condition of not bringing someone

steps.m: then if (connects this condition to what was said before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement. It's like saying 'so' or 'then' in English before starting an 'if' statement.
This is a negative particle that negates present tense verbs and changes their meaning to past negative. It is not derived from a root.
The shadda (ّ) is added to emphasize the word because it comes after فَإِن (and if). This makes the meaning stronger, like saying 'definitely not' instead of just 'not'.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense form
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels for present tense
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added ي with kasra to show the action is happening to 'me'
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'
Added هِ to show what is being referred to (it/him)

steps.m: with/by it/him (referring to proof they need to bring)
steps.n: Added small alif (ۦ) after ه as a pronunciation guide
This is a negative particle that makes something not happen

steps.m: then not (connects to تَأْتُونِى - you bring me)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this negative statement to what came before. It shows that because they didn't bring someone, then as a result something won't happen.

steps.m: to measure out, to weigh out portions

steps.n: Changes from a verb (to measure) into a noun meaning the act itself - like changing 'to weigh' into 'weighing'

steps.m: measuring, weighing (as the object of فَلَا 'then no')
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the negative phrase فَلَا (then no)
Starting with the basic particle ل which shows direction or purpose
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for you all (shows something belongs to or is for your group)
steps.n: Added كُمْ which shows this applies to multiple people being addressed. In this ayah, it's referring to the measure/portion (كَيْلَ) that belongs to the group being spoken to.
Added vowels to make the word mean a location or presence
Added kasra at the end to prepare for connecting to another word
Added ى at the end to show this is happening with or near me. This word is telling us where the measure/portion (كَيْلَ) mentioned in the verse is located.
This is a negative particle that makes something not allowed or negates what comes after it

steps.m: and not (connects to فَلَا كَيْلَ لَكُمْ عِندِى - 'then there will be no measure for you from me')
