steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negative statement with the previous condition. This connects to the earlier statement about not getting any measure (grain) if they don't bring their brother.
This pattern shows something becoming a quality (like becoming near)
Added ت at start to show 'you' as subject, changed middle vowel to sukun for present tense pattern
Added و to show multiple people are doing the action
Added نِ to show the action affects 'me'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs

steps.m: they said (subject of سَنُرٰوِدُ - we will try to persuade)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did the action. This word is connected to سَنُرٰوِدُ (we will try to persuade) which comes after it, showing what they said.

steps.m: to try to get or reach something
to keep trying to convince or persuade
steps.n: Adding alif makes it mean doing something back and forth or repeatedly, like when you keep trying to convince someone
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing it, and changed the end to ُ to show it's happening now

steps.m: we will keep trying to convince (about أَبَاهُ - his father)
steps.n: Added سَ at start to show this will happen in the future. In this ayah, they're talking about convincing Ya'qub (their father) about something.
This is a preposition that shows something moving away from something else or talking about something

steps.m: away from him, about him (referring to their father mentioned in أَبَاهُ)
steps.n: Adding هُ at the end shows this relates to 'him'. In this ayah, it refers to their father who is mentioned right after this word (أَبَاهُ).
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic noun
Changed the ending to show this word is being acted upon by the verb سَنُرٰوِدُ (we will try to persuade)
Added هُ at the end to show this is his father. This whole word is being acted upon by the verb سَنُرٰوِدُ (we will try to persuade)
This little word connects what comes before with what comes after, like saying 'and' in English
Adding إنّ makes what comes after it stronger and more certain, like saying 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: and indeed we (will do what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end shows we are the ones doing the action. This connects to the previous statement about convincing Yusuf's father.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show who does the action
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added fathas to make it past tense. The middle letter و changed to ا which is common in Arabic. This shows one male person did this action in the past.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Added vowels to form basic noun meaning 'young man' or 'servant'
Changed from singular to plural by changing inside vowels and adding ان at the end - this is a special plural form for young people/servants
Added لِ prefix meaning 'to/for'
Added possessive pronoun هِ meaning 'his'
This root carries the basic meaning of placing something or making something into something else
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from 'he made' to 'you make!' by adding alif at start and changing ending - now it's a command to one person
Changed alif to hamza-wasl (ٱ) and added plural command ending وا with a small alif above it to show it's addressed to multiple people

steps.m: to cut or divide into parts

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the first vowel to kasra and added alif after second letter to make a noun for the result of cutting/dividing (in this case, goods divided for trade)
Added ة at the end to show this is one specific instance of merchandise
Added هُمْ at the end to show the merchandise belongs to them. This word is the object of the command 'put' (اجْعَلُوا)
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it shows where something should be put - in their bags.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to travel or move from one place to another

steps.n: Changes from a verb about traveling into a noun for a bag used during travel
Changes the single saddlebag into multiple saddlebags using a common plural pattern
Adds kasra at the end because it comes after the word في (in/into)
Adds هِمْ at the end to show these belong to them
This word shows hope or expectation about something
Adding هُم shows who the hope relates to - in this case 'they/them'

steps.m: perhaps they (expressing hope they will recognize it)
steps.n: Adding sukun (ْ) at the end helps connect this word smoothly to the next word يَرْجِعُونَ (they return)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: they recognize it (referring to their merchandise)
steps.n: Added هَآ to show what is being recognized (their merchandise mentioned earlier in the verse)
This word connects two events in time - it shows that something will happen when they return to their family. In this ayah, it connects with the verb يَرْجِعُونَ (they return).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added اِنْ at the beginning to show the action happens by itself - they returned on their own, not someone returned them
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Word already has all necessary parts including the ا and ن prefixes
This is a basic connecting word (like 'to' in English) that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to أَهْلِهِمْ (their family) to show where they are returning to.
Added an alif symbol (ٰ) above the ى which helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning
Added a small alif (ٓ) above which helps with pronunciation when continuing to the next word but doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to something after it (showing possession)
Added هِمْ at the end to show the family belongs to them
The shadda (ّ) on ل shows emphasis, making the meaning stronger

steps.m: perhaps they (expressing hope they will recognize it)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'they/them' to show who the hope is about. This connects to يَعْرِفُونَهَآ (they recognize it) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at the start and changing the middle vowel
The ونَ ending shows this is done by a group. This verb comes after لَعَلَّهُمْ (perhaps they) in the ayah.
This is a temporal particle that means 'when' and usually refers to something that happened in the past. It often introduces a condition that leads to another action.

steps.m: Then when (connecting to رَجَعُوٓا۟ - they returned)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else. Here it connects to them returning to their father.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added وا to show multiple people did the action (to their father)
Added pronunciation marks (ٓ and ۟) that don't change the meaning
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to أَبِيهِمْ (their father) to show they returned to him.
Added an alif symbol (ٰ) to show the long vowel sound at the end. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word. This is just a pronunciation mark and doesn't change the meaning.
Root letters أ-ب-و combine to make the word 'father'. The و drops off when used alone.
Adding ي at the end makes it mean 'my father' instead of just 'father'
Added هِمْ at the end to show it belongs to them (the brothers)
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a special type of word.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action, and changed the last vowel to ُ to connect with وا. The small mark ۟ above وا is just for proper reading.

steps.m: to nurture/care for (as a father does)

steps.n: The root letters أ-ب-و become the basic word أَب meaning 'father'
Adding ا at the end makes the word mean 'two' of something
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to show we're calling out to someone

steps.m: O our father (speaking to their father in the story of Yusuf)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show it's 'our father' - the brothers are speaking to their father Ya'qub
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: it was prevented (the weighing/measuring was prevented from us)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show that this happened to something rather than someone doing it. This word is connected to الْكَيْلُ (the weighing/measuring) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition showing something coming from somewhere or someone
Added نا which means 'us' or 'our'. The ن from مِن combines with the ن from نا to make a doubled ن sound (shown by ّ)

steps.m: to measure out (especially grain or other goods)

steps.n: Transforms verb root into noun meaning 'measuring out'. This word is the subject of مُنِعَ (was prevented) in the phrase 'measuring was prevented from us' (مُنِعَ مِنَّا ٱلْكَيْلُ)

steps.m: the measuring out (of goods) (subject of مُنِعَ - was prevented)
steps.n: The word got 'the' (ال) in front and a mark on top (ُ) to show it's doing the action
Adding أَ at start makes it a command to someone
Adding sukun at end completes the command form

steps.m: so send! (command in response to previous statement)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this is a response or result of what was said before
This is a preposition meaning 'with' - it doesn't come from a root since it's a particle
Added نَا at the end to show this means 'us'. This نَا is attached to show who the word 'with' refers to.
The alif is elongated to help with pronunciation at the end of the word. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the first two root letters with fatha to form the basic noun. The final weak letter و is dropped in this form.
The word 'brother' gets an 'a' sound at the end because it's what is being sent. Like saying 'send brother' in Arabic.
The suffix نَا shows possession ('our') and this word is being acted upon by the verb أَرْسِلْ (send) in the command 'send our brother with us'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
The weak letter ي changes to ت when building certain verb forms

steps.m: we will measure out (requesting permission from أَبِيهِمْ)
steps.n: Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and made the ending quiet (with sukun) because it comes after 'so send'
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to لَحٰفِظُونَ (guardians) later in the ayah, showing what we are indeed doing.

steps.m: for him (referring to their brother who they will protect)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) to لَ (for/to) to show who the action is for. The ۥ mark is just to show the pronunciation of هُ.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed pattern to show who does the action
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added fatha at end to show this is the subject doing something
Root letters ق-و-ل got vowels (َ) to make 'he said' in the past tense
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
This is a question word that turns a statement into a yes/no question, like how we add 'do' at the start of English questions. In this ayah, it's asking 'would I trust you...'
Added a sukun (ْ) on the last letter to show where to stop when reading

steps.m: to trust or feel safe with someone/something
to cause someone to feel safe/trust
steps.n: Adding alif at start changes meaning from 'feel safe' to 'make safe/trust'

steps.m: I should trust you all (in response to هَلْ/should I?)
steps.n: Changed to 'I' form and added كُمْ (you all) to show who is being trusted. The ُ shows it's answering 'should I?' (هَلْ)
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or about something else. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ى changes to يْ to connect with the next suffix. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect meaning.

steps.m: upon him (referring back to the brother mentioned in أَخِيهِ)
steps.n: Added هِ ('hi') at the end to mean 'upon him', like adding 'him' to 'on' to make 'on him'
This is a basic word used to show that something is being excluded from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it's used to compare how they trusted with their brother before.
These are two different words - إِلَّا means 'except' and is used to exclude something. It's not made from إِل.
Basic comparison word that shows something is similar to something else
Adding ما makes the comparison more general and complete
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) but pronounced the same way

steps.m: to trust or feel safe with someone/something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. The middle kasra (ِ) shows this is about feeling or experiencing trust.
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action in the past

steps.m: I trusted you all (referring to the brothers of يوسف)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who was trusted - 'you all'. In this ayah, يعقوب is asking his sons if he should trust them with يوسف's brother like he trusted them before with يوسف.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of, upon, or about something else. Here it connects to 'his brother' to show trust placed upon him.
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the ى. This is a writing style that shows the word ends with a long 'aa' sound.
Added an elongation mark (ٓ) which shows this word is stretched when read. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added short 'a' sound (fatha) to first two letters to make the word mean a specific brother rather than the general concept of brotherhood
Changed the word to show it belongs to someone by adding kasra and ي
Added هِ to show it belongs to him. This word follows عَلَىٰٓ (upon) in the ayah.
Basic particle that shows where something comes from or starts
Added kasra under م to show how to pronounce it correctly. In this ayah, it comes before the word قَبْلُ (before) to mean 'from before'
Added vowel marks to create the noun meaning 'before'. The fatha and sukun pattern is common for making time words.
Added damma at the end to show this word is in the nominative case
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of the sentence. Allah is being described as خَيْرٌ (better) in this ayah.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before. It's like saying 'therefore' or 'so' in English.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the base form
Added double dammah to make the word indefinite and show it's the subject
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he protected'
Changed to active participle form to show who does the action. This describes how Allah is better (خَيْرٌ) - as a protector.

steps.m: as a protector (describing how Allah is better)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how Allah is better. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) in this word.
This is the basic pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who is described as being 'better as a protector'
The وَ connects this phrase to what came before, joining the statement 'Allah is better as a protector' to the previous discussion
Root letters got vowels to make past tense 'he showed mercy'
Changed to superlative form to show highest degree of mercy. This word describes Allah in this ayah.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changes from 'he showed mercy' to 'one who shows mercy' by adding alif after first letter and kasra under second letter. Like changing 'wrote' to 'writer'.
Sound masculine plural form using ين suffix
The word gained 'ال' (the) at the start, making it mean 'THE most merciful' instead of just 'merciful ones'. When used for Allah, it shows He has the highest level of mercy.
Added ا at the end to show this word introduces something that happened in the past (in this ayah, it introduces 'when they opened their baggage')
Added و at the beginning to connect this part with what came before it in the story
Connected the root letters together with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people (they) did the action
Added the small circle mark (۟) above وا to show proper pronunciation
This forms a noun meaning 'goods' or 'provisions'
Added vowels and alif to make a noun meaning something that provides benefit
Changed alif to alif with bar on top (optional spelling variation)
Added fatha to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: their goods/provisions (object of وَجَدُوا - they found)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show these goods belong to them. This word is what they found (object of وَجَدُوا)
The ُ (dhamma) at the end shows this is in the nominative case
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action

steps.m: to cut or divide into portions

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'a portion for trade' by adding kasra at start, alif after second letter, and ta at end
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is the object of a verb
Added هُمْ at the end to show the merchandise belongs to them
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled د is merged with shadda for easier pronunciation
Changed to passive voice by changing first vowel to damma
Added ت to show this happened to something feminine in the past
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns
Added هِمْ at the end to show who is being referred to (them)
Adding short vowels 'a' after each letter to make 'he said' in past tense
Changed middle letter و to ا and added وا at the end to make 'he said' into 'they said'
Connected the root letters with vowels to form the basic noun 'father'
Added vocative particle يا with extended alif (ٰ) to call out to someone
Adds possessive pronoun suffix نا meaning 'our'
This is a question word that asks about something. In this ayah, they are asking 'what more could we want?' when they see their merchandise returned to them.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: we seek (something we want from يٰٓأَبَانَا - O our father)
steps.n: Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, changed vowels to show present tense, and added ي at end because it's a weak verb. This is part of the children speaking to their father Ya'qub (as), explaining what they want.
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
Added ها for emphasis and clarity in pointing to something
Changed to feminine form by adding ِهِ at the end
Added small ي (ۦ) as a pronunciation guide

steps.m: to cut or divide into portions

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from 'divided' (verb) to 'merchandise' (noun) - something that gets divided up for selling

steps.m: our merchandise (that was returned to us)
steps.n: Added 'نا' (our) at the end to show it belongs to us. The alif changed to make it easier to say. This is talking about the merchandise they found returned to them.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show passive voice (something being done to something else) and combined the double د with shadda

steps.m: it was returned (referring to بِضٰعَتَهُمْ - their merchandise)
steps.n: Added ت to show the subject is feminine (referring to their merchandise - بِضٰعَتَهُمْ in this ayah). Changed the middle vowel to fatha because of this addition.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. It's used to show where something is going.

steps.m: to us (object of رُدَّتْ - was returned)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show 'us'. This word connects to رُدَّتْ (was returned) in the ayah, showing who the merchandise was returned to.
Added kasra after م and connected the letters to make a basic noun form
The ن at the start shows 'we' are doing the action

steps.m: and we provide food (to our family)
steps.n: The و connects this to the previous actions mentioned. From context, they are talking about providing food to their family (أَهْلَنَا)

steps.m: to settle, inhabit, belong to family

steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb نَمِيرُ (we provide for)

The suffix نَا is a first person plural possessive pronoun meaning 'our'. The fatha before نَا is added as a connecting vowel. This word is the direct object (accusative case) of the verb نَمِيرُ which means 'we will get provisions for'.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to present tense 'we' form. This word connects to أَخَانَا (our brother) which is what they are protecting
و at the start means 'and'
The root letters أ-خ-و join together to make the simple word 'brother'. The و at the end disappears when used alone.
Added ا at the end to show this is the object being acted upon. This word is what is being protected in نَحْفَظُ أَخَانَا (we protect our brother).
Added نَا at the end to show this brother belongs to us. The whole phrase نَحْفَظُ أَخَانَا means 'we protect our brother'.
Added ت between first and second root letters and changed ي to ا to form Pattern VIII, showing doing action for oneself
Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' and ُ at end to show present tense
Added و at beginning to connect to previous phrase about providing for family

steps.m: to measure out (especially grain or other goods)

steps.n: Changed the action of measuring into a noun meaning one unit or instance of measurement
The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of نَزْدَادُ (we will increase) - they will get more of this measure
The ي makes it mean 'one who does something', here meaning 'one who carries loads'
Adding the two dots under makes it mean 'a' camel instead of 'the' camel
The notes are incorrect since tanwin was already present in both before/after words - no change occurred
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is replaced with a small alif above to show elongation
Adding لِ changes the meaning from 'this' (near) to 'that' (far)
Adding كَ makes it more specific and emphatic
Changed from action to the thing itself. This word is what's being increased in نَزْدَادُ (we increase).
Adding tanween makes it 'a measure' instead of 'the measure'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective describing the measure/amount mentioned in the ayah as being easy
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form
The middle weak letter changes to match common pronunciation

steps.m: will not (negates أُرْسِلَهُ - I will send him)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that makes the following verb impossible in the future. Here it negates the verb أُرْسِلَهُ (I will send him), making it mean 'I will not send him'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from active to passive by adding أُ (u) at start and changing middle vowel to i - now means 'was sent' instead of 'he sent'
Added هُ suffix meaning 'him'. The word comes after لَنْ which negates future action.
This is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'along with'. In this ayah, it's describing who the person will be sent with.
The كُمْ suffix shows this refers to multiple people being addressed - in this case, Yusuf's brothers who are asking their father to send their younger brother with them
This is a basic particle that shows when something stops or reaches its limit. It's not made from a root - it's a complete word by itself.
Adding the shadda makes the meaning stronger and more definite
Adding ى at the end helps connect this word to the verb that comes after it (تُؤْتُونِ meaning 'you give'). It's like saying 'until you give...'
The small alif (ٰ) above the ى is just a writing mark that shows this is a special kind of ى. It doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he brought' to 'you bring' by adding تُ at start
Added ِ to show the action is being done to 'me'
The نِ was already there in the first word - it wasn't added. This is actually removing the ي (yaa) at the end while keeping the نِ that means 'me'
Root letters got vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he was firm'
Adds مَ at start to make it a noun meaning 'something that makes firm/secure', like a promise or pledge

steps.m: a solemn pledge (that they gave to يَعْقُوب)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is the object being given in the story
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a word ending in ن or م, making the م sound stronger
The fatha (َ) at the end connects this word to the next word اللَّهِ (Allah), showing that they go together to mean 'from Allah'
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل, making this the unique name for The One True God
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word. This word shows who the oath (مَوْثِقًا) is sworn by.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense with 'you' as the doer
Added لَ to show emphasis and certainty
Added نَّ for extra emphasis

steps.m: you will most certainly bring to me
steps.n: Added نِى to show the action is directed towards the speaker
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is the preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'him' or 'it'
Added ۦٓ which is just a pronunciation mark that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: except (connects to أَن يُحَاطَ - that you are surrounded)
steps.n: This is the basic form of the exception particle that means 'except' or 'unless'. In this ayah, it connects to the phrase 'that you are surrounded', showing the exception to their promise.

steps.m: except (connects to أَن يُحَاطَ - that you are surrounded)
steps.n: The final alif is lengthened (آ instead of ا) for emphasis in Quranic writing style. This is a common stylistic feature that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: that (introduces what happens to يُحَاطَ - you all are surrounded)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects two ideas. Here it introduces what might happen to the brothers (being surrounded). It's like saying 'unless that you are surrounded' in English.
Added an alif between ح and ط, and vowels, to make it mean 'he surrounded' in the past
Added يُ at the beginning to make it passive - showing someone is being surrounded instead of surrounding others
Added kasra to the letter ب to make it readable

steps.m: with you all (being surrounded by danger)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to. In this ayah, it refers to the brothers being surrounded by danger.
Basic form of the word meaning 'when' with regular alif added
The alif is elongated (آ) to add emphasis to the word

steps.m: and when (connects to ءَاتَوْهُ - they gave him)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this event with what comes next. In this ayah, it connects 'when' to 'they gave him'
Added basic past tense form with fatha marks. The weak letter ي changes to ى at the end.
The و at the end shows multiple people did the action. The ى changes to وْ to show plural.

steps.m: they gave it to him (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: The هُ at the end shows who received what was given. This word is connected to قَالَ (he said) where 'they' are the ones doing the giving.
Added مَ at the start to turn the root into a noun meaning 'something that binds/secures', like a promise or pledge
Added هُمْ to show this belongs to multiple people (them)
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs
Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel marks

steps.m: Allah (the one true God) (subject of وَكِيلٌ)
steps.n: Added ال to specify the one true God, doubled the ل with shadda, and added ُ to show this is the subject of وَكِيلٌ (guardian/trustee) in this ayah
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or in charge of something else
The alif at the end is written with a special mark (ىٰ) to show it's being paused on. In this ayah, it shows what we're talking about is 'upon/over what we say'
In this context, مَا is being used as a relative pronoun meaning 'that which' to refer to what is being said. It is the object of the verb نَقُولُ (we say).
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed vowels to show this is happening now or in the future. The middle و combines with the vowels in a special way.
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are the ones doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: a trustworthy guardian (who is relied upon in this matter)
steps.n: Changed the middle vowel to kasra and added ي to show someone who does this action professionally, then added tanween damma to show it's indefinite. In this ayah, this word appears at the end of a statement about Allah being a witness over an agreement.
Added fathas (َ) to the root letters to make it past tense 'he said'. The pattern fa'ala (فَعَلَ) shows basic past tense verbs.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this with what came before it, like saying 'and'
Adding vowel marks to create basic past tense verb form
The notes are incorrect. بَنِي means 'sons/children' (not 'he built'). يٰ is added at the start to say 'O' when calling out to sons/children
Added shadda and fatha at the end to mean 'my' (like saying 'my son' instead of just 'son')
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it mean 'do not' instead of just the regular verb. Here it makes تَدْخُلُوا۟ (enter) mean 'do not enter'.
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Basic root letters got vowels to make past tense 'he entered'
Changed to present tense with ت prefix for 'you', sukun on middle letter, and damma on last letter
Added وا at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
This is a basic preposition showing the starting point. Here it specifically refers to entering from one entrance, connecting to بَابٍ (entrance/door) that comes after it.
Added a small ن (called nun ghunna) after the main ن to show a special pronunciation when connecting to the next word بَابٍ (door/entrance)
Changed the middle و to ا to make a noun for the thing itself
Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is after the word مِنۢ (from) in the ayah
Root 'to be one' becomes a word meaning 'one' by adding vowels
Added the tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this word is describing the door. Also changed the alif to an alif with overbar (ٰ) which is a common writing style in the Quran.
Added vowel marks to make past tense 'he entered' from the root meaning 'to enter'
Changed from 'he entered' to 'enter!' by adding ٱ at start and changing vowels - this makes it a command to one person
Added وا at the end to make it a command for many people (like saying 'all of you enter!')

steps.m: and enter! (command to group) (connected to لَا تَدْخُلُوا۟ - do not enter)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this command with the previous prohibition. This creates contrast: 'do not enter through one gate AND enter through different gates'
Basic preposition showing where to start entering from. Here it connects the verb 'enter' (تَدْخُلُوا۟) with 'a gate' (بَابٍ) to show they should not enter from one gate.
Changed from single door to plural doors by adding أ at start and وا in middle - this is a common plural pattern

steps.m: any doors/gates (in this ayah: through different doors)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this refers to any doors, not specific ones. The long alif (ٰ) is just a writing style variation.
Adding shadda intensifies the meaning of separation

steps.m: to become split up into parts
steps.n: Adding تَ shows the splitting happens by itself, like when a group spreads out on their own
Changes from action (splitting) to description (being split)
Added ة to match with 'doors' since doors is feminine in Arabic
Added ٍ (kasratan) at the end and doubled the م (meem) to match the word it describes (doors/أَبْوٰبٍ)
This is the base particle 'maa' which can mean 'not' or 'what' depending on context. The آ is just a writing variant of ا.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English.
Root letters غ-ن-ي joined together to make the base word غني meaning 'rich/sufficient'
Adding أُ at the start makes it mean 'I' do the action
Basic preposition meaning movement away from something or someone
Added the plural masculine pronoun كُم to show who the preposition applies to
This is the basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
The n sound blends into the next letter, shown by the shadda mark
The fatha at the end shows this connects to the next word (اللَّهِ) in the sentence
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word
Basic preposition showing separation or starting point. In this ayah, it shows they should enter through separate gates, not just one.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'thing'

steps.m: of/from a thing (acted on by أُغْنِى)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is affected by the word مِن (from/of) before it. Also changed ي to ى which is just a writing style change.
Basic conditional particle that introduces conditions or adds emphasis
Added kasra at the end because it's followed by the definite noun ٱلْحُكْمُ (the judgment). This connects the particle to what follows it.
Changed the root into a noun showing the power or right to make decisions. This word is connected to إِنِ (indeed) in this ayah.
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
The ُ ending shows this word is the subject of the sentence, indicating that authority/judgment belongs to Allah alone
Starting with the two separate particles that combine to form this word: إِنْ (if/indeed) and لَا (not)
The ن of إِنْ combines with the ل of لَا, creating a doubled ل (shown by ّ). This changes the meaning from 'if not' to 'except'.
This is the basic word for deity or object of worship
Adding shadda on the ل makes this the proper name for the One True God in Islam
Adding لِ at the beginning shows this belongs to Allah or is for Allah. The ِ at the end shows this word is being affected by something before it in the sentence.
Preposition عَلى with attached pronoun هِ
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Adding the shadda (doubled middle letter) makes the action more intense and means 'to entrust something to someone else' rather than just 'to rely on'
Adding تَ shows doing the action for oneself
Changed from a statement (he relied) to a command (you rely!)
Changed from command form to 'I did it' in past tense by adding -tu ending
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation of basic preposition
Changed ى to يْ to prepare for adding the pronoun suffix
Added هِ at the end to show the meaning refers to 'him'

steps.m: and upon him (connected to تَوَكَّلْتُ - I rely)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what comes before it. In this ayah, it's connecting to تَوَكَّلْتُ (I rely), showing what is being relied upon.
Added vowels and doubled middle letter to make it mean 'made someone do something' instead of just 'doing it'
Added ت at beginning to show doing the action to yourself - changing from 'making someone responsible' to 'making yourself rely on someone'
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end
Adds 'then/so' to connect this action with what came before
Changes it to a command/suggestion - 'let him' or 'he should'
Changed final vowel to kasra for proper pronunciation when connecting to next word

steps.m: to trust, rely upon, or put one's confidence in

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter ك shows doing the action thoroughly or completely
Adding ت shows doing the action to oneself - here it means taking on trust personally
Adding مُ at start and ِ in middle creates a noun meaning 'someone who does this action'
Adding ون makes it refer to multiple people doing the action

steps.m: the ones who place complete trust
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to specific people rather than just any people who trust

steps.m: the ones who place complete trust
steps.n: Adding the fatha at the end completes the plural form
This is a time particle that shows something happened right after something else, like saying 'as soon as' or 'when' in English

steps.m: and when (connects to what happens next in دَخَلُوا۟)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This shows that one action follows another, like saying 'and then when' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show a completed action. This word comes after وَلَمَّا (and when) which introduces what happened next.
The suffix وا changes the subject from 'he' to 'they (masculine plural)'. In this ayah, it refers to Prophet Jacob's (يَعْقُوبَ) sons entering as their father commanded them.

steps.m: from (showing where they entered from in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from, comes from, or is made from. In this ayah, it shows which direction or entrance points they used to enter.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to form the basic word. The fatha on ح and sukun on ي show this is about location.
Added damma at the end to complete the word. This makes it a fixed word that doesn't change its ending.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form. This word is related to يُغْنِى (it avails) in this ayah.
Added هُمْ at the end to show who received the command (them)
Added fatha on first letter and damma on second letter to create the noun 'father'
Added هُم to show possession. This word is the subject of يُغْنِى (it suffices) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the word ما which can mean 'what' or 'that which' depending on how it's used. In this ayah, it means 'what' because it's connecting to عَلَّمْنٰهُ (we taught him).
Added a shadda (double mark) on the م to make it stronger when pronounced. This adds emphasis but doesn't change the basic meaning.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in past tense. This word is negated by ما in the ayah, showing that something was not sufficient.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense. The ي at the end changes to ى because it's a weak letter. This verb is the subject of كَانَ (was) in this ayah.
This is a preposition that shows movement away from something or indicates the topic being discussed. In this ayah, it's used with يُغْنِى (avail/benefit) to show what won't help them.

steps.m: away from them (object of يُغْنِى - will not avail)
steps.n: Added هُم to show this applies to multiple people (Jacob's sons). This word connects to يُغْنِى showing what won't help them.
Basic preposition showing source or origin
The n sound at the end of previous word combines with this word's m sound, making it stronger
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word (اللَّهِ)
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل, making it a unique name for The One God
Added ِ at the end to show this belongs to something else (in this case, showing it's 'of Allah' in 'in the name of Allah'). Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it's used with حَيْثُ (where/wherever) to mean "from where"
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make it into a noun. The hamza at the end is written above the line.
Added tanwin kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِن (from/of). The ي is written with alif maqsura (ى) when followed by hamza.
This is a basic particle used for exception. It's not derived from a root but is a complete word on its own.
Added shadda and alif to complete the exception particle. In this ayah, it introduces an exception to what was mentioned before - specifically a need that Jacob had.
Root meaning 'to need' becomes a simple noun meaning 'one specific need'

steps.m: a need (object of قَضَىٰهَا - he fulfilled it)
steps.n: Shows this word is being acted upon by the verb قَضَىٰهَا (he fulfilled it) in this ayah

steps.m: in/within (connects to نَفْسِ 'self/soul' in this ayah)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing something is inside or within something else. Here it connects to the word نَفْسِ to show what was inside Jacob's self/soul.
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to make it into a noun meaning 'self' or 'soul'
Added kasra at the end to show this is Yaqub's self/soul. This connects it to the word يَعْقُوبَ (Yaqub) that comes right after it in the ayah.
This is a proper name that was used in ancient Hebrew before Arabic. It comes from a root meaning 'to follow behind' because Jacob was born holding his twin brother's heel.
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon. In this ayah, it refers to 'the self of Jacob' (نَفْسِ يَعْقُوبَ).

steps.m: to decide, fulfill, or carry out completely

steps.n: Added fathas to show past tense and changed ي to ى because it's at the end. This verb relates to يُغْنِى (suffice) in the ayah, showing what Jacob did.
Added alif khanjariya (small alif) above the ى to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: he fulfilled/carried out it (referring to حَاجَةً - the need)
steps.n: Added هَا which refers back to حَاجَةً (the need/wish) mentioned earlier in the ayah. This shows what was fulfilled.
Basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: and indeed he (referring to يَعْقُوبَ - Jacob)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who the statement is about. The ۥ mark shows this is pronounced with a long vowel.
Added damma to create the word meaning 'possessor of' or 'owner of'
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis, showing that Jacob definitely/surely has this quality. This word is describing يَعْقُوبَ (Jacob) in this ayah.
Root meaning 'know' becomes the noun 'knowledge' by adding the vowel 'i' and making the middle letter silent
Added a special mark (tanween) to show this is 'a knowledge' or 'some knowledge' rather than 'the knowledge'
Starting with the basic particle ل that shows purpose or reason
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning. This happens because it comes after إنَّ in the ayah

steps.m: because of what (that we taught him)
steps.n: Added ما which makes the word refer to 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it refers to the knowledge that was taught
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter changes the meaning from simply knowing to making others know (teaching)
Added sukun to prepare for adding pronouns
Added نا to show 'we' did the action
Added هُ to show who received the teaching
Added shadda and fatha at the end to make the word stronger and more emphatic
Added و at the beginning to connect this contrasting statement with what came before it
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this is describing a state of being many or abundant
Forms comparative/superlative by adding أ at start and changing vowels. This word is describing the amount of people who don't know in this ayah.
Added a short 'a' sound (fatha) at the end because it connects to 'but/however' (لٰكِنَّ) in this sentence
The initial hamza (أ) drops because it's not needed in common usage
Added ال (the) at the start to mean 'the people' instead of just 'people'

steps.m: of the people (possessed by أَكْثَرَ - most)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this word belongs to أَكْثَرَ (most) - meaning 'most of the people'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) into 'they do not know'. The fatha (َ) is added to show it's being used as a complete word rather than just letters.
Added fatha (َ) on ل to show proper pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Present tense 'they' verb with ون ending for multiple people
This is a temporal particle that shows something happened right after something else, like saying 'as soon as' or 'when' in English

steps.m: and when/and after (connects to دَخَلُوا they entered)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with what happened before. This shows this event happened after something else in the story.
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to show this happened in the past
Added وا at the end to show many people (Yusuf's brothers) did this action together in the past
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to يُوسُفَ (Yusuf) to show they entered upon/to him.
Added an alif symbol (ىٰ) at the end to show how to pronounce the word correctly when stopping at it. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the proper name, borrowed from ancient Hebrew יוֹסֵף (Yosef)

steps.m: Yusuf/Joseph (as the object being acted upon)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows that Yusuf is the object being acted upon in this sentence. This matches with the context where they 'entered upon Yusuf'.
Added fatha vowels to create basic past tense form
Added alif after hamza to make the verb causative - changing 'he sheltered' to 'he gave shelter'
Added alif madd (long vowel) to show this is a complete past tense action
Basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something
The ى changes to ي before adding pronouns - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect meaning
Adding هِ makes it mean 'to him'. In this ayah, it refers to Yusuf who was mentioned earlier.
The root letters أ-خ-و form the word 'brother' by adding a fatha (small diagonal line) above the أ. The و at the end disappears when used alone.
Adding ا at the end makes it mean 'two' of something, like two brothers
Added هُ at the end to show it means 'his brother'. This word is the object (receiver) of the verb ءَاوَىٰٓ (he took in/sheltered) in this ayah.
Added vowel marks to show this is about something that happened in the past

steps.m: he said (introducing what Yusuf said to his brother)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas. This is a common change that happens with و and ي in the middle of words to make them easier to say.
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'definitely' before a statement

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to أَخُوكَ - your brother)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show 'I' am speaking. The نّ gets a kasra (ِ) sound before ي. This word connects to the next part where Yusuf tells his brother 'I am your brother'
The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning
This is a basic personal pronoun meaning 'I'. It's one of the fundamental building blocks of Arabic and doesn't come from a root.
The small circle (۠) is added to show this is being spoken directly in speech. In this ayah, Prophet Yusuf is revealing his identity to his brother.
The root letters get vowels (a-u) to make the word mean 'brother' instead of just the general idea of brotherhood
Adding كَ at the end changes 'brother' to 'your brother'. This word connects to إنّ earlier in the sentence to say 'Indeed I am your brother'.
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' in Arabic

steps.m: so/then not (connected to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a response or result following what was said. This connects to the previous word قَالَ (he said)
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added form VIII pattern (افتعل) which shows something happening to oneself
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by adding ت at start
Changed middle vowel to kasra for present tense form
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'

steps.m: with what/with that which (refers to يَعْمَلُونَ - they used to do)
steps.n: Added مَا which means 'what' or 'that which'. Together with بِ it forms a compound meaning 'with what' or 'with that which'. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَعْمَلُونَ (they used to do).

steps.m: to be or exist in a certain state

steps.n: Connected the letters with vowels. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This word shows who was doing the action in يَعْمَلُونَ (they were doing).
Adding وا at the end changes 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural). This shows that a group of people were doing the action in يَعْمَلُونَ.
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, with ْ (sukun) on م and ُ (damma) at end to show 'he' is doing the action

steps.m: they work/act/do (referring to what they were doing to يوسف's brother)
steps.n: Added ون at the end to change from 'he' to 'they' (3 or more males). This refers back to يوسف's brothers and what they were doing.
This is a basic time particle that means 'when' something happens. It's often used to connect two events where one leads to another.

steps.m: Then when (connects to جَهَّزَهُم - he furnished them)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this event leads to what happens next in the story. Here it connects to 'he furnished them' (جَهَّزَهُم) in the next part.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle root letter ه to indicate intensive or thorough preparation

steps.m: he equipped them thoroughly (with their provisions)
steps.n: Adding هُم shows who received the action. This word connects to بِجَهَازِهِمْ (with their provisions) later in the ayah
Changes the action of preparing into the actual supplies or equipment itself
Added بِ which means 'with' - this shows they are using or carrying these supplies

steps.m: with their supplies/equipment (that يُوسُفُ gave them)
steps.n: Added هِمْ which shows these are their supplies. This refers back to the supplies that Yusuf gave them, mentioned in فَلَمَّا جَهَّزَهُم (when he equipped them) just before this word.

steps.m: to put or place something somewhere
he put (the drinking cup in السِّقَايَةَ)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word shows what Yusuf did with the drinking cup mentioned later as السِّقَايَةَ.
Added ا after second letter and kasra on first letter to form a noun meaning the action itself

steps.m: a drinking vessel (used to give water)
steps.n: Added ة to make it refer to the specific tool used for giving drink

steps.m: the drinking vessel (used to give water)
steps.n: Added ال with shadda on س to make it refer to a specific drinking vessel

steps.m: the drinking vessel (object of جَعَلَ - he put)
steps.n: Added fatHa to show this is the object that was put (by Yusuf) into his brother's belongings
This is a basic preposition that shows location. Here it specifically shows that something was placed inside the bag.
The ي is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed the root into a noun for a concrete object used when traveling

steps.m: of/in the saddlebag (belonging to أَخِيهِ - his brother)
steps.n: The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this saddlebag belongs to or is connected to something. In this ayah, it belongs to his brother (أَخِيهِ).
Added ي and kasra to show this brother belongs to something/someone
The هِ at the end shows who the brother belongs to (his). This word is in the genitive case because it comes after رَحْلِ (saddle/mount) in a possession relationship.
This is a basic connecting word (particle) that shows one thing happens after another with some time in between. It's like saying 'after that' or 'next' in English.
Added the vowel marks ُ (u-sound) and ّ (doubles the م) to show how to pronounce it correctly. This word connects to أَذَّنَ (called out) showing this happened after the previous actions.

steps.m: he announced loudly (followed by مُؤَذِّنٌ - announcer)
steps.n: Added shadda to intensify the meaning, showing this was a loud public announcement. This connects to the next word مُؤَذِّنٌ which means 'announcer'.

steps.m: to proclaim loudly, announce with emphasis
steps.n: The doubled ذ makes the announcing more emphatic/intense

steps.m: an announcer (who is calling out in this scene)
steps.n: Changed from a verb (to announce) to a person noun (announcer) by adding مُ at the start and changing the middle vowels
Adding the vowel mark makes it readable
Adding ت makes this word feminine
Adding ُ shows this word is in the nominative case

steps.m: O you (feminine, addressing العِيرُ - the caravan)
steps.n: Added attention particle ها to complete the vocative expression. This word is addressing العِيرُ (the caravan) which is treated as feminine in Arabic.
Added kasra to make it a noun meaning 'caravan' - a group that travels together with goods
Added ٱلْ to make it specific (the caravan), and ُ to show it's the subject doing something in the sentence
This is an emphatic particle that adds certainty to statements
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. This word connects to لَسٰرِقُونَ (thieves) in the next part of the ayah to mean 'indeed you all are thieves'

steps.m: to take something that belongs to others without permission

steps.n: Added alif after first letter to show this is someone who does the action of stealing
The ون ending shows we're talking about multiple thieves (more than two)
The لَ at the start adds emphasis to show they are definitely thieves. This is part of the response to the accusation in the story.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Added وا to show multiple people did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added 'أ' at start changes it from just 'facing' to actively 'turning towards' something - makes the action more purposeful
Masculine plural past tense verb conjugation

steps.m: and they turned towards (عَلَيْهِم - towards them)
steps.n: Adding و at the start connects this to the previous action (قَالُوا۟ - they said). The word عَلَيْهِم after it shows who they turned towards.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or directed at something else - like saying 'on' or 'upon' in English

steps.m: upon them (referring to the people being asked about in قَالُوا)
steps.n: Adding هِم shows this applies to a group of people (them). This connects to the previous word قَالُوا (they said) since it's referring to the same group.
Combined the question word مَا with ذَا (this) to form a stronger question word. This is like saying 'what thing?' in English.

steps.m: what (asking about تَفْقِدُونَ - you are missing)
steps.n: The م is doubled (gets a shadda) because it comes right after قَالُوا (they said). This is a common pattern when asking a question after 'they said'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense form
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you (all) lose/miss (asking the brothers what they lost)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are being addressed. This is part of a question being asked to Yusuf's brothers about what they lost.
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The و changes to ا because it's between two vowels.
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: we miss/lose (the صُوَاعَ ٱلْمَلِكِ - king's measuring cup)
steps.n: The ن at the start shows 'we' are doing the action, the ِ (kasra) and ُ (damma) show it's happening in the present
Changed the root into a noun meaning the tool used for measuring/drinking. This is what they are missing, as shown by it being the object of نَفْقِدُ (we miss/we lost).
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon (what is being lost/missed)
Changes the root into a noun meaning 'one who rules/owns'

steps.m: the king (referring to a specific king)
steps.n: Added ال to show we're talking about a specific king, and added ِ at the end because this word is being possessed by something else in the sentence
This is a basic interrogative/relative pronoun meaning 'whoever' or 'who'
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction, like saying 'for' or 'to' in English
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added short 'a' sounds (fathas) to each letter to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he came'
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
The alif is written with a maddah (آ) to show emphasis. This word is part of what 'they said' (قَالُوا) in this ayah.
Basic preposition that shows how something is done or used
Added هِ at the end to show this refers to 'him' or 'it' (referring back to صُوَاعَ - the king's drinking cup)
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changes from verb 'to carry' into noun meaning 'a single load'
The U sound at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence
Added vowels and ي to make a noun for an animal that carries loads

steps.m: a camel (that will carry the load)
steps.n: Added tanween (two kasras) at the end to show this is any camel, not a specific one. This relates to حِمْلُ (load) mentioned earlier in the ayah, as this camel will carry that load.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of the basic pronoun meaning 'I'
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English

steps.m: and I (the guarantor of حِمْلُ بَعِيرٍ)
steps.n: Added a small mark (۠) above the final alif to show this is a complete pronoun. In this ayah, the 'I' refers to the person guaranteeing the camel-load reward mentioned.
Added kasra (ِ) to show this connects something to what came before. In this ayah, it connects جَآءَ (came) with حِمْلُ (load).
Added هِ to show who/what is being connected. Here it refers back to صُوَاعَ (the king's drinking cup).
Added small ۦ mark to help with pronunciation when continuing to the next word.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (to claim) into a noun meaning someone who claims/guarantees something

steps.m: a guarantor (of bringing back what was lost)
steps.n: Added tanwin (double damma) to show this is indefinite - referring to any guarantor, not a specific one
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed from قَوَلَ by replacing weak letter و with ا
Added وا to show multiple people (3 or more men) did the action. This word starts the response where they swear by Allah.
This is the base word - Allah, which is God's proper name in Arabic. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
Adding the kasra (ِ) at the end shows possession or connection
Adding تَ at the beginning and ٱ (which is a special form of ال) makes this into an oath, like saying 'I swear by Allah!'
This is a basic particle that shows certainty or completion of an action

steps.m: certainly indeed (responding to تَٱللَّهِ - by Allah)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis. This combination is often used in responses to oaths, like here where it responds to their oath 'by Allah'
Added vowels (َ ِ َ) to the root letters to make it mean 'he knew' in the past
Added تُ (tu) at the end to change from 'he knew' to 'I knew'
Changed the ending from تُ (I) to تُم (you all) to show many people instead of one person
This is the basic negative particle ما that tells us something didn't happen. In this ayah, it's used with جِئْنَا (we came) to say 'we did not come'
The shadda (ّ) is added to make the 'م' sound stronger, which adds emphasis to the negation. This emphasizes that they really did not come to cause corruption.
The middle letter ي changes to ا and the final أ changes to ء due to Arabic spelling rules. Added fatha vowels to show past tense.
Added نَا at the end to change from 'it was brought' to 'we brought/came' - نَا shows we did it
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a basic past tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to show causing something to happen
Changed أ to نُ to show 'we' are doing the action, and added ِ (kasra) on س to show it's subjunctive mood

steps.m: so that we may cause corruption
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose - 'in order to/so that we may' do something. This connects to the previous statement in the ayah.
This is a preposition that shows something is located inside or within something else. In this ayah, it connects to الْأَرْضِ (the earth) to show location.
Changed the final ي to ى which is a common writing style variation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added marks (fatha and sukun) to the root letters to make the basic word for 'earth'
Added 'al' to make it specific and kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in/on)
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the previous statement, adding 'and' to their denial of being thieves.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters
Added نَّا at the end to show 'we' in the past tense. The و is dropped because it comes between two vowels. This word connects to سٰرِقِينَ (thieves) to mean 'we were thieves'.

steps.m: to take something that belongs to others secretly

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the word from an action (steal) to describe someone who does that action (thief)
Added ين to show multiple thieves and to show this word completes the meaning of كُنَّا (we were). The long alif (ٰ) is just a writing style variation.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action. This word starts a new sentence where 'they' are speaking.
This is the question word 'what' that's being used to ask about the punishment or recompense mentioned after it
Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from what was said before. It connects to the previous statement قَالُوا (they said)

steps.m: to give what someone deserves (good or bad)

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make a past tense verb. The ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Changed from an action to a thing by adding ء at the end
Added ُ to show this is the main subject (like saying 'THE reward' instead of just 'a reward'). The hamza (ء) changed to (ؤ) because it came after a vowel.
Added هُ to show it belongs to him. The ٰٓ and ۥٓ are just pronunciation helpers.

steps.m: if (introducing condition in كُنتُمْ كٰذِبِينَ - if you are liars)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects two parts: 'if you are liars' (the condition) leads to something else (the result). It works like 'if' in English - if something happens, then something else will happen as a result.
Added basic past tense vowels
The weak letter و drops out when we add a sukun, and the first vowel changes to match what's coming next

steps.m: you all were (addressing the brothers in the story)
steps.n: Added تُمْ at the end to show this is talking about 'you all' in the past
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
This pattern shows someone who does the action. The word is the predicate (khabar) of كنتم, telling us what 'you all' were.
Changed from singular 'liar' to plural 'liars' by adding ين at the end. Links back to كنتم (you all) in the verse.
The alif is written as a small alif (ٰ) above the letter, and a fatha is added at the end because this word is affected by كنتم
Adding the basic vowel pattern for past tense verbs
Changed middle letter و to ا and added وا at the end to make it 'they said' instead of 'he said'

steps.m: to give what is due (good or bad)

steps.n: Added fathas to make it mean 'he rewarded/repaid' in the past tense
Changes the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action
Adds ُ at the end to show this is doing the rewarding (like saying 'the reward is')

steps.m: his reward (referring to the thief's punishment)
steps.n: Adds هُ to show the reward belongs to someone (in this case, the thief). This word is part of explaining the punishment.
This is a word that refers to people in a general way. Here it connects to وُجِدَ (was found) to mean 'whoever was found'
Added the fatha mark (َ) above م to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'man'
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: it was found (in رَحْلِهِ his saddlebag)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show that something was found rather than someone doing the finding. This word connects to رَحْلِهِ (his saddlebag) which tells us where the thing was found.
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it shows what was found inside his saddlebag.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters were made into a noun meaning 'luggage' by adding vowel marks (fatha-sukun pattern)
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something/someone
Added هِۦ to show the baggage belongs to him. In this ayah, this refers to the baggage where the king's cup was found.
Starting with the basic pronoun meaning 'he'
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before. This word is the subject of جَزٰٓؤُهُۥ (his recompense) in this ayah.

steps.m: to repay or give what is due

steps.n: The root letters got vowels (fat-ha marks) to make it mean 'he repaid' in the past
Adding alif changes it from a verb (repaid) to a noun (reward)
Adding damma shows this noun is in the nominative case (subject)
Added هُۥ at the end to show possession (his/its). This word is the subject of وُجِدَ (was found) in the sentence.
Starting with the comparison particle ك that shows similarity
Added ذا which is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this/that'
Added لِ to make the word point more strongly to something (like saying 'exactly like that')

steps.m: like that, similarly, in that way (pointing to what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Added final ك which makes it point to something specific that was mentioned before
Added fathas to connect the root letters into a past tense verb

Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. Changed the vowels to show it's happening in the present. The ى at the end shows it's connected to the word الظَّالِمِينَ (the wrongdoers) who are being repaid.

steps.m: to do wrong, to be unjust
one who does wrong (acted on by نَجْزِى)
steps.n: Changed to show the person who does the action. This word is acted on by the verb نَجْزِى (we recompense).
The ين ending makes it plural (more than one) for male wrongdoers. This word is the object (the ones receiving the action) of the verb نَجْزِى (we recompense).
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite - 'the wrongdoers' instead of just 'wrongdoers'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past. This word describes what Yusuf did when searching the bags.

steps.m: so he began (searching through their bags)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action followed after something else. In this story, it shows Yusuf starting to search after deciding to check the bags.
Changes root meaning 'gather/preserve' into a word for a thing that holds stuff (like a pot or box)
Changes from singular to plural by adding أ at start and changing middle letters to make أَوْعِيَة (like how 'box' becomes 'boxes' but with Arabic pattern)
Shows these containers belong to them

steps.m: with their containers (used with فَبَدَأَ - so he began)
steps.n: The letter 'bi' (ب) was added at the start to mean 'with' - showing the containers were used to do something

steps.m: to face, accept, or come before

steps.n: Root meaning 'face/accept' becomes a simple word meaning 'before' in time
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being possessed by the word after it (وِعَآءِ meaning container)
Changed from action to thing by adding special pattern of vowels and letters

steps.m: of the container (of أَخِيهِ - his brother)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this container belongs to or is connected to something (in this case, his brother)
The kasra vowel is added to show this word means 'brother of' (someone)
Changed from 'brother' to 'his brother' by adding هِ (meaning 'his') at the end. The و changed to ي because it comes before هِ.

steps.m: then (after that) (connects to ٱسْتَخْرَجَهَا - he took it out)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows one thing happened after another. The ُ (damma) and ّ (shadda) make it stronger, showing a clear break between events.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adds 'seeking/trying to take something out' - like pulling something out with effort

steps.m: he sought to bring her/it out
steps.n: Adds 'her/it' as the object - shows what was being taken out

steps.m: he sought to bring her/it out
steps.n: The alif helps with pronunciation at the start of the word
This is a basic preposition showing that something was taken from/out of his brother's container. In this ayah, it's describing where Yusuf's people found something.
Changes the root meaning 'contain' into a noun meaning 'container' by adding vowels
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) but pronounced the same
Added kasra at the end to show this container belongs to or is connected to something (in this case, his brother)
Connected the root letters and added fatha to make the basic word for 'brother'. The final weak letter و is dropped in this form.
Added kasra to show this word will be possessed by someone
Added yaa at the end to mean 'my'
Added هِ at the end to show possession (his). This word is possessed by وِعَآءِ (container) in the ayah.
Starting with the comparison prefix ك that shows similarity
Added ذٰ (that) to specify what is being compared to
Added لِ to connect this word to what comes next, like a bridge between words
Added كَ at the end to show this is being addressed to someone

steps.m: to plan or devise (usually with clever or tricky intent)

steps.n: Added vowels to make the past tense verb 'kaada', which means 'almost did' something. This is a common verb used to show something nearly happened.
The middle letter ا is dropped and vowels change because this is a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Adding نَا changes the subject from 'he' to 'we' and shows this happened in the past
Added damma (ُ) on first letter to show proper pronunciation of the name
Added fatha (َ) at the end to show Yusuf is receiving the action of the verb كِدْنَا (we planned)

steps.m: for Yusuf/Joseph (showing purpose in كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ - we planned for Yusuf)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to show this was done for Yusuf's benefit
This is a negative particle that makes the verb كَانَ (was) into 'was not'. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase 'he was not able to' (مَا كَانَ).
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels in a past tense verb
Connected the root letters together
Added يَ at start to show 'he' does the action, and vowels to show it happens in present time
Changed the ending vowel to fatha to show subjunctive mood
Added لِ at start to show purpose - this explains why something else happens
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final و (waw) changes to ا (alif) because it's at the end of the word.
Added هُ at the end to show the brother belongs to him (Yusuf's brother). This word is the object (thing being taken out) in relation to اِسْتَخْرَجَ (he took out) in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it shows where Yusuf would take his brother - in the king's law/system.

steps.m: to judge, govern, or follow a way

steps.n: The root letters د-ي-ن become the noun 'religion' by adding the kasra sound (the small line under the د)
The final kasra shows this word is connected to 'the king' (الْمَلِكِ) that comes right after it, making it mean 'religion of the king'
Added vowels to make the basic noun 'king' from the root meaning 'to possess/rule'
Added ال to make the word definite (specific)
Added kasra at the end to show possession or belonging
Added elongated alif (آ) at the end to show emphasis and connection to what follows. This word introduces an exception clause that connects to أَن يَشَآءَ (that Allah wills).
This is a connecting word that joins two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to the word يَشَآءَ (He wills) to show what might happen. In English, we would say 'except that Allah wills'.
Root letters ش-ي-أ became شَاءَ (shaa'a) by adding vowels to make past tense 'he wanted'
Added يَ at beginning to make present tense. The word is in subjunctive mood (ending with fatha) because it comes after أَن in the ayah.
Added ا after second root letter to show long vowel sound

steps.m: The One Who Alone is Worthy of Worship
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل, showing this is a special proper noun

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَشَآءَ - He wills)
steps.n: Added ُ to show Allah is the subject doing the action of willing in this ayah. Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The ن at the start shows 'we' are doing the action, and the ُ at the end shows it's happening in the present. This verb is acting on the word دَرَجٰتٍ (degrees/ranks) that comes after it.

steps.m: to move upward step by step

steps.n: Added ة to make it into a noun for one instance of a rank or level
The alif with bar (ٰ) lengthens the sound. The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows it's indefinite. This word is the object of نَرْفَعُ (We raise) in this ayah.
Added emphasis (shadda) to show strong connection to the next word نَشَآءُ (we will). This emphasizes that Allah raises in ranks specifically those whom He wills.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Root letters ش-ي-أ became 'he wanted' by changing ي to ا (alif) and adding vowels
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed the ending to show it's happening now

Forms a noun meaning 'above' with fatha on first letter and sukoon on second letter. This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by the word كُلِّ (every/all) in the phrase 'above every possessor of knowledge'.
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
The doubled ل (shadda) makes the meaning stronger, showing it means absolutely everything with no exceptions. The ُ (damma) shows it's the main word in its phrase.
The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this word is connected to the word after it (عِلْمٍ meaning 'knowledge'), making it mean 'of all/every knowledge'
This is the basic form of the word that shows ownership or possession

steps.m: owner of, possessor of (describing كُلِّ - each/every)
steps.n: Changed the ending to show this word is being used to describe something else. Here it's describing كُلِّ (each/every) in the phrase 'above every possessor of...'
Changed the verb into a noun by adding specific vowel marks. This makes it mean the thing itself (knowledge) instead of the action (to know).
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this word is being described by عَلِيمٌ (All-Knowing) in the ayah.
Adding ي between the second and third root letters with the vowels makes this describe someone who has this quality very strongly

steps.m: All-Knowing, Most Knowledgeable (as a general attribute)
steps.n: The tanween (double damma) shows this is describing Allah's attribute of being All-Knowing in a general sense
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Added optional marks that don't change the meaning: ٓ (madd) shows long vowel sound, ۟ (small circle) shows plural
This is a conditional particle that introduces a possible situation. Here it connects to the verb يَسْرِقْ (he steals) to say 'if he steals'

steps.m: to take something that belongs to others without permission

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added يَ at start to show present/future time, changed vowels to show ongoing action

steps.m: he steals (in the condition 'if he steals')
steps.n: Changed the ending to sukun because this word comes after إِن (if) in the ayah, showing it's part of a condition
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation - fatha on ق and sukun on د

steps.m: then certainly (connects to سَرَقَ - he stole)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this statement to what comes after it, showing a sequence of thoughts. Here it connects to the word سَرَقَ (he stole) that follows.

steps.m: to take something that belongs to others without permission
he stole (what they're accusing Benjamin of)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This is what the brothers are saying about Benjamin, accusing him of stealing.
Added fatha on first letter and dropped the weak letter و to form the basic noun

steps.m: a brother (subject of سَرَقَ - he stole)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb سَرَقَ (stole) in this ayah
Starting with the basic particle ل that shows direction or purpose
The shadda shows this merged with a previous ل sound
Added هُ at the end to show this means 'him'
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it combines with قَبْلُ (before) to mean 'from before'.
Root meaning 'front/before' becomes a simple noun meaning 'before' (like 'before dinner')
