Adds damma to show this word is in nominative case
Added أ at beginning to show doing something to oneself or internally
Added فَ to show this happened after something else in the story

steps.m: then he kept it secret (acted on)
steps.n: Added ها to show what was kept secret (referring to the previous thought/statement)
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew יוֹסֵף (Yosef), meaning "He (God) will add"

steps.m: Yusuf (Joseph) (subject of أَسَرَّهَا - he kept it secret)
steps.n: Added damma marks to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb أَسَرَّهَا (he kept it secret) in this ayah
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it's used with نَفْسِهِۦ (himself) to show Yusuf kept something within himself.

steps.m: to breathe, be precious, or relate to the self/soul

steps.n: Root meaning 'self/soul' becomes a noun showing something owns/possesses it (like 'my self' or 'his self')
Adds هِ to show it belongs to him. The word is in genitive case because it comes after the preposition فِى (in).
This is a special word that makes something that happened in the past negative, like changing 'he wrote' to 'he did not write'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This word negates the verb يُبْدِهَا (show it) that comes after it.
Added يُ at the beginning to show who is doing the action (he) and when (present/future). The و at the end drops because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: he reveals it (referring to the thought/feeling Yusuf had)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show what is being revealed (it/her). In this ayah, the 'it' refers back to what Yusuf was keeping to himself.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added هُ which means 'him/it'
Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them'
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows the basic form before the weak letter change.
The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Basic form of the 'you' pronoun
Added ت with damma to make it stronger and clearer

steps.m: you all (addressing the brothers of Yusuf)
steps.n: Added م with sukun to show it's talking to multiple people
Added short 'a' sounds (fatha marks) to each letter to make a word meaning 'worse than' when comparing two things
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on ر makes the meaning stronger
Adding double dammah makes it indefinite and nominative case

steps.m: to exist or be in a place

steps.n: Adding ا after second root letter turns it into a noun meaning 'place'

steps.m: a place (being described as شَرٌّ - worse)
steps.n: Adding ًا (tanween) at the end shows this is receiving the action - it's the place being talked about
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning 'divine' but is a unique word.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly
Added damma at the end to show Allah is doing the action in this sentence

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of أَعْلَمُ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. Allah is the one who knows (أَعْلَمُ) in this verse.

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of أَعْلَمُ)
steps.n: Changed the alif to a waṣla form since it connects with the و
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning and changed vowels to make it mean 'more knowing'. This word is comparing Allah's knowledge to what the brothers are describing.
This is the base interrogative particle that means 'what'
Added بِ at the beginning to show the meaning 'with'. This combination is connected to the verb تَصِفُونَ (you describe) in this ayah, showing what they are describing with.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to present tense with ت at start showing 'you', changed middle vowel to ِ and end to ُ to show ongoing action
Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the describing

steps.m: he said (introducing what they say to العزيز)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The و from the root becomes ا in this pattern.

steps.m: they said (the brothers speaking to العزيز)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people (the brothers) are speaking
Adding damma to show it's in nominative case
Added vocative particle يٰٓ to call out to someone
Added ها to make it a complete vocative phrase
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show someone or something that has this quality very strongly
Added ال to make it specific, referring to a particular mighty one

steps.m: the mighty/powerful one (as the one being addressed)
steps.n: Added damma at the end because this word is being used as a title to address someone directly in this ayah
This is the basic form of the word that shows possibility or condition
Adding shadda changes this from showing possibility to showing certainty. This word emphasizes the statement that comes after it.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter ل
Added the pronoun ه with damma to mean 'him'
Added the elongation marks ۥٓ which help with pronunciation but don't change the meaning
Root letters أ-ب-و become the word 'father' by adding the vowel mark (fatha) above أ
Added ا and tanween (ً) at the end to show this is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/verily) in the sentence
The root letters ش-ي-خ got vowels (fatha and sukoon) to change from a verb meaning 'to be old' into a noun meaning 'an old person'

steps.m: an elderly man (who they are talking about in their request)
steps.n: Added -an ending to show this is the object being talked about (like saying 'an old man' instead of just 'old man')

steps.m: old, elderly (describing أَبًا شَيْخًا - an elderly father)
steps.n: Forms an adjective describing the father mentioned in the ayah. The word شَيْخًا also means elderly, reinforcing this meaning.
Adds tanween alif to show this word is describing their father (أَبًا) in the sentence
Changes the word into a command by dropping the first letter and adding vowel marks. This tells one person to do something.
Adds فَ at the beginning to connect this command with what was said before, like saying 'so' or 'then' in English
Adds damma to indicate imperative form
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a word meaning a single thing or person
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb خُذْ (take)

steps.m: one of us (as object of خُذْ/take)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show this means 'of us'. The whole phrase means 'take one of us' in the ayah.

steps.m: to exist or be in a place

steps.n: Changed from basic root 'to be' into past tense 'was' by adding vowels
Added مَ at the beginning to create a noun meaning 'place where something exists'
Added هُ at the end to show the place belongs to him. This word is what is being taken in the command 'take' (خُذْ).
Added pronunciation helpers (ۥٓ) that don't change the meaning
This is an emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' at the start of a sentence.
Added ا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This word connects to نَرَىٰكَ (we see you) in this ayah, where 'we' are the ones doing the seeing.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' as the doers, and changed the end to ى because it's happening now. The أ is dropped because it comes between two vowels.
Added كَ at the end to show you are being seen. The ىٰ has an alif above it (called an alif maqsurah) to show it's stretched out when reading.
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word الْمُحْسِنِينَ (the good-doers). This is a helper sound that makes the words flow better when speaking.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding م at the start and changing vowels turns this into a word describing someone who does the action
The word changed to mean 'many people who do good' by adding ين at the end. It has this ending because it comes after مِنَ (from/of)
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular good-doers
Added vowel marks to make it past tense. This shows someone did the action in the past.
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ because of weak letter و - this is a common change in Arabic words with و in the middle
Added an alif (ا) between first two root letters and vowels to make it 'he sought refuge' in past tense

Added مَ at beginning to transform the verb into a noun meaning the place or concept of seeking refuge. In this ayah, it's used in the phrase 'Allah's protection' to express seeking divine protection from doing something wrong.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic noun form meaning 'a deity'
The word Allah (الله) comes from the word for deity (إله) but is a unique name. The kasra (-i) sound at the end shows it belongs to or connects with the word 'refuge' (معاذ) before it.
A particle that connects this part to what came before and introduces what will happen. Here it connects to the previous phrase 'God forbid' and introduces what they won't do.

steps.m: to grasp or take possession of something

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense with 'we' as the subject by adding نَ at the start and changing vowels
Changed to subjunctive mood, often used after 'that' or 'to'

steps.m: except (the one in whom we found our property)
steps.n: This is a basic exception particle that shows something or someone is excluded from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it excludes the person who had their cup from those they won't take.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who they found their goods with.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This word is the main verb in this part of the verse.
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the thing that provides benefit. This word is what was found (object of وَجَدْنَا - we found).
Added نَا at the end to show it belongs to "us". The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this word.

steps.m: to be present, to be with
steps.n: This word is used to show location or possession, like saying something is 'at' or 'with' someone
Added vowels to make it a preposition showing location or possession
Added pronoun suffix ه with damma to show 'him' as the possessor
Added optional lengthening marks after the pronoun to show emphasis
This is an emphasis particle that makes the following statement stronger. It connects to ظٰلِمُونَ (wrongdoers) to emphasize that they would definitely be wrongdoers.
Added آ at the end which shows 'we'. The آ here represents نا (meaning 'we'), but the ن merged into the previous نّ. This 'we' is the subject doing the action in ظٰلِمُونَ (wrongdoers).
Basic particle showing time or consequence

steps.m: then/in that case (showing consequence of وَجَدْنَا - we found)
steps.n: Adding tanween alif (ًا) changes the meaning to show a result or consequence. Here it shows what would happen if they took someone other than the one who had their goods.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action
Makes the word plural to show multiple people
Adds emphasis to show certainty
This is a basic particle meaning 'when' that shows something happened after another thing happened
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what happened before
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show doing something intensely - in this case, reaching a state of complete hopelessness
Added وا to show multiple people and changed initial alif to waṣla form
Basic preposition meaning 'from'

steps.m: from him (referring to their brother who was taken)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه with damma (هُ) at the end to show who is being referred to

steps.m: to be alone/private, to separate oneself

steps.n: Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding fatha (a) vowels

steps.m: they went aside privately (to consult with each other)
steps.n: Adds masculine plural suffix وا with damma before it to show multiple brothers went aside together

steps.m: to speak privately or consult in secret

steps.n: Root letters turned into 'he' past tense verb by adding the short 'a' sounds (َ)
Changed from a verb (he spoke privately) to a noun meaning someone who regularly has private conversations - like changing 'speak' to 'speaker'
Added tanween alif to show this describes how they gathered - in private consultation
Added vowel marks to show this is about someone (he) doing the action in the past. This word tells us what their eldest brother (كَبِيرُهُمْ) did.
The middle letter و changes to ا to make it easier to say. This is very common in Arabic words that have و in the middle.

steps.m: elder/great one (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Root ك-ب-ر became كَبِيرُ (great one) by adding vowels and ي. The ُ ending shows it's the subject - the one who is great and is speaking

steps.m: their elder one (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Adds possessive pronoun suffix meaning 'their'
This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of the verb after it to a negative past tense

steps.m: did not (asking about تَعْلَمُوا - you all know)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to turn the negative statement into a question. This combination is asking if something did not happen.
Added vowels to change from root 'know' to past tense 'he knew'
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show it's happening now
Added وا at the end to show many people are doing the action
Basic particle form that connects parts of a sentence, like saying 'that' in English
Adding the shadda and vowel marks makes the meaning stronger and connects it to the verb تَعْلَمُوٓا۟ (you know), showing what is known with certainty
Connected the root letters أ-ب-و with vowels to make the word 'father'
Changed the ending to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Added كُمْ to show this is your (plural) father. This word is what's being known in تَعْلَمُوا (you know)

This is a helper word that makes the verb أَخَذَ (has taken) stronger. When قَدْ comes before a past-tense verb like أَخَذَ, it shows that something definitely happened or is complete, like saying 'indeed' or 'already' in English.
Connected the letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb. This word shows what the older brother did in the story, as he is the one speaking (قَالَ - he said).
Combination of preposition عَلَى (upon) and pronoun كُم (you plural)
Root letters got vowels to make past tense 'he' form
Added مَ at the beginning and changed vowels to make a noun meaning 'the thing that was secured' (like a promise or pledge)
Adding shadda (double mark) on م makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
Added tanween fatha to show this is the object being taken in the phrase 'took a pledge'
Basic preposition meaning 'from' with sukun at end for normal pronunciation
The ن merges with the م that comes after it, shown by the shadda (double letter mark)
Added fatha at end to connect with the next word (اللَّهِ). This shows it means 'from Allah'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added 'al' to make it specific/definite, like changing 'a god' to 'the god'
The word 'Allah' comes from 'al-ilah' (the god), but is a special name just for God. The ِ at the end shows it belongs to or connects with another word (covenant)
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic word
Changing the middle vowel to a sukun (no vowel) turns this from a verb into the word 'before'
The word 'before' gets a u-sound (damma) at the end to show it's being used as a subject or after certain words
This is a question word that asks about what happened. In this ayah, it's asking about what they did wrong regarding Yusuf, connecting to the next word فَرَّطتُمْ (you neglected/failed).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action
Changed تُ to تُمْ to show many people being addressed
Basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it connects to يُوسُفَ (Joseph) to show what the speaker is 'in regards to' or 'concerning'.
This is the basic form of the proper name Joseph (يوسف) in Arabic. It comes from the Hebrew name Yosef (יוֹסֵף).
The fatha (َ) at the end shows that Joseph is being talked about as the object in this sentence, specifically what they 'gave up hope about' (ٱسْتَيْـَٔسُوا۟ مِنْهُ)
Basic negative particle that negates future actions
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence. This connects to the verb أَبْرَحَ (I will leave) that comes after it, making the complete meaning 'so I will never leave'

steps.m: to leave or move away from a place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes it to 'I will' form
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun

steps.m: the earth (object of أَبْرَحَ - I will leave)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's the object being acted upon in the sentence. This word is what's being left/departed from in the phrase 'I will not leave the earth'.
Starting with the basic particle letters connected with fatha
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on ت strengthens the meaning of reaching a limit

steps.m: until/up to (connects to يَأْذَنَ - he gives permission)
steps.n: Adding alif maqsura (ىٰ) at the end makes this word connect to what comes after it. Here it connects to يَأْذَنَ meaning 'until he gives permission'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Changed from past to future tense by adding يَ at the start, showing something that hasn't happened yet. Used after 'until' (حَتَّىٰ) to show waiting for permission.
Added kasra to the preposition ل to show it connects to what comes after it
Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'
Added the elongation mark (ٓ) to show the long vowel sound. This word is receiving the action of يَأْذَنَ (permits/gives permission) in this ayah.

steps.m: to care for/nurture as a father

steps.n: Root أ-ب-و becomes the basic word for 'father' by adding the vowel marks a-u (َُ)
Changed the final vowel to kasra to show this word will be possessed by something
Added ى at the end to show the father belongs to 'me'
Added elongation mark (ٓ) to show the long vowel sound is emphasized at the end

steps.m: or (connecting يَأْذَنَ 'he gives permission' with يَحْكُمَ 'He judges')
steps.n: Basic conjunction that connects two possible alternatives. In this ayah, it connects two possibilities: either the father gives permission OR Allah makes a judgment.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and damma on middle root letter
Added fatha at end to show this is subjunctive mood - used when something might/could happen
Adding fatha ending to indicate subjunctive mood
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel patterns
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters with shadda. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَحْكُمَ - He judges)
steps.n: Added ُ to show Allah is the one doing the action (judging) in this verse
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'

steps.m: to/for me (subject of يَأْذَنَ - gives permission)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'. This word is connected to يَأْذَنَ (gives permission) in this ayah, showing who receives the permission.
Added diacritics to show the correct pronunciation of the basic pronoun
Added the conjunction وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before. This pronoun هُوَ (he) refers to Allah who is the subject of the verb يَحْكُمَ (judges) in this ayah.
Root letters for 'good' get vowels to make a noun meaning 'good/better/best'

steps.m: best (describing الله as the best of judges)
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject/doer - here it shows Allah is the best of something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed pattern to show who does the action (like changing 'judge' to 'judge-r')
Added ين to make it plural (more than one judge)

steps.m: the judges (possessed by خَيْرُ - best of)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and a final َ because it's possessed by the word 'best' (خَيْرُ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changed from 'he went back' to 'go back!' by adding اِ at start and changing vowels to make it a command
Changed from singular 'go back!' to plural 'all of you go back!' by adding وا at the end
Word is complete with both plural suffix وا and command prefix اِ
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. It connects words by showing where something is going.
Added alif maksura (ى with small alif above) at the end. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small vertical line (ٓ) above the word. This mark helps with proper Quranic recitation but doesn't change the meaning.
The و drops because it's a weak letter at the end
Adding ي makes it mean 'my father' instead of just 'father'
The كُمْ shows the father belongs to multiple people being spoken to
Changed the root 'say' into a command by adding a damma (u sound) - like telling someone 'say this!'
Added a sukoon (silent mark) on the middle of the word to complete the command form
Added وا to make the command for a group instead of one person
Added فَ to connect this command with the previous statement (ٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ - return)

steps.m: to nurture/care for (as a father does)

steps.n: Added fatha to make it a noun meaning father
Added alif at end to show there are two fathers
Added نا at the end to make it 'our father' - نا means 'our' when attached to nouns
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to show we're calling out to the father, and lengthened the final vowel for emphasis
Added kasra vowel mark to help with pronunciation
Added shadda to strengthen the meaning. This particle makes what follows more certain and changes the ending of the next word.
Forms the basic noun 'son' from the concept of being brought into existence
Changes the word to show it's receiving the action of the verb سَرَقَ (stole)
Adds 'your' to show whose son it is. This son is being talked about as the one who stole.

steps.m: to take something that belongs to someone else without permission
he stole (what they're accusing the brother of)
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) after each letter to show this happened in the past. In this ayah, the brothers are telling their father about what they think his son (their brother) did.
This is a particle used to negate verbs (meaning 'not') or ask questions (meaning 'what'). In this ayah, it means 'not' because it's negating what they knew.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. Here it's specifically negating what they knew, connecting to عَلِمْنَا (we knew) in the ayah.
Root letters got vowels added to make past tense 'he witnessed'
Changed final vowel to sukun to prepare for suffix
Added نَآ to show 'we' did this action. This is part of what the brothers are saying to their father.

steps.m: except/only (introduces an exception to شَهِدْنَا - we testified)
steps.n: Basic form of the exceptive particle, composed of two parts: إِنْ (if/indeed) + لا (no/not)

steps.m: except/only (introduces an exception to شَهِدْنَا - we testified)
steps.n: The ل and ل merge together with a shadda (ّ) to make it easier to say. The first vowel mark (ِ) stays on إ and we add a fatha (َ) on the shadda.
This is a relative pronoun that refers to the knowledge mentioned later in عَلِمْنَا (we knew)
Added the preposition بِ to show this knowledge was the basis or means of their testimony
Added vowels (َ ِ َ) to the root letters to make it mean 'he knew' in the past
Adding نا changes it from 'he knew' to 'we knew'
This is a particle that can mean different things. Here it means 'not' because it's negating the verb شَهِدْنَا (we witnessed).
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects the denial of witnessing with the previous statement about the son stealing.
The و is a weak letter that affects how the word is formed
Added vowels to connect the letters and make the basic past tense form
The word changes from 'he was' to a command meaning 'be!' by dropping the middle letter و and changing the vowels
Added نا at the end to mean 'we' and show past tense
This form turns the concept of being hidden into a noun meaning 'the unseen'
Changes from describing an action (being hidden) to naming the thing itself (unseen realm)
Adding ال makes it specific - talking about a particular unseen realm
Adding لِ at the beginning shows this is being directed to or for something. The ِ at the end shows it follows the لِ.
Basic meaning is about protecting or preserving something
This pattern shows someone who does the action regularly or as their role
Added ين to make it plural. This word comes after كُنَّا (we were) in the phrase 'we were not protectors of the unseen'
Changed root letters into command form by adding marks (sukun-fatha-sukun) to tell one person to ask
Added kasra at the end to show it's a command
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect with previous statement
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters

steps.m: a village/town (a place where people gather)
steps.n: Adding ة turns the action of gathering into a place where people gather (like a town)
Adding ال makes the noun definite

steps.m: the village/town (object being asked about in وَسْـَٔلِ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being asked about in the phrase 'and ask about the town'
This is the base form of the feminine relative pronoun used to connect descriptions to feminine nouns
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one that' instead of just 'that'
The ال becomes ٱلّ with a shadda, and the final ي is written as ى. This word connects 'the town' to the description 'we were in it'
The middle weak letter و drops out in past tense, and vowels adjust accordingly
Added نَّا at the end to show 'we' did the action in the past
Basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it's connected to the verb كُنَّا (we were) to show where we were.
Added ها to refer back to الْقَرْيَةَ (the town) which is feminine in Arabic. This connects the preposition to what it's talking about.
Added kasra to create the noun meaning 'caravan'
Added the definite article 'the'

steps.m: and the caravan (object of وَسْـَٔلِ - ask about)
steps.n: Added conjunction 'and' at the beginning and fatha at the end to show this is the object being asked about in the phrase 'ask about the caravan'
This is the basic form of the feminine relative pronoun, used to connect descriptions to feminine nouns
Added ٱلْ (the) and doubled the letter ت to make it mean 'the one which' instead of just 'which'
Added a small alif (ٓ) after the ى to show this word connects to the verb كُنَّا (we were) that comes after it
This form makes it mean a time or place before something
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to come forward' instead of just 'to face'
Adding نا at the end shows 'we' did the action instead of 'he'
This is a basic word showing where something is located. In this ayah, it's talking about being in a town.

steps.m: in it (referring to القَرْيَةَ - the town)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to the town (القَرْيَةَ) mentioned earlier. Since 'town' is feminine in Arabic, ها means 'it' referring to something feminine.
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' in English
Adding ا at the end makes it about 'we/us'

steps.m: and indeed we (who are saying لَصٰدِقُونَ/truly truthful)
steps.n: Adding وَ at the beginning connects this to the previous statement. This word connects to لَصٰدِقُونَ (truly truthful) which comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second letter to show who does the action
Added ون to make it plural (more than one person)
Added لَ at start to emphasize we are DEFINITELY telling the truth

steps.m: he said (introducing what Ya'qub says next)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to make the basic past tense form. This word introduces Ya'qub's response in the next part of the ayah.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle

This is a particle that shows Ya'qub (as) is correcting or contradicting what was just said. In this ayah, he's rejecting the implied excuse and saying that their souls made something appealing to them instead.
Added sukun (ْ) on the last letter to show where the word stops

steps.m: to make something seem attractive or appealing (even if it's wrong)
he made something seem attractive/appealing (even if wrong)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels and doubled the middle letter to show intensity of making something seem appealing

steps.m: it (your soul) made (something seem attractive) (subject of أَنفُسُكُمْ)
steps.n: Added ت to show the subject is feminine (referring to أَنفُسُكُمْ - your soul/self)
Added fatha to the letter ل to show it's a preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'

steps.m: for you all (the ones whose souls made something seem pleasing)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end which means 'you all'. This shows who the preposition 'for' is directed to. In this ayah, it refers to the people being addressed about what their souls made seem pleasing to them.
Added vowels to make the basic noun meaning 'self' or 'soul'
Changes from singular 'self' to plural 'selves' by adding أ at the start and changing middle vowel to ُ

steps.m: your (plural) selves (subject of سَوَّلَتْ - made fair-seeming)
steps.n: Adds كُمْ at the end to show the selves belong to multiple people being addressed. This word is the subject (doer) of سَوَّلَتْ (made fair-seeming).
Root meaning 'command' becomes the noun 'matter/affair' - this is what was made to look attractive in the verse

steps.m: a matter (object of سَوَّلَتْ - made attractive)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is an indefinite noun being acted upon. It's what their souls made attractive to them.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to turn the action into a noun meaning the quality itself
Added double-damma to show this is the main subject being talked about

steps.m: so patience (as response to previous statement)
steps.n: Added fa at beginning to show this is Yaqub's response to what was just said - like saying 'Well then, patience...'
Added vowels and ي to create an adjective. This word describes the patience (صَبْرٌ) that Jacob shows in the face of difficulty.
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing a general, noble kind of patience
This makes it into a common word meaning 'perhaps' or 'maybe'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added damma to show this is the subject

steps.m: that (connects to يَأْتِيَنِى - he brings them)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two parts of the sentence together. Here it connects the hope (عَسَى) with what is hoped for (that Allah brings them). It works like 'that' in English when you say 'I hope that this happens'.
Added يَ at beginning to show 'he' is doing the action in present time. Added vowels to make it a complete word.
Added نِى at the end to show the action is happening to 'me'
Added kasra to the letter ب to make it readable. This is a preposition that shows how something is done.
Added ه with kasra to show this applies to a single male person or thing

steps.m: with them (referring to bringing all his sons back)
steps.n: Added م with sukun to change from singular (him) to plural (them). In this ayah, it refers to Ya'qub's hope that Allah will bring all his sons back to him.

steps.m: to join or bring things together

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show completeness, with vowel marks to connect the letters
Added ًا at the end to show this word describes how something happens in the sentence
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to 'indeed' - it makes the meaning stronger and more certain
Adding هُ at the end shows who or what we're talking about - in this case 'he'
The ۥ mark is just a pronunciation helper showing to make the هُ sound longer
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it refers back to Allah and connects to الْعَلِيمُ (the All-Knowing) which comes right after it.
Adding ي between the second and third root letters with the specific vowel pattern creates a special form showing complete and perfect knowledge
Adding ال makes it definite (like 'the' in English) and shows this is one of Allah's divine names. The ُ shows this is describing Allah directly.
Added ي between second and third root letters to show someone who has this quality strongly
Added ال to make it definite (like 'the' in English) and ُ to show it's describing Allah. When used as a name of Allah, it means He has perfect and complete wisdom.

steps.m: to turn away from, to leave

steps.n: The doubled ل shows this action was done with intensity or emphasis
Adding تَ shows this is something he did to himself - he chose to turn away

steps.m: and he turned himself away (from عَنْهُمْ - from them)
steps.n: Added 'and' (و) at the start to connect to what happened before. The long ى shows he really turned away completely.

steps.m: away from them (referring to his sons who returned without Benjamin)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this is about multiple people (his sons). This word connects to وَتَوَلَّىٰ (and he turned away) to show Prophet Yaqub turned away from his sons.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he said' in the past
The weak letter و changes to ا because it comes after fatha

steps.m: and he said (introducing what Ya'qub said next)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this to the previous action. This introduces what Ya'qub said after turning away from his sons.

steps.m: to grieve, to feel deep sorrow

steps.n: Adding short vowels (fat-ha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he felt sorrow'
Added يا (ya) at the start to express 'Oh!' when calling out in sadness - like saying 'Oh, how sad!'

steps.m: Oh my sorrow! (expressing deep grief about يُوسُفَ)
steps.n: Added ىٰ at the end to show possession (my sorrow) and extend the sound for emphasis in lamenting. This word expresses Jacob's deep grief about losing Joseph.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else. Here it connects to يُوسُفَ (Yusuf) to show what he was grieving over.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word - 'a' sound (َ) after ع and after ل
Added the small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's pronounced with a long 'aa' sound at the end
This is a non-Arabic proper name, so no morphological analysis is needed
Added damma vowel marks to help with pronunciation

steps.m: Joseph (as the object being grieved over)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Joseph is being acted upon - he is the one being grieved over in 'Oh my sorrow over Joseph!'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Added alif at start and shadda at end to show color change intensifying
Added ت to show feminine subject (referring to عَيْنَاهُ - his eyes)

steps.m: and she/it became intensely white (referring to his eyes)
steps.n: Added و to connect with previous statement
Added vowel marks (fatha and sukoon) to the root letters to make the word for 'eye'
Added alif at end to show there are two of something
Added هُ to show the eyes belong to him (Ya'qub). This word is the subject doing the action in ٱبْيَضَّتْ (became white)
Basic preposition indicating source, origin, or separation from something
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word الْحُزْنِ (sadness/grief). This combination means 'from sadness/grief'
Changes the action of feeling sad into the emotion itself
Shows this grief is connected to the word مِنَ (from/because of) that comes before it in the ayah

steps.m: the grief (that made his eyes turn white from مِنَ ٱلْحُزْنِ)
steps.n: The ال shows this is referring to the specific grief that made Jacob's eyes turn white from crying over Joseph
This is a pronoun meaning 'he'. Since it's a basic particle, we start with its letters separated.

steps.m: so he (connected to وَٱبْيَضَّتْ - became white)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what happened before - his eyes became white from grief, so he...
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he held back' in the past
Changed to pattern فَعِيل to show someone completely filled with held-back emotions
Connected the root letters and changed و to ا because it comes between two fathas. This makes the word mean something that happened in the past.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action. This word is connected to تَفْتَؤُا۟ (you continue) in the next part of the ayah.
This is the base word - Allah, which is God's proper name in Arabic. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows possession or connection
Adding تَ at the beginning makes this into an oath, like saying 'I swear by Allah!' This is a special form only used with Allah's name.
Added تَ at the beginning to mean 'you', making it 'you stop/cease'. The sukun (ْ) shows where to pause between sounds.
The word gets a ḍammah (ُ) at the end because it's a present tense verb in a normal sentence (like saying 'you are not stopping')
Added ا with small و above (۟) at the end to show emphasis. In this context, it's used with تَٱللَّهِ (by Allah) before it to make a strong oath meaning 'By Allah, you will not stop...'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Present tense prefix تَ shows you are doing it, and the vowel pattern change shows it's happening now

steps.m: you remember/mention (addressing Yaqub as the doer of the action)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows Yaqub is doing the action of remembering/mentioning
This is the basic form of the proper name Yusuf (Joseph). The damma marks help with pronunciation.

steps.m: Joseph (as the object being remembered in تَذْكُرُ)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows that Joseph is the object being remembered in the phrase 'you remember Joseph'
This is a particle that shows a limit or endpoint. It connects two parts of a sentence, with the second part being the endpoint or goal.
Added the elongated alif (ىٰ) at the end. In this ayah, it connects to the word حَرَضًا (critically ill/extremely grief-stricken), showing the endpoint of their concern about Yusuf.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
تَ at start shows either 'you' or 'she' as the doer
The ending changes to fatha because it comes after حَتَّىٰ (until/unless) which requires this change
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb

steps.m: a severely ill/wasted away person (predicate of تَكُونَ)
steps.n: Added tanwin alif (ًا) to show this describes someone's state. This word follows تَكُونَ (to become) in the ayah, which is why it has this ending.

steps.m: or (connects تَكُونَ حَرَضًا with تَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْهٰلِكِينَ)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two possibilities together: either 'becoming extremely ill' or 'being among those who perish'
This is a particle that shows something is part of a group or comes from somewhere
Adding the basic vowels creates the simplest past tense verb meaning
The weak letter و changes to ا due to hollow verb pattern
Changed from past to present tense - notice the يَ at the start shows it's happening now. The و that was hidden in كَانَ comes back in يَكُونُ
Changed from 'he' to 'you/she' by replacing يَ with تَ at the start
The word changes its ending from 'u' to 'a' because حَتَّىٰ (until) came before it, showing what might happen in the future
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added َ at the end to connect smoothly with the next word الْهٰلِكِينَ (the perished ones). This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.

steps.m: to die, perish, or be destroyed
steps.n: Basic past tense form of the verb
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Makes the word plural to talk about multiple people (comes after مِنَ 'from/of' in this ayah)

steps.m: the ones who are perishing (after مِنَ)
steps.n: Adds 'the' to specify which people and shows this comes after the word مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah
Added short vowels (fatha) to make the past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
This particle adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
When مَا is added to إِنَّ, it changes the meaning from just emphasis to restriction - showing that ONLY this thing is true and nothing else
The alif is written with a long mark (آ) when it comes at the end of a word, but this doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to complain about something to someone

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' am doing the action. Changed middle vowel to damma and removed last vowel because it's a present tense verb. The و at the end is from the root.

steps.m: to spread out feelings/emotions (especially grief or worry)

steps.n: Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to show the intensity of the emotional state

steps.m: my distress (that I am telling to اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added ِى at the end to show this is my personal distress. In this ayah, Ya'qub (Jacob) is telling Allah about his deep personal grief.
Changed from action to the feeling itself. This word is what is being complained about in the phrase 'I complain of my grief'.
Added ى at the end to show it's my grief. The alif at the end (ىٓ) is just for pronunciation.
Added وَ at beginning to connect this with the previous word بَثِّى (my distress), showing both are being complained about.
This is a preposition that shows I am complaining TO Allah, indicating the direction of the complaint
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel marks

Added ال (the) at the start, doubled the middle letter with shadda, and added kasra at the end to show this word is affected by the word مِنَ (from) that comes before it in the ayah. The hamza at the start changed to alif with wasl mark because it comes after another word.
Connected the root letters with vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Added أ at start to make it comparative ('more/better'), not the 'I' form. Changed vowels to make it mean 'know better than' - like saying 'more knowing'
Added و at beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Basic preposition showing source or origin
Added َ (fatha) at the end to connect smoothly to the next word اللَّهِ (Allah). This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
The word Allah comes from the word for 'god/deity' (ilah), but adds AL in front to mean 'THE God' - the one and only. The i at the end shows it's connected to another word.
This is a relative pronoun connecting to the verb أَعْلَمُ (I know). In this context, it means 'that which' rather than being a question word.

This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَعْلَمُونَ (you know) into its opposite meaning (you do not know). It's one of the most basic and common words for saying 'no' or making something negative in Arabic.
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how it's pronounced with an 'a' sound
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you (all) know (in contrast to what Allah knows)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people. This word contrasts with أَعْلَمُ (I know) earlier in the verse, emphasizing the difference between human and divine knowledge.
Adding vowel marks to make it a complete past tense verb
Adding يٰ (yaa) at the start turns this into a way to call out to your son - like saying 'O my son!' or 'Hey son!'
Changed the ending to ىَّ which is another way to write 'my' when calling out to someone
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he went' to a command 'go!' by adding alif at start and putting a sukoon (stop mark) on the last letter to make it sound shorter and stronger like a command

steps.m: go! (all of you) (command to يٰبَنِىَّ - O my sons)
steps.n: Added وا to make it plural command. This is addressing Yaqub's sons, as shown by يٰبَنِىَّ (O my sons) before it.
Connected the root letters and added basic past tense vowels
Added 'تَ' and doubled middle letter to show doing action thoroughly

steps.m: so he searched thoroughly (after يٰبَنِىَّ ٱذْهَبُوا۟)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action follows after 'O my sons, go!' (يٰبَنِىَّ ٱذْهَبُوا۟)

steps.m: so search thoroughly (command to multiple people)
steps.n: Changed to a command form for multiple people (Ya'qub telling his sons to search)
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'despair' with what they shouldn't despair from - Allah's mercy.
Added damma marks to show proper pronunciation of the name

steps.m: Joseph (as the object being searched for)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Joseph is the object being searched for in the phrase 'search for Joseph'
The word changes to show 'my brother' or 'brother of' by adding the kasra sound
Adds 'wa' (and) at the start, and changes the ending to show 'my brother'
Adds هِ to show the brother belongs to Yusuf. This word follows مِن (from/of) in the phrase 'seek news of Yusuf and his brother.'
This is the basic negation word that makes things negative, like saying 'not' or 'don't' in English
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation

steps.m: and not (connects to فَتَحَسَّسُوا۟ - and search)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this idea with the previous command to search. It's like saying 'search AND don't lose hope'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed middle vowel to sukun and final vowel to damma for present tense
Added وا at the end to make it plural (talking to many people instead of one person), and written in special Quran spelling
Basic preposition showing the starting point or source of an action. Here it shows what they should search for - Yusuf.
The root meaning changes to refer to the spirit or soul
Changed from a basic root meaning 'to bring relief' into a simple noun meaning 'relief/comfort' by adding vowel marks
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being used after a preposition
The shadda (double letter) was added to make the 'r' sound stronger, showing this is a very special relief. The kasra shows it belongs to Allah.
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a unique name for The One True God

Added ِ at the end to show that this word belongs to or describes something else (in this case, it shows that the mercy belongs to Allah). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form.
This is a special word used at the start of sentences to add emphasis, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Added هُ (he/it) at the end to show who is being emphasized. The ۥ mark is just to show the 'u' sound more clearly. In this ayah, it refers back to Allah's comfort/relief mentioned earlier.
Basic negative particle that tells people not to do something. In this ayah, it's telling them 'do not despair' (لَا تَا۟يْـَٔسُوا۟)
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Basic meaning of losing hope
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the pattern to show this happens in the present/future
Changed spelling to match Quranic orthography. This word comes after لا (not) to mean 'he does not lose hope'
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it shows what they should search starting from/about Yusuf.
Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning the comfort or relief itself
Added kasra at the end to show this comfort belongs to Allah in the phrase روح الله (Allah's comfort/relief)
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a unique name for The One True God
Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at end to show possession

This is a basic particle used for exception. It shows that what comes after it is excluded from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it excludes 'the disbelieving people' from those who don't lose hope in Allah's mercy.

steps.m: to stand firmly, to rise up, to establish

steps.n: Changed the root meaning of standing/establishing into a noun for a group of people who stand together as an established community

steps.m: the people (who do not despair of Allah's mercy)
steps.n: The word gained 'ال' (al) at the start to mean 'the people' instead of just 'people', and the ُ (damma) at the end shows it's doing the main action

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added alif after first letter to show who does the action. The ِ (kasra) under ف shows this is someone who actively does this.
Added ون to show this refers to a group of people (more than two)
Added ال (written as ٱلْ) at the beginning to make the noun definite, referring to specific disbelievers
The ال (the) is written as ٱلْ, the long 'a' sound is shown with ٰ, and the ُونَ ending shows this is a plural noun doing the action (rejecting/disbelieving)
This is the basic word meaning 'when' that shows something happened right after something else
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of something before it
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: they entered (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added وا to show many people did this action. This group is the same 'they' who speak in the next word قَالُوا (they said)
Basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns

steps.m: upon him (referring to يوسف who they entered upon)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to show this means 'him'. This refers back to Prophet Yusuf who they entered upon.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action

steps.m: to point to or indicate something
steps.n: Basic root meaning
The root letters combine with a doubled middle letter (shadda) to make a question word meaning 'which'
Added يٰٓ at start to call attention, like saying 'hey' to someone
Added a small u-vowel mark (damma) to make it a proper way to call out to someone, like saying 'Hey you!'
Added ها to make it a general way to address people, like saying 'O everyone!'
Basic root meaning
Added ي between second and third root letters and vowels to show someone who has this quality strongly. This word is being used to address Yusuf (as) in this ayah.

Adding ال makes it specific (like 'the' in English), and the ُ shows this is being used to address someone. This is part of يٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ (O mighty one) where Yusuf's brothers are addressing him with respect.
Basic root meaning
Root letters م-س-س were connected and the two س combined into one with a shadda (doubled) to make the past tense verb 'he touched'
Added نَا at the end to show that 'us' is receiving the action. In this ayah, the brothers are telling Yusuf that hardship has affected them.
Basic root meaning
Connected the root letters together. This word is being acted upon by the verb مَسَّ (touched/afflicted) in this ayah.
Added نا at the end to show it belongs to 'us'. This word along with نا (us) are being affected by hardship in the verse.
و at start means 'and'
Basic root meaning
Changed from action to noun by adding damma and doubling middle letter. This word describes what affected them in the phrase 'touched us'.

steps.m: the hardship (that affected مَسَّنَا - touched us)
steps.n: Added ال to show this is talking about their specific hardship mentioned in the phrase 'touched us'
Basic root meaning
The root letters ج-ي-أ change to جِئْ (ji') to make the command 'come!' - the middle letter ي becomes a hamza
Adding نا at the end shows we are the ones who did the action (we came)
Adding و at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Basic root meaning
Forms a noun meaning a single piece or portion from the action of cutting/dividing
Adding ة makes it feminine and specifies a single instance

steps.m: with a portion (of merchandise they brought to trade)
steps.n: بِ at start means 'with', tanween (ٍ) shows it's indefinite ('a' not 'the'). This refers to the small amount of merchandise they brought to trade.

steps.m: to push or drive something away
steps.n: Basic root meaning
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show it's something receiving the action, like a weak animal being pushed along

steps.m: something definitely pushed away/of little worth
steps.n: Added shadda on م to emphasize the meaning
Added ة to make it feminine and refer to بِضٰعَةٍ (merchandise) in the ayah
Added tanween kasra to show this describes the merchandise (بِضٰعَةٍ) they brought

steps.m: to fulfill completely or give full measure
steps.n: Basic root meaning
Changed to command form by adding أَ at start and changing middle letter to وْ. This tells someone to give full measure in trade.

steps.m: so fulfill! (give full measure) (command in response to مَسَّنَا - touched us)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this is a response to their statement about hardship (مَسَّنَا). It's like saying 'since hardship touched us, therefore give us full measure'
Starting with the basic preposition ل which shows direction or purpose

steps.m: for us/to us (object of أَوْفِ - fulfill)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show this is happening to 'us'. This word connects to أَوْفِ (fulfill) in the ayah, showing who should receive the full measure.

steps.m: to measure out (especially grain or other goods)
steps.n: Basic root meaning

steps.m: a measure, measured portion (object of أَوْفِ - give in full)
steps.n: Changed the action into a noun meaning one instance of measuring. This word is what should be given in full measure.

steps.m: the measure (object of أَوْفِ - give in full)
steps.n: ال makes it specific, َ shows it's receiving the action

steps.m: to speak truth, to be honest
steps.n: Basic root meaning

steps.m: to confirm with actions, to prove true
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning more active - moving from being truthful to showing truth through actions
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'

steps.m: and give charitably (as proof of sincerity)
steps.n: Adding تَ changes it from 'confirming' to 'actively giving charity' - shows someone doing the action themselves
The sukun makes it a command form, telling someone to perform the action
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
When على connects to pronouns, the ى changes to يْ to make it easier to add endings
Added نَآ at the end to show 'us'. In this ayah, Yusuf's brothers are asking him for charity, so they're saying 'be charitable upon us'.
Basic particle that connects parts of sentences, showing conditions or introducing statements
Adding shadda on the ن changes this from a simple connector to a strong emphasis word. It makes what comes after it more important, like adding an exclamation mark in English
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
The word 'Allah' gets a stronger L sound (shadda), starts with a connecting alif, and ends with a fatha because it comes after inna
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he rewards (referring to Allah in إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ)
steps.n: Added يَ at the beginning to show it's happening now or in the future. The ِ (kasra) on ز shows it's a certain type of present tense verb. This word connects to Allah (إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ) who is doing the rewarding.

steps.m: to give truthfully, to give charity
steps.n: Basic root meaning
Added shadda to show doing something with extra effort or repeatedly
Added تَ at the start to show doing the action voluntarily for oneself
Changed to show the person who does this action

steps.m: those who give charity (object of يَجْزِى)
steps.n: Made plural to show multiple people who give charity. This word is being acted upon by يَجْزِى (rewards)
Added ال to make it specific charitable people
Added vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb done by 'he'
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
This is a simple word that turns a statement into a yes/no question, like adding a question mark in English. Here, Yusuf is asking his brothers 'Do you know what you did?'
Added vowels (َ ِ َ) to the root letters to make it mean 'he knew' in the past
Added تُ at the end to change it from 'he knew' to 'I knew' - this ending can also mean 'you (male) knew' or 'she knew' depending on context

steps.m: you all knew (addressing multiple people in هَلْ عَلِمْتُم)
steps.n: Added م to show we're talking to multiple people. This word is being used in a question (هَلْ = 'did?')
Basic interrogative particle used to ask questions about things or actions
Added shadda (doubled م) to emphasize the questioning. This makes the question more forceful, like saying 'What exactly did you do?' instead of just 'What did you do?'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is part of what was said (قَالَ - he said).
Added sukun on ل to prepare for adding endings
Added تُم at the end to show this is addressing multiple people in the past
This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew/Aramaic through Arabic. The يُو at the start is part of the foreign name structure.

steps.m: Joseph (as the object being acted upon)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Joseph is being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: to/with Joseph (connected to فَعَلْتُم - what you did)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show how the action was done to/with Joseph. This connects to the verb فَعَلْتُم (what you did) in the ayah.
Connected the letters and dropped the weak letter و to make the basic noun form
Added kasra at the end to prepare for possession
Added ي at the end to mean 'my'
Added هِ to show this is Yusuf's brother being talked about
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
This is the basic form of the word that refers to a specific time in the past

steps.m: when (referring to their past actions with يُوسُفَ)
steps.n: The sukun (ْ) mark shows this word is complete and connects to what happened in the past. In this ayah, it refers to what they did to Prophet Yusuf.
This is the basic form of the pronoun used to address someone directly
Adding م with sukun at the end makes it refer to multiple people
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action
Added ون to make it refer to multiple people who lack knowledge
Added َ (fatha) to show normal grammatical case

steps.m: he said (introducing what they are about to say about يُوسُفُ)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: they said (the brothers speaking to يُوسُفُ)
steps.n: The وا at the end shows that multiple people (the brothers) did the action
This is a basic word that adds emphasis to what comes after it
Added كَ at the end to show this is talking about 'you' (when speaking to one male)
Added أَء at the beginning to turn this into a question showing surprise or disbelief. In this ayah, they are asking in amazement if Yusuf is really who they think he is.
This is a pronoun meaning 'you' (when speaking to one male). In this ayah, his brothers are addressing Joseph.
The لَ adds emphasis to show certainty, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'. Here, Joseph's brothers are expressing their sudden realization that he is truly Joseph.
This is a proper name, showing who is being talked about
The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Joseph is the one doing the action (saying) in this ayah
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the basic form before the weak letter changes.
The و (waw) changes to ا (alif) which is a common change for hollow verbs
This is the basic form of the first person singular pronoun meaning 'I'. It's one of the fundamental pronouns in Arabic that isn't derived from a root.
Added alif at the end to make it a complete standalone pronoun that can be used by itself in a sentence.
Added a special mark (small superscript alif) to show emphasis. This word is being used as the subject of يُوسُفُ (Yusuf) in the statement 'I am Yusuf'.

This is a proper name of Hebrew origin (יוֹסֵף Yosef) meaning 'He (God) will add'. It entered Arabic as يُوسُف and doesn't follow typical Arabic root patterns. In this ayah, Yusuf is confirming his identity to his brothers.

steps.m: Yusuf (as the subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Yusuf is the subject (doer) of the verb قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby. The alif elongates the sound to help point to things.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
The final alif is lengthened to add emphasis when pointing to something specific - in this case, Yusuf is pointing to his brother
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to prepare for the possessive suffix
Added ى at the end to show it's 'my brother'. In this ayah, this is being said to Yusuf by his brothers.
This is a particle that adds emphasis to the verb مَنَّ (bestowed) that follows it. It shows that something definitely happened in the past.

steps.m: to give a special favor or blessing
steps.n: The root means to give a favor or blessing to someone
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he bestowed favor (subject of قَدْ in this ayah)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the giving of favor. This word follows قَدْ which confirms that the action definitely happened.

steps.m: a deity, one who is worshipped
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun for 'one who is worshipped'
Added the u-mark at the end to show it's in the nominative case
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else
Added ي with sukun to prepare for connecting with نا
Added نا which means 'us', with an extra alif at the end to show emphasis. In this ayah, it refers to the favor that Allah bestowed upon Yusuf and his brother.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English

steps.m: indeed he/it (referring to the one who has taqwa and patience)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end which means 'he/it'. In this ayah, it refers back to the person mentioned before who has taqwa (God-consciousness) and patience.
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'whoever' or 'he who'. In this ayah, it refers to the person who does the actions mentioned after it (being mindful of Allah and being patient).
Root letters that mean protecting or guarding something
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he protected'
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel
Added ت and merged it with و to show protecting oneself (like putting up a shield)
Final weak letter ي dropped in jussive mood
The root means to be patient or endure
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Adding ي at the start and changing the vowels makes it mean 'he is patient' instead of 'he was patient'

steps.m: and he endures patiently (conditional after مَن)
steps.n: Added و (and) to connect with previous phrase. This whole phrase means 'whoever fears [Allah] and endures patiently'
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. The shadda (ّ) shows extra strength in the meaning.

steps.m: then indeed (connects to previous statement about being patient)
steps.n: The فَ shows this statement follows from the previous one about being patient and mindful of Allah. It's like saying 'so' or 'then' in English to show one thing leads to another.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows emphasis.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَتَّقِ - fears/is mindful of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is being acted upon. In this ayah, it shows who is being feared/respected in the phrase 'whoever fears Allah'.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُضِيعُ (he wastes/loses) into 'he does not waste/lose'. It's one of the most common ways to say 'no' or 'not' in Arabic.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added يُ at start to show present tense, changed middle vowels, and added ُ at end. This verb is negated by لا in the ayah, meaning 'does not waste'
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make the noun form meaning 'reward' or 'payment'

steps.m: reward (object of يُضِيعُ - does not waste)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb يُضِيعُ (does not waste/lose) in the phrase 'Allah does not waste the reward'
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing the middle vowels turns it from 'did good' to 'doer of good'
Makes it plural - more than one person who does good. The word is in the genitive case because it comes after أَجْرَ (reward) in a possession relationship, meaning 'reward of the good-doers'.
Added 'the' at the beginning and a mark at the end to show this word belongs to 'reward' (أَجْرَ) in the phrase 'reward of the good-doers'
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The middle weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs

steps.m: they said (subject of تَٱللَّهِ لَقَدْ ءَاثَرَكَ)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word is connected to what follows, where they swear by Allah about how He preferred Yusuf over them.
This is the base word for Allah, the proper name of God in Islam
Added kasra at the end to show this is being used in an oath
Added تَ at the beginning and ٱ (hamzat al-wasl) to create the oath form. This تَ is a special prefix used specifically for oaths with Allah's name.
This is a basic particle that confirms or emphasizes actions that happened in the past. In this ayah, it's emphasizing how Allah favored Yusuf.

Added لَ at the beginning for extra emphasis. Together with قَدْ, it makes the statement very strong, like saying 'most certainly' in English. Here it emphasizes how Yusuf's brothers are admitting Allah preferred him over them.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and changed the first letter to hamza with madd to show past tense for 'he'

steps.m: he preferred you (object of the verb)
steps.n: The كَ added at the end means 'you' - it shows who was preferred. Like saying 'preferred' vs 'preferred YOU'

steps.m: a deity, one who is worshipped
steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Changed from 'any god' to 'Allah' - this is a unique word that comes from adding ال to إله but has special pronunciation. The ُ at the end shows Allah is doing something
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'over'
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns at the end
Added نا at the end which means 'us' or 'our'
Basic connecting word that joins this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: and if, even though (connects to كُنَّا - we were)
steps.n: Adds إِن which shows a condition or concession. Here it introduces their admission 'even though we were sinners'
Added fathas to connect the letters and make the past tense form
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels
Changes from past tense 'was' to a command meaning 'be!'
Added a doubled ن (called shadda) to strengthen the word before adding the next ending
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Root letters connected with vowels to make the noun meaning 'a mistake'
Changed the word to describe someone who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple people
Added 'la' at start to emphasize we were definitely wrong
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to what comes next - 'no blame'
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
This is the basic negative particle that makes something not true. In this ayah, it negates تَثْرِيبَ (blame), meaning 'there is no blame.'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation of the first letter

steps.m: to blame or criticize someone for their mistakes
blame (object of لَا/no in لَا تَثْرِيبَ/no blame)
steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'blame' or 'criticism'. This word is being negated by لَا (no) to say there will be no blame.
This is a basic preposition showing something is physically or metaphorically on top of something else
Changed ى to يْ to connect with the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon you all (receiving the lack of blame mentioned in لَا تَثْرِيبَ)
steps.n: Added كُمُ at the end to show this affects multiple people being spoken to. In this ayah, it shows that multiple people are receiving the statement that there is no blame on them.
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'day'
Added ال to make it specific/definite - 'the day' instead of just any day
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, kasra on second root letter, and damma at the end. This word shows what Allah does (forgives).
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added U mark at end to show this is the subject/doer
This is a preposition that shows direction or purpose. Here it connects with the verb يَغْفِرُ (forgives) to show who is being forgiven.
Added كُمْ which means 'you all'. In this ayah, it shows who Allah is forgiving.
This is a pronoun that means 'he'. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who is the subject of أَرْحَمُ (most merciful)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects 'He forgives you' with 'and He is most merciful'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed رَحِمَ (showed mercy) to أَرْحَمُ by adding أَ at start to make it a superlative form meaning 'most merciful'. Like changing 'merciful' to 'most merciful of all'

steps.m: to show mercy, to be compassionate

steps.n: Added alif after first root letter to create active participle
Makes the word plural by adding ين ending
Added 'Al' (the) at the start which made the 'R' sound doubled. The '-een' ending was already there in both words - it wasn't added
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he went' to a command 'go!' by adding alif at start and putting a sukoon (no vowel) on the last letter
Changed from commanding one person to commanding many people by adding وا at the end
Changes from basic root into a word for a physical object (shirt)
Added بِ to show this shirt is being used for something (in this case, to help restore vision)

steps.m: with my shirt (object being used to restore vision to يَأْتِ بَصِيرًا)
steps.n: Added ى to show it's specifically Yusuf's shirt that will help restore his father's vision
This is the attention-drawing part that starts many pointing words. It's like saying 'look!' or 'hey!'
Added ذا which means 'this' to complete the pointing word. Together ها + ذا makes a word that points to something nearby.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ه and a fatha on ذ to help with pronunciation. This word is pointing to قَمِيصِى (my shirt) that Yusuf is talking about.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to show causing something to happen, and sukun on second letter
Added و and damma at end to make it plural (more than one person doing the action)
Added ف at beginning to connect this command with what came before it

steps.m: and throw it! (command to a group)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show what is being thrown
Added fatha marks to show correct pronunciation. This word connects وَجْهِ (face) to what came before it.
Added small alif (ٰ) after ى to show it's a long vowel sound. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: to face or turn towards something

steps.n: Added fatha and sukun to make the basic noun 'face'. This word belongs to أَبِى (my father) in this ayah.
Added kasra at the end to show this word owns or connects to the word after it (أَبِى - my father).

steps.m: father, to nurture/care for as a father

steps.n: Root letters أ-ب-و turned into the simple word for 'father'
Adding kasra to prepare for possessive suffix
Adding first person possessive pronoun ى (pronounced as ī) to show ownership/possession. This is a common way to say 'my' in Arabic.
Added يَ at beginning to show 'he' in present tense, with أْ (sukun) to help pronunciation
Final ي is dropped because this is in a conditional phrase after فَ (so/then)

steps.m: to see or perceive (with eyes or mind)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show someone who has the quality of seeing/perceiving very well

steps.m: seeing/perceiving (describing Yaqub's state in this ayah)
steps.n: Added tanween fatHa to show this describes the state of Yaqub when his sight returns after the shirt is placed on his face
Changed from root word to a command by adding a stop mark (sukun) and 'oo' sound (damma) - like saying 'Come!'
Added و to make it plural command
Added نِ to show the action is directed towards the speaker
The ي shows who is receiving the action - in this case 'me'
Added و with fatha to connect with previous command
Root letters got vowels to make the common word for 'family'
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with' and kasra at end because it's connected to what follows
Added كُمْ to show the family belongs to multiple people being addressed
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Added أَ at the start changes it from 'gather' to mean 'all together' - like when you gather everything completely

steps.m: all together (as objects of يَأْتُونِى - they bring)
steps.n: Added ين to show this describes multiple people/things being brought. This word is describing the family members being brought in يَأْتُونِى (they bring)
This is a time particle that introduces an event that happened in the past. In this ayah, it introduces when the caravan departed.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This shows the sequence of events in the story.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create past tense. This word refers to the caravan (الْعِيرُ) departing.
Added تِ to show the subject is feminine (الْعِيرُ/the caravan is treated as feminine in Arabic)
Added kasra to create the noun form of the word
Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to a specific caravan that was mentioned before
Adding damma shows this is the subject doing the departing in فَصَلَتِ ٱلْعِيرُ (the caravan departed)
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows the basic form before the weak letter change.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs

steps.m: to nurture/care for (as a father does)

steps.n: The root letters get vowel marks (fat-ha and damma) to make the word mean 'father'
Added هُمْ to show possession. This word is the subject (doer) of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to a word that adds emphasis to what comes next, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed' in English
Adding ي at the end shows this is about me (I/my). This word connects to أَجِدُ (I find) later in the sentence, showing who is doing the finding.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word, but the meaning stays the same
Changed to present tense first person (I) by adding أ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, and adding damma at end
Added لَ at beginning to add emphasis. This is Yaqub (Jacob) emphasizing that he can smell Yusuf's scent.

steps.m: to move through air/space (like wind, spirit, or scent)

steps.n: The و changes to ي after kasra, making this a noun meaning the thing that moves through air
The fatha at the end shows this is the object being found/smelled in the phrase 'I find the scent of Yusuf'

steps.m: Joseph (proper name of Prophet Ya'qub's son)
steps.n: Added damma marks to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: Joseph (as the object of أَجِدُ رِيحَ - I find the smell of)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this is the object being acted upon in the phrase 'I find the smell of Joseph'
Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation. لَوْ is a conditional particle that introduces hypothetical situations.
Added لا (not) to create the compound meaning 'if not for'. This combination is used to express that something would have happened if not for something else.

steps.m: if not for (introducing a hypothetical situation in أَن تُفَنِّدُونِ)
steps.n: Changed the final alif to an extended alif (آ) for emphasis in pronunciation. This word introduces a hypothetical situation about what would happen if they didn't criticize him.
This is a helper word that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects what the father is saying with what others are doing (making fun of him).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Changes from past to present tense and adds 'you/she' as the doer
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
لا makes the verb negative - 'do not'
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The و (weak letter) changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action. The small mark after وا (۟) helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.
This is the base word for Allah, the proper name of God in Islam
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
